# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Gradient Descent (–2 marks):** Students often forget the negative sign in $\Delta w = -\eta \frac{\partial E}{\partial w}$, causing weights to *move away* from the minimum. The final formula $\Delta w = \eta \cdot \delta \cdot y$ is what you must write.
> 2. **Confusing Output and Hidden Delta (–2 marks):** The output delta uses the *target*; the hidden delta uses *back-propagated* weighted deltas — NOT the target.
> 3. **Forgetting the Sigmoid Derivative (–1 mark):** When using sigmoid, $f'(z) = f(z)(1-f(z))$. Using a linear derivative yields wrong numerical answers.
> 4. **Not Normalizing Inputs (–1 mark):** Real KTU questions may deduct marks if inputs are not in $[0,1]$ range when sigmoid is used.
> 5. **Skipping the Bias Update (–1 mark):** Biases are updated exactly like weights but with input value $1$.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Back Propagation Network. List any two advantages and two limitations of BPN.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** BPN is a supervised, multi-layer feed-forward neural network trained using the Error Back Propagation algorithm, where the output error is propagated backward to update connection weights via gradient descent.
- **Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
  1. Can learn arbitrary non-linear mappings (Universal Approximation).
  2. Self-organizing — no need to hand-design features.
  3. Generalizes well to unseen data when properly regularized.
- **Limitations (1 mark — any two):**
  1. Slow convergence; sensitive to learning rate.
  2. May converge to local minima instead of global.
  3. Black-box model — poor interpretability.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Generalized Delta Rule? How does it differ from the simple Perceptron Delta Rule?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Generalized Delta Rule (2 marks):** It is the multi-layer extension of the perceptron learning rule. The weight update is $\Delta w_{ij} = \eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot y_i$, where $\delta_j$ is the *local gradient* computed via the chain rule. For output neurons, $\delta_k = (y_k^{\text{target}} - y_k^{\text{pred}}) f'(net_k)$; for hidden neurons, $\delta_j = (\sum_k \delta_k w_{jk}) f'(net_j)$.
- **Difference (1 mark):** The simple Perceptron Delta Rule applies only to a *single-layer* network with a *linear* activation and updates based on the *direct* classification error. The Generalized Delta Rule handles *multiple layers*, *non-linear* activations (e.g., sigmoid), and propagates error *backward* through hidden layers.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a multi-layer Back Propagation Network. Differentiate between **pattern mode** and **batch mode** training. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the given BPN with 2 inputs, 2 hidden neurons (sigmoid activation) and 1 output neuron (sigmoid activation), the initial weights and one training pattern are:

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $x_1, x_2$ | 0.4, 0.6 |
| Target $t$ | 0.9 |
| $w_{11}, w_{12}$ | 0.2, 0.1 |
| $w_{21}, w_{22}$ | 0.3, 0.4 |
| $w_{1o}, w_{2o}$ | 0.5, 0.6 |
| Biases (all) | 0.1 |
| Learning rate $\eta$ | 0.5 |

Perform **one complete epoch of forward and backward pass** and compute the updated weights. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Architecture (3 marks):**

A BPN has three or more layers:

1. **Input Layer:** Distributes inputs $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ to the next layer. No computation occurs here.
2. **Hidden Layer(s):** Each neuron computes a weighted sum and applies a non-linear activation (e.g., sigmoid). Hidden layers enable learning of complex non-linear relationships.
3. **Output Layer:** Produces the network's prediction $\mathbf{y}^{\text{pred}}$ via the chosen output activation (sigmoid for classification, linear for regression).

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):** Input → Hidden (with sigmoid) → Output (with sigmoid) → Error computation → Backward propagation → Weight update → Loop.

**Pattern Mode vs Batch Mode (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Pattern (Online/Stochastic) Mode | Batch Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **When weights are updated** | After every single training pattern. | After passing all $N$ patterns once. |
| **Update frequency** | $N$ updates per epoch. | $1$ update per epoch. |
| **Memory requirement** | Low. | High (must store deltas for all patterns). |
| **Convergence behaviour** | Noisy but escapes local minima. | Smooth but may get stuck. |
| **Speed per epoch** | Fast. | Slow. |

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Forward Pass:**

Hidden neuron 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
net_1 &= (0.2)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.6) + 0.1 = 0.08 + 0.18 + 0.1 = 0.36 \\
y_1 &= f(0.36) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-0.36}} \approx 0.5890
\end{aligned}
$$

Hidden neuron 2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
net_2 &= (0.1)(0.4) + (0.4)(0.6) + 0.1 = 0.04 + 0.24 + 0.1 = 0.38 \\
y_2 &= f(0.38) \approx 0.5938
\end{aligned}
$$

Output neuron:

$$
\begin{aligned}
net_o &= (0.5)(0.5890) + (0.6)(0.5938) + 0.1 = 0.2945 + 0.3563 + 0.1 = 0.7508 \\
y^{\text{pred}} &= f(0.7508) \approx 0.6792
\end{aligned}
$$

**Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \frac{1}{2}(0.9 - 0.6792)^2 = \frac{1}{2}(0.0487) = 0.0243
\end{aligned}
$$

**Backward Pass:**

Output delta:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_o = (0.9 - 0.6792) \cdot (0.6792)(1 - 0.6792) = (0.2208)(0.2178) \approx 0.0481
\end{aligned}
$$

Hidden → Output weight updates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{1o}^{\text{new}} &= 0.5 + (0.5)(0.0481)(0.5890) = 0.5 + 0.01416 = 0.51416 \\
w_{2o}^{\text{new}} &= 0.6 + (0.5)(0.0481)(0.5938) = 0.6 + 0.01428 = 0.61428 \\
b_o^{\text{new}} &= 0.1 + (0.5)(0.0481)(1) = 0.1 + 0.02405 = 0.12405
\end{aligned}
$$

Hidden deltas:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1 &= (0.0481)(0.51416) \cdot (0.5890)(1 - 0.5890) = 0.02473 \cdot 0.2421 = 0.00599 \\
\delta_2 &= (0.0481)(0.61428) \cdot (0.5938)(1 - 0.5938) = 0.02955 \cdot 0.2412 = 0.00713
\end{aligned}
$$

Input → Hidden weight updates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{11}^{\text{new}} &= 0.2 + (0.5)(0.00599)(0.4) = 0.2 + 0.00120 = 0.20120 \\
w_{21}^{\text{new}} &= 0.3 + (0.5)(0.00599)(0.6) = 0.3 + 0.00180 = 0.30180 \\
b_1^{\text{new}} &= 0.1 + (0.5)(0.00599) = 0.1 + 0.00300 = 0.10300 \\
w_{12}^{\text{new}} &= 0.1 + (0.5)(0.00713)(0.4) = 0.1 + 0.00143 = 0.10143 \\
w_{22}^{\text{new}} &= 0.4 + (0.5)(0.00713)(0.6) = 0.4 + 0.00214 = 0.40214 \\
b_2^{\text{new}} &= 0.1 + (0.5)(0.00713) = 0.1 + 0.00357 = 0.10357
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** New prediction $\approx 0.689$ (slight improvement toward $0.9$). **Full marks awarded.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Generalized Delta Rule** for updating weights in a hidden layer of a BPN, clearly showing the application of the chain rule. State the final weight update equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **local minima** and **saddle point** problems in BPN training. Discuss any **three techniques** to overcome these issues during training. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Setup:** Consider hidden neuron $j$ receiving input $y_i$ from previous layer with weight $w_{ij}$, and feeding output $y_j$ to output neuron $k$ via weight $w_{jk}$.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the total error:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E = \frac{1}{2} \sum_k \left( y_k^{\text{target}} - y_k^{\text{pred}} \right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Express $y_k^{\text{pred}} = f(net_k)$ and $net_k = \sum_j w_{jk} y_j + b_k$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the chain rule in three nested layers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} = \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_j} \cdot \frac{\partial y_j}{\partial net_j} \cdot \frac{\partial net_j}{\partial w_{ij}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Expand $\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_j}$ using the chain rule over all output neurons:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial y_j} &= \sum_k \frac{\partial E}{\partial y_k^{\text{pred}}} \cdot \frac{\partial y_k^{\text{pred}}}{\partial net_k} \cdot \frac{\partial net_k}{\partial y_j} \\
&= \sum_k \left[ -(y_k^{\text{target}} - y_k^{\text{pred}}) \right] f'(net_k) w_{jk} \\
&= -\sum_k \delta_k w_{jk}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Combine and write the final update:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial E}{\partial w_{ij}} &= -\left( \sum_k \delta_k w_{jk} \right) f'(net_j) \cdot y_i \\
\delta_j &= \left( \sum_k \delta_k w_{jk} \right) f'(net_j) \\
\Delta w_{ij} &= \eta \cdot \delta_j \cdot y_i \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Local Minima (2 marks):**
The MSE loss surface in BPN is highly non-convex due to non-linear activations. Gradient descent may converge to a *local minimum* (a low point that is not the global lowest point), producing a network with suboptimal performance. The error appears to plateau, but the weights are not at their best possible values.

**Saddle Points (2 marks):**
A *saddle point* is a position where the gradient is zero but the curvature is positive in some directions and negative in others. Modern deep networks frequently encounter saddle points (rather than local minima). Gradients become vanishingly small → training stalls even though the network has not converged.

**Three techniques to overcome (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Momentum-based update:** Add $\alpha \cdot \Delta w^{\text{prev}}$ to the weight update. This gives the optimizer "inertia" to roll past small bumps and saddle points.

2. **Adaptive Learning Rate (AdaGrad / RMSProp / Adam):** Each weight has its own learning rate that scales with the history of gradients. Frequently-updated weights get smaller steps; rarely-updated weights get larger steps — helping escape plateaus.

3. **Random Initialization + Multiple Restarts:** Initializing weights randomly and training the network multiple times from different starting points increases the chance of finding the global minimum (or at least a good local one). Mini-batch SGD also introduces noise that helps escape local minima.

(Alternative valid techniques: **Learning rate decay**, **simulated annealing** concept, **using ReLU activation** to reduce vanishing gradient, **batch normalization**, etc.)

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam**

- [ ] **BPN = Multi-layer + Backprop + Gradient Descent.** All three are inseparable.
- [ ] **Forward pass:** Input → Hidden → Output. Activation is applied at hidden and output neurons.
- [ ] **Loss function:** $E = \frac{1}{2} \sum (t - y)^2$. Memorize *with the half* — it cancels the 2 in derivative.
- [ ] **Output delta:** $\delta_k = (t_k - y_k) \cdot f'(net_k)$.
- [ ] **Hidden delta:** $\delta_j = \left( \sum_k \delta_k w_{jk} \right) \cdot f'(net_j)$. The $\sum$ is the *backward* error flow.
- [ ] **Weight update:** $\Delta w = \eta \cdot \delta \cdot y_{\text{input}}$. **No negative sign** in the final formula.
- [ ] **Bias update:** Same rule with input = 1.
- [ ] **Sigmoid derivative:** $f'(z) = f(z)(1-f(z))$. This is the most-used in KTU numericals.
- [ ] **Learning rate:** $0 < \eta < 1$. Typical KTU values: $0.1, 0.5, 0.8$.
- [ ] **Momentum:** $0 < \alpha < 1$. Added to accelerate convergence.
- [ ] **Pattern vs Batch:** Pattern mode updates after every sample; batch mode updates after all samples.
- [ ] **Epoch vs Iteration:** Epoch = one full pass through training set; iteration = one weight update.
- [ ] **Local minima + Saddle points** are the two main convergence problems; momentum + adaptive learning rates are standard fixes.
- [ ] **Vanishing gradient:** Sigmoid saturates → $\delta$ becomes tiny → hidden layers barely learn. Use ReLU/Tanh to mitigate.
- [ ] **Universal Approximation Theorem:** A BPN with *one* sufficiently wide hidden layer can approximate *any* continuous function on a compact domain to arbitrary accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fuzzy Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Fuzzy Set # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Fuzzy Set? — The Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)** > A **Fuzzy Set** $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is a collection of objects $\{x_1, x_2, x_3, \ldots, x_n\}$ in which there is no crisp (well-defined) boundary separating the members from the non-members. Mathematically, a fuzzy set is a mapping from the universe of discourse $X$ into the unit interval $[0, 1]$. > > $$\tilde{A} = \{\, (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X \,\}$$ > > where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the **Membership Function** (also called the *membership grade* or *degree of belonging*) of $x$ in $\tilde{A}$, satisfying $0 \le \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \le 1$. The value **0** represents absolute non-membership, **1** represents absolute membership, and any value strictly between **0 and 1** represents *partial* or *graded* membership. ## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — Why Fuzzy Logic Exists > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: "How tall is 'tall'?"** > Imagine a group of people standing in a room. In classical (crisp) set theory, a person is either **"tall"** (membership = 1) or **"not tall"** (membership = 0). But what about someone who is 175 cm? Are they tall? Not really. What about 185 cm? Sort of. What about 200 cm? Definitely. The world isn't black-and-white — it has shades of grey. **Fuzzy Set Theory** (introduced by **Professor Lotfi A. Zadeh** in 1965) gives us a mathematical way to express this *gradual transition* from "not tall" to "tall" using values between 0 and 1. | Aspect | Classical (Crisp) Set | Fuzzy Set | |---|---|---| | Membership value | Binary: **0 or 1** | Continuous: **any value in [0, 1]** | | Boundary | Sharply defined | Smoothly graded | | Logic foundation | Aristotelian (True/False) | Multi-valued logic | | Suitability | Precise engineering math | Humanistic, linguistic, AI problems | ## 1.3 The Universe of Discourse ($X$) The **universe of discourse** $X$ is the *total collection of all possible elements* under consideration for the fuzzy set. It can be **discrete** (finite/countable) or **continuous** (uncountable). **Discrete form:** $$A = \{\, \mu_1/x_1 + \mu_2/x_2 + \mu_3/x_3 + \ldots + \mu_n/x_n \,\} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\mu_i}{x_i}$$ **Continuous form:** $$A = \int_{X} \frac{\mu_A(x)}{x}$$ > [!NOTE] > The symbols $\sum$ and $\int$ here **do not** denote arithmetic summation or integration — they are **set-theoretic union operators** indicating the aggregation of (element, membership-grade) pairs. ## 1.4 Why the Tilde Notation ($\tilde{A}$)? A **tilde** is placed on top of a crisp symbol to denote that the quantity has been *fuzzified*. Thus: - $A$ → crisp set - $\tilde{A}$ → fuzzy set - $x$ → crisp element - $\tilde{x}$ → fuzzified linguistic variable # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Membership Function — The Heart of Fuzzy Sets The **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ is the *defining characteristic* of a fuzzy set. It is a curve that defines *how each point in the input space is mapped to a membership value* (or *degree of belonging*) between 0 and 1. ### Logical Interpretation of Membership Grades | Value of $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Interpretation | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ | $x$ is **fully** a member of $\tilde{A}$ | Core element | | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0$ | $x$ is **not at all** a member of $\tilde{A}$ | Outside the support | | $0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1$ | $x$ is a **partial** member of $\tilde{A}$ | Fuzziness region | ## 2.2 Essential Characteristics of Fuzzy Sets (KTU High-Yield) These properties are **routinely tested** in KTU ESE papers and form the analytical spine of Module 2. 1. **Support of a Fuzzy Set** ($S(\tilde{A})$) — The crisp set of *all* elements having a *non-zero* membership grade. $$S(\tilde{A}) = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0 \,\}$$ 2. **Core of a Fuzzy Set** ($C(\tilde{A})$) — The crisp set of all elements having membership grade *exactly equal to 1*. $$C(\tilde{A}) = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1 \,\}$$ 3. **Height of a Fuzzy Set** ($h(\tilde{A})$) — The *largest* (supremum) membership grade attained. $$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X}\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$ 4. **Normal Fuzzy Set** — A fuzzy set is called **normal** if its height equals **1**. $$\tilde{A}\ \text{is normal} \iff h(\tilde{A}) = 1$$ 5. **Sub-normal Fuzzy Set** — A fuzzy set is *sub-normal* if its height is strictly less than 1. It can always be *normalized* by dividing every membership grade by $h(\tilde{A})$. 6. **Crossover Points** — The points in $X$ where the membership function equals **0.5** (the *fuzzy boundary*). $$\text{Crossover points} = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5 \,\}$$ 7. **$\alpha$-cut (Alpha-Cut) of a Fuzzy Set** — The crisp set of all elements whose membership grade is *at least* $\alpha$, where $\alpha \in [0, 1]$. $$A_\alpha = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge \alpha \,\}$$ 8. **Strong $\alpha$-cut** ($A_{\alpha}^{+}$) — The crisp set of all elements with membership grade *strictly greater than* $\alpha$. $$A_{\alpha}^{+} = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha \,\}$$ 9. **Convex Fuzzy Set** — A fuzzy set is convex if *all* of its $\alpha$-cuts are convex (i.e., intervals). $$\mu_{\tilde{A}}\big(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2\big) \ge \min\big(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1),\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2)\big)$$ ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the following table — every KTU ESE question in this module is built from these formulas.** | # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fuzzy set definition | $\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$ | Definition | | 2 | Membership range | $0 \le \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \le 1$ | Constraint | | 3 | Support | $S(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ | Crisp set | | 4 | Core | $C(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ | Crisp set | | 5 | Height | $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_x \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | Scalar | | 6 | Normality test | $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$ | Condition | | 7 | Crossover points | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5$ | Scalar set | | 8 | $\alpha$-cut | $A_\alpha = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge \alpha\}$ | Crisp set | | 9 | Strong $\alpha$-cut | $A_\alpha^{+} = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$ | Crisp set | | 10 | Convexity condition | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \ge \min(\mu_1, \mu_2)$ | Condition | | 11 | Union (Zadeh) | $\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Operation | | 12 | Intersection (Zadeh) | $\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min(\mu_A(x), \mu_B(x))$ | Operation | | 13 | Complement | $\mu_{A^{c}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$ | Operation | > **Note on Operation Formulas (11, 12, 13):** These *Zadeh operators* are the simplest and most KTU-tested choice. Other families (product, Lukasiewicz, Dombi, Hamacher, Yager) also exist and are covered later in Module 2. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Fuzzy sets are the **mathematical backbone** of: - **Washing Machines** (LG/Bosch): Classifying "load amount" and "dirt level" linguistically. - **Automotive Control** (Honda, Nissan, Subaru): Anti-lock braking, automatic transmission shifting. - **Air-Conditioners** (Mitsubishi, Samsung): Fuzzy temperature control. - **Camera Autofocus**: Distinguishing "near / far / medium". - **Stock Market Prediction, Medical Diagnosis, Weather Forecasting.** ## 2.5 Crisp Set vs Fuzzy Set — Side-by-Side Comparison > [!NOTE] > **KTU frequently asks: "Distinguish between classical set and fuzzy set"** — this table is your exam-ready answer. | Property | Classical (Crisp) Set | Fuzzy Set | |---|---|---| | Defined by | Characteristic function $C_A(x) \in \{0,1\}$ | Membership function $\mu_A(x) \in [0,1]$ | | Membership | Binary (0 or 1) | Continuous (0 to 1) | | Boundary | Sharp | Gradual | | Notation | $A = \{x \mid P(x)\}$ | $\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x))\}$ | | Logic type | Two-valued (Boolean) | Infinite-valued | | Computation | Boolean algebra | Fuzzy algebra | | Discovered by | G. Boole, G. Cantor | L. A. Zadeh (1965) | | Application | Precise engineering | AI, control, linguistics | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems ## 3.1 Problem 1 — Identifying Support, Core, and Height (Board Pattern) > **Problem:** A fuzzy set is defined on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$ as > $$\tilde{A} = \{\, 0.1/1 + 0.3/2 + 0.6/3 + 1.0/4 + 0.8/5 + 0.4/6 + 0.0/7 \,\}$$ > Find the **support**, **core**, **height**, and verify whether $\tilde{A}$ is **normal**. ### Step 1 — Write the Membership Grades Explicitly Extract the (element, grade) pairs: | $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | 0.1 | 0.3 | 0.6 | 1.0 | 0.8 | 0.4 | 0.0 | ### Step 2 — Compute the Support $S(\tilde{A})$ By definition, support consists of *all* $x$ with **strictly positive** membership: $$S(\tilde{A}) = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0 \,\}$$ Going through each element: - $x=1$: $\mu=0.1 > 0$ ✓ - $x=2$: $\mu=0.3 > 0$ ✓ - $x=3$: $\mu=0.6 > 0$ ✓ - $x=4$: $\mu=1.0 > 0$ ✓ - $x=5$: $\mu=0.8 > 0$ ✓ - $x=6$: $\mu=0.4 > 0$ ✓ - $x=7$: $\mu=0.0 \not> 0$ ✗ $$\boxed{S(\tilde{A}) = \{1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 4,\ 5,\ 6\}}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Core $C(\tilde{A})$ By definition, the core contains all $x$ with membership grade **exactly equal to 1**: $$C(\tilde{A}) = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1 \,\}$$ Scanning the table, only $x=4$ has $\mu=1.0$. $$\boxed{C(\tilde{A}) = \{4\}}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Height $h(\tilde{A})$ $$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X}\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \max\{0.1,\ 0.3,\ 0.6,\ 1.0,\ 0.8,\ 0.4,\ 0.0\}$$ $$\boxed{h(\tilde{A}) = 1.0}$$ ### Step 5 — Normality Test A fuzzy set is **normal** if and only if $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$. Since $h(\tilde{A}) = 1.0 = 1$, the set is **normal**. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Problem 2 — $\alpha$-Cuts and Crossover Points > **Problem:** For the same fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ above, find the **0.5-cut** ($A_{0.5}$), the **0.6-cut** ($A_{0.6}$), and the **crossover points**. ### Step 1 — Find $A_{0.5}$ (the 0.5-cut) $$A_{0.5} = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge 0.5 \,\}$$ | $x$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $\ge 0.5$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.3 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.6 | ✓ | | 4 | 1.0 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.8 | ✓ | | 6 | 0.4 | ✗ | | 7 | 0.0 | ✗ | $$\boxed{A_{0.5} = \{3,\ 4,\ 5\}}$$ ### Step 2 — Find $A_{0.6}$ (the 0.6-cut) $$A_{0.6} = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge 0.6 \,\}$$ | $x$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ | $\ge 0.6$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.3 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.6 | ✓ | | 4 | 1.0 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.8 | ✓ | | 6 | 0.4 | ✗ | | 7 | 0.0 | ✗ | $$\boxed{A_{0.6} = \{3,\ 4,\ 5\}}$$ ### Step 3 — Find the Crossover Points $$\text{Crossover} = \{\, x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5 \,\}$$ No element has membership grade exactly 0.5. $$\boxed{\text{Crossover points} = \varnothing\ \text{(empty set)}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Problem 3 — Zadeh Operations on Two Fuzzy Sets > **Problem:** Given two fuzzy sets > $$\tilde{A} = \{0.2/1 + 0.5/2 + 0.8/3 + 1.0/4 + 0.6/5\}$$ > $$\tilde{B} = \{0.3/1 + 0.7/2 + 0.4/3 + 0.9/4 + 0.5/5\}$$ > Compute $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, and $\tilde{A}^{c}$. ### Step 1 — Union $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ (Zadeh max operator) $$\mu_{A \cup B}(x) = \max\big(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\big)$$ | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\max$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.2 | 0.3 | **0.3** | | 2 | 0.5 | 0.7 | **0.7** | | 3 | 0.8 | 0.4 | **0.8** | | 4 | 1.0 | 0.9 | **1.0** | | 5 | 0.6 | 0.5 | **0.6** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{0.3/1 + 0.7/2 + 0.8/3 + 1.0/4 + 0.6/5\}}$$ ### Step 2 — Intersection $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ (Zadeh min operator) $$\mu_{A \cap B}(x) = \min\big(\mu_A(x),\, \mu_B(x)\big)$$ | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\min$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.2 | 0.3 | **0.2** | | 2 | 0.5 | 0.7 | **0.5** | | 3 | 0.8 | 0.4 | **0.4** | | 4 | 1.0 | 0.9 | **0.9** | | 5 | 0.6 | 0.5 | **0.5** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{0.2/1 + 0.5/2 + 0.4/3 + 0.9/4 + 0.5/5\}}$$ ### Step 3 — Complement $\tilde{A}^{c}$ $$\mu_{A^{c}}(x) = 1 - \mu_A(x)$$ | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $1 - \mu_A$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.2 | **0.8** | | 2 | 0.5 | **0.5** | | 3 | 0.8 | **0.2** | | 4 | 1.0 | **0.0** | | 5 | 0.6 | **0.4** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A}^{c} = \{0.8/1 + 0.5/2 + 0.2/3 + 0.0/4 + 0.4/5\}}$$ --- ## 3.4 Problem 4 — Verification of De Morgan's Law (Fuzzy) > **Problem:** Verify De Morgan's Law: $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$, using the same $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ from Problem 3. ### LHS Computation We already have $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ from Step 1 of Problem 3: $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{0.3,\ 0.7,\ 0.8,\ 1.0,\ 0.6\}$$ Taking the complement: $$(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \{1-0.3,\ 1-0.7,\ 1-0.8,\ 1-1.0,\ 1-0.6\}$$ $$= \{0.7,\ 0.3,\ 0.2,\ 0.0,\ 0.4\}$$ ### RHS Computation From Step 3 of Problem 3: $$\tilde{A}^{c} = \{0.8,\ 0.5,\ 0.2,\ 0.0,\ 0.4\}$$ We also need $\tilde{B}^{c}$: $$\tilde{B}^{c} = \{1-0.3,\ 1-0.7,\ 1-0.4,\ 1-0.9,\ 1-0.5\} = \{0.7,\ 0.3,\ 0.6,\ 0.1,\ 0.5\}$$ Now compute $\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$ (Zadeh min): | $x$ | $\mu_{A^{c}}$ | $\mu_{B^{c}}$ | $\min$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.8 | 0.7 | **0.7** | | 2 | 0.5 | 0.3 | **0.3** | | 3 | 0.2 | 0.6 | **0.2** | | 4 | 0.0 | 0.1 | **0.0** | | 5 | 0.4 | 0.5 | **0.4** | $$\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c} = \{0.7,\ 0.3,\ 0.2,\ 0.0,\ 0.4\}$$ ### Verification $$\text{LHS} = \{0.7,\ 0.3,\ 0.2,\ 0.0,\ 0.4\} = \text{RHS}\ \checkmark$$ De Morgan's Law is verified. ∎ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Concept Map — Anatomy of a Fuzzy Set ```mermaid flowchart TD startA[Universe of Discourse X] --> startB[Define Membership Function muA x] startB --> startC{Grade of x in fuzzy set A} startC --> branchA[muA x = 1
Core Element] startC --> branchB[0 less than muA x less than 1
Partial Membership] startC --> branchC[muA x = 0
Not a Member] branchA --> deriveA[Core C of A] branchB --> deriveB[Support S of A] deriveB --> deriveC[Alpha cuts A alpha] deriveC --> deriveD[Crossover points at 0.5] deriveA --> deriveE[Height h of A = 1 implies Normal] deriveB --> deriveF[Convexity Test] startA --> opA[Zadeh Operations] opA --> opB[Union = max] opA --> opC[Intersection = min] opA --> opD[Complement = 1 minus mu] ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Fuzzification Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR inputA[Crisp Numerical Input x] --> inputB[Input Universe of Discourse X] inputB --> inputC[Select Membership Function Type] inputC --> choiceA[Triangular] inputC --> choiceB[Trapezoidal] inputC --> choiceC[Gaussian] inputC --> choiceD[Sigmoid] inputA --> block1[Fuzzifier Module] choiceA --> block1 choiceB --> block1 choiceC --> block1 choiceD --> block1 block1 --> outputA[Fuzzy Set A tilde with mu values in 0 1] outputA --> outputB[Rule Base and Inference Engine] outputB --> outputC[Defuzzifier] outputC --> outputD[Crisp Control Action y] ``` ## 4.3 Visual Comparison — Crisp Set vs Fuzzy Set Membership ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph panelA["CRISP SET (Step Function)"] crispX[Input x] --> crispLogic{Threshold Test} crispLogic --> crispYes[x in A : mu = 1] crispLogic --> crispNo[x not in A : mu = 0] end subgraph panelB["FUZZY SET (Smooth Membership Function)"] fuzzyX[Input x] --> fuzzyLogic[Membership Function muA x] fuzzyLogic --> fuzzyGrad[mu ranges from 0 to 1 smoothly] fuzzyGrad --> fuzzyLabel[Degree of Belonging] end panelA --> compare[Comparison: Binary vs Gradual] panelB --> compare compare --> result[Crisp: Sharp Boundary | Fuzzy: Smooth Transition] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Property Identification for a Given Fuzzy Set ```mermaid flowchart TD startQ[Given Fuzzy Set A tilde] --> step1[Extract all pairs x, muA x] step1 --> step2{Find maximum muA x} step2 --> step3{Is maximum equal to 1?} step3 --> stepY[Yes: A tilde is Normal] step3 --> stepN[No: A tilde is Sub-normal] step1 --> step4[Find all x where muA x greater than 0] step4 --> step5[Construct Support S of A] step1 --> step6[Find all x where muA x = 1] step6 --> step7[Construct Core C of A] step1 --> step8[Find all x where muA x = 0.5] step8 --> step9[Identify Crossover Points] step1 --> step10[Choose alpha in 0 1] step10 --> step11[Find all x with muA x greater than or equal to alpha] step11 --> step12[Construct Alpha-cut A alpha] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define a fuzzy set with an example. How is it different from a classical set? **Model Answer:** A **fuzzy set** $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is a collection of elements each assigned a **membership grade** in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$: $$\tilde{A} = \{\, (x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X,\ 0 \le \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \le 1 \,\}$$ **Example:** Let $X = \{10, 20, 30, 40, 50\}$ represent temperatures in °C. The fuzzy set "warm" may be: $$\text{Warm} = \{0.2/10 + 0.5/20 + 0.8/30 + 0.6/40 + 0.2/50\}$$ | Aspect | Classical Set | Fuzzy Set | |---|---|---| | Membership | Binary (0 or 1) | Continuous in $[0, 1]$ | | Boundary | Sharply defined | Smoothly graded | | Defined by | Characteristic function | Membership function | | Suitable for | Precise mathematics | Vague, linguistic data | --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define support, core, and height of a fuzzy set. **Model Answer:** For a fuzzy set $\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$: 1. **Support** $S(\tilde{A})$ — The crisp set of *all* elements with **strictly positive** membership: $$S(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$ 2. **Core** $C(\tilde{A})$ — The crisp set of *all* elements with membership grade **exactly 1**: $$C(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$$ 3. **Height** $h(\tilde{A})$ — The **supremum** of the membership function over $X$: $$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X}\, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$ A fuzzy set is **normal** if $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A** — Set Theory Operations + Property Analysis **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Given two fuzzy sets on $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$: $$\tilde{A} = \{0.1/1 + 0.4/2 + 0.7/3 + 0.9/4 + 0.5/5\}$$ $$\tilde{B} = \{0.2/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.3/3 + 0.8/4 + 0.7/5\}$$ Find (i) $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, (ii) $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, (iii) $\tilde{A}^{c}$, (iv) $\tilde{B}^{c}$ using Zadeh operators. **(b) [7 Marks]** For the set $\tilde{A}$ above, find the support, core, height, and 0.6-cut. State whether $\tilde{A}$ is normal. --- ### **Solution to Question A** #### Part (a) — Zadeh Operations **Step 1: Union** $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$ — take the **maximum** grade for each element. | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\mu_{A \cup B} = \max$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | 0.2 | **0.2** | | 2 | 0.4 | 0.6 | **0.6** | | 3 | 0.7 | 0.3 | **0.7** | | 4 | 0.9 | 0.8 | **0.9** | | 5 | 0.5 | 0.7 | **0.7** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{0.2/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.7/3 + 0.9/4 + 0.7/5\}}$$ **Step 2: Intersection** $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$ — take the **minimum** grade for each element. | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_B$ | $\mu_{A \cap B} = \min$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | 0.2 | **0.1** | | 2 | 0.4 | 0.6 | **0.4** | | 3 | 0.7 | 0.3 | **0.3** | | 4 | 0.9 | 0.8 | **0.8** | | 5 | 0.5 | 0.7 | **0.5** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{0.1/1 + 0.4/2 + 0.3/3 + 0.8/4 + 0.5/5\}}$$ **Step 3: Complement** $\tilde{A}^{c}$ — subtract each grade from 1. | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\mu_{A^{c}} = 1 - \mu_A$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | **0.9** | | 2 | 0.4 | **0.6** | | 3 | 0.7 | **0.3** | | 4 | 0.9 | **0.1** | | 5 | 0.5 | **0.5** | $$\boxed{\tilde{A}^{c} = \{0.9/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.3/3 + 0.1/4 + 0.5/5\}}$$ **Step 4: Complement** $\tilde{B}^{c}$ — subtract each grade from 1. | $x$ | $\mu_B$ | $\mu_{B^{c}} = 1 - \mu_B$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.2 | **0.8** | | 2 | 0.6 | **0.4** | | 3 | 0.3 | **0.7** | | 4 | 0.8 | **0.2** | | 5 | 0.7 | **0.3** | $$\boxed{\tilde{B}^{c} = \{0.8/1 + 0.4/2 + 0.7/3 + 0.2/4 + 0.3/5\}}$$ #### Part (b) — Properties of $\tilde{A}$ **Support** $S(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$ — every element has a non-zero grade: $$\boxed{S(\tilde{A}) = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}}$$ **Core** $C(\tilde{A}) = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$ — no element has a grade of exactly 1: $$\boxed{C(\tilde{A}) = \varnothing\ \text{(empty set)}}$$ **Height** $h(\tilde{A}) = \max\{0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.9, 0.5\} = 0.9$ $$\boxed{h(\tilde{A}) = 0.9}$$ **Normality test:** Since $h(\tilde{A}) = 0.9 \neq 1$, the fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ is **sub-normal** (not normal). **0.6-cut** $A_{0.6} = \{x \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \ge 0.6\}$: | $x$ | $\mu_A$ | $\ge 0.6$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.1 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.4 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.7 | ✓ | | 4 | 0.9 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.5 | ✗ | $$\boxed{A_{0.6} = \{3, 4\}}$$ --- ### **Question B** — α-Cuts, Normalization & De Morgan's Verification (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** For the fuzzy set $$\tilde{C} = \{0.0/1 + 0.2/2 + 0.6/3 + 1.0/4 + 0.7/5 + 0.3/6 + 0.0/7\}$$ compute: (i) the **support**, (ii) the **core**, (iii) the **0.3-cut**, (iv) the **0.7-cut**, (v) the **crossover points**, and (vi) the **strong 0.5-cut** $C_{0.5}^{+}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** If $\tilde{C}$ were *not* normal, explain with a formula how it can be normalized. Also verify the **De Morgan's Law** $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$ for the sets given in Question A. --- ### **Solution to Question B** #### Part (a) — Properties of $\tilde{C}$ Membership grades for $\tilde{C}$: | $x$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $\mu_C$ | 0.0 | 0.2 | 0.6 | 1.0 | 0.7 | 0.3 | 0.0 | **(i) Support** $S(\tilde{C}) = \{x \mid \mu_C(x) > 0\}$: $$\boxed{S(\tilde{C}) = \{2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}}$$ **(ii) Core** $C(\tilde{C}) = \{x \mid \mu_C(x) = 1\}$: $$\boxed{C(\tilde{C}) = \{4\}}$$ **(iii) 0.3-cut** $C_{0.3} = \{x \mid \mu_C(x) \ge 0.3\}$: | $x$ | $\mu_C$ | $\ge 0.3$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.0 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.2 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.6 | ✓ | | 4 | 1.0 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.7 | ✓ | | 6 | 0.3 | ✓ | | 7 | 0.0 | ✗ | $$\boxed{C_{0.3} = \{3, 4, 5, 6\}}$$ **(iv) 0.7-cut** $C_{0.7} = \{x \mid \mu_C(x) \ge 0.7\}$: | $x$ | $\mu_C$ | $\ge 0.7$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.0 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.2 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.6 | ✗ | | 4 | 1.0 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.7 | ✓ | | 6 | 0.3 | ✗ | | 7 | 0.0 | ✗ | $$\boxed{C_{0.7} = \{4, 5\}}$$ **(v) Crossover Points** — Elements with $\mu_C(x) = 0.5$: **None.** $$\boxed{\text{Crossover points} = \varnothing}$$ **(vi) Strong 0.5-cut** $C_{0.5}^{+} = \{x \mid \mu_C(x) > 0.5\}$: | $x$ | $\mu_C$ | $> 0.5$? | |---|---|---| | 1 | 0.0 | ✗ | | 2 | 0.2 | ✗ | | 3 | 0.6 | ✓ | | 4 | 1.0 | ✓ | | 5 | 0.7 | ✓ | | 6 | 0.3 | ✗ | | 7 | 0.0 | ✗ | $$\boxed{C_{0.5}^{+} = \{3, 4, 5\}}$$ #### Part (b) — Normalization & De Morgan's Law **Normalization of a sub-normal fuzzy set:** If a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ has height $h(\tilde{A}) = h^* < 1$, it can be normalized by dividing every membership grade by $h^*$: $$\mu_{A_{\text{norm}}}(x) = \frac{\mu_A(x)}{h^*} = \frac{\mu_A(x)}{\sup_x \mu_A(x)}$$ For example, if $\tilde{A}$ had $h(\tilde{A}) = 0.8$, then for $x=4$ with $\mu_A(4) = 0.8$: $$\mu_{A_{\text{norm}}}(4) = \frac{0.8}{0.8} = 1.0$$ After normalization, the new height becomes exactly 1, making the set *normal*. **Verification of De Morgan's Law** $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$: **LHS:** From Question A, $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{0.1/1 + 0.4/2 + 0.3/3 + 0.8/4 + 0.5/5\}$. Taking the complement: $$(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \{0.9/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.7/3 + 0.2/4 + 0.5/5\}$$ **RHS:** From Question A, $\tilde{A}^{c} = \{0.9, 0.6, 0.3, 0.1, 0.5\}$ and $\tilde{B}^{c} = \{0.8, 0.4, 0.7, 0.2, 0.3\}$. Taking the union (max): | $x$ | $\mu_{A^{c}}$ | $\mu_{B^{c}}$ | $\max$ | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0.9 | 0.8 | **0.9** | | 2 | 0.6 | 0.4 | **0.6** | | 3 | 0.3 | 0.7 | **0.7** | | 4 | 0.1 | 0.2 | **0.2** | | 5 | 0.5 | 0.3 | **0.5** | $$\tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c} = \{0.9/1 + 0.6/2 + 0.7/3 + 0.2/4 + 0.5/5\}$$ **Comparison:** LHS = RHS = $\{0.9, 0.6, 0.7, 0.2, 0.5\}$ $$\boxed{\text{De Morgan's Law verified.}\ \checkmark}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the strict inequality** in the support definition — it is $\mu_A(x) > 0$, *not* $\ge 0$. Marks deducted if you include elements with grade exactly 0. > 2. **Confusing $\alpha$-cut with strong $\alpha$-cut** — they differ at the boundary ($\ge$ vs $>$). > 3. **Using $+$ for arithmetic sum** in fuzzy set notation — it is a *set union operator*, not addition. The expression is $\{0.1/1 + 0.3/2\}$, not $0.1/1 + 0.3/2 = 0.4/1$ (this is a very common KTU error). > 4. **Omitting the $0 \le \mu \le 1$ range** in any formal definition — the range clause is mandatory and carries marks. > 5. **Mixing up max and min** in union/intersection — examiners explicitly check the operator used. > 6. **Forgetting to test normality** in property-based problems — height check is almost always required. > 7. **Not verifying De Morgan's Law** in two columns (LHS and RHS) — show both, side-by-side. > 8. **Wrong symbol for fuzzy set** — must use tilde ($\tilde{A}$), not $A$ alone, in your formal notation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE** - ⭐ **Founder:** Lotfi A. Zadeh, University of California, Berkeley, **1965**. - ⭐ **Formal definition:** $\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$, where $0 \le \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \le 1$. - ⭐ **Notation rule:** Use $\sum$ and $\int$ for union of (element, grade) pairs in discrete and continuous universes — **not** for arithmetic. - ⭐ **Support** = strictly positive grades ($\mu > 0$). - ⭐ **Core** = grades exactly equal to 1 ($\mu = 1$). - ⭐ **Height** = supremum (max) of all grades. - ⭐ **Normal** $\iff$ height = 1; **Sub-normal** $\iff$ height < 1. - ⭐ **Crossover point** = element(s) with grade 0.5. - ⭐ **$\alpha$-cut** = grades $\ge \alpha$; **Strong $\alpha$-cut** = grades $> \alpha$. - ⭐ **Zadeh Union** = $\max(\mu_A, \mu_B)$. - ⭐ **Zadeh Intersection** = $\min(\mu_A, \mu_B)$. - ⭐ **Zadeh Complement** = $1 - \mu_A$. - ⭐ **Normalization formula:** $\mu_{\text{norm}}(x) = \mu(x) / h(\tilde{A})$. - ⭐ **Convex fuzzy set:** $\mu(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda) x_2) \ge \min(\mu_1, \mu_2)$. - ⭐ **Crisp vs Fuzzy:** binary vs graded membership; sharp vs smooth boundary; characteristic function vs membership function. - ⭐ **Operations to memorize for ESE:** Union (max), Intersection (min), Complement ($1-\mu$), Algebraic product, Algebraic sum, Bounded sum, Bounded difference. - ⭐ **De Morgan's Laws** hold for Zadeh operators — be ready to verify them in the exam. - ⭐ **Applications:** Washing machines, ABS braking, AC temperature control, camera autofocus, stock prediction, medical diagnosis. - ⭐ **Always show tabulated working** in your answer script — examiners give partial marks for clean tables.

topics.title: Properties and Operations on Fuzzy Sets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties and Operations on Fuzzy Sets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fuzzy Set

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuzzy Set (Zadeh, 1965 Notation):**
> A **Fuzzy Set** $\tilde{A}$ in a universe of discourse $X$ is characterized by a **membership function** $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ that maps each element $x \in X$ to a real number in the closed interval $[0, 1]$.
> $$\mu_{\tilde{A}} : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

The membership value $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$ represents the **degree of belonging** of $x$ to $\tilde{A}$. Mathematically, a fuzzy set is written as:

$$\tilde{A} = \int_{x \in X} \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{x} = \{(x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \ \vert\ x \in X\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - If $X$ is continuous, we use the integral sign $\int$ symbolically.
> - If $X$ is discrete, we use the summation sign $\sum$.
> - The symbol $/$ does **not** denote division — it is a *separator* between the membership grade and the element.

## 1.2 Universe of Discourse ($X$)

The **Universe of Discourse** is the collection of all possible elements relevant to a particular fuzzy concept. It is the *universal space* over which fuzzy sets are defined.

Example: For the concept of "comfortable room temperature", $X = [10^\circ C, 40^\circ C]$.

## 1.3 Membership Function ($\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$)

The **Membership Function** assigns a grade between **0 and 1** to each element:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = \begin{cases} 0 & \rightarrow x \text{ is definitely NOT a member of } \tilde{A} \\ 1 & \rightarrow x \text{ is definitely a member of } \tilde{A} \\ 0 < \mu < 1 & \rightarrow x \text{ is a partial member} \end{cases}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for any valid membership function:**
> $$0 \leq \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \leq 1 \quad \forall x \in X$$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Weather is Hot" 🌡️

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Plain English Explanation:**
> In **classical (crisp) set theory**, a temperature of $28^\circ C$ is either *Hot* (=1) or *Not Hot* (=0). There is no in-between.
>
> In **fuzzy logic**, the same temperature $28^\circ C$ could have:
> - $\mu_{\text{Hot}}(28) = 0.55$ (somewhat hot)
> - $\mu_{\text{Warm}}(28) = 0.85$ (very warm)
> - $\mu_{\text{Cold}}(28) = 0.10$ (barely cold)
>
> This reflects **human reasoning** better — language is rarely absolute! ☁️

## 1.5 Distinguishing Crisp vs Fuzzy Sets

| Feature | Crisp Set | Fuzzy Set |
|---|---|---|
| Membership values | $\{0, 1\}$ only | $[0, 1]$ (real numbers) |
| Boundary | Sharp, well-defined | Gradual, smooth |
| Notation | $x \in A$ or $x \notin A$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0,1]$ |
| Logic | Boolean (True/False) | Multi-valued |
| Example | $T \geq 30^\circ C$ is "Hot" | $T = 30^\circ C$ is "Hot" with degree $0.75$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Properties of Fuzzy Sets

### A. Support of a Fuzzy Set
> [!NOTE]
> The **Support** of $\tilde{A}$ is the crisp set of all elements with strictly positive membership.
> $$\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$

### B. Core of a Fuzzy Set
The **Core** contains all elements with full membership (degree = 1).
$$\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$$

### C. Height of a Fuzzy Set
The **Height** is the supremum (maximum) of all membership grades.
$$h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X}\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

### D. Normality
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A fuzzy set is **Normal** if its height equals 1. Otherwise, it is **Subnormal**.
> $$\tilde{A} \text{ is Normal} \iff h(\tilde{A}) = 1$$

### E. $\alpha$-Cut and Strict $\alpha$-Cut
> [!NOTE]
> An **$\alpha$-cut** of $\tilde{A}$ is a crisp set containing all elements with membership $\geq \alpha$.
> $$A_{\alpha} = \{x \in X \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}, \quad \alpha \in (0,1]$$
> **Strong $\alpha$-cut** uses strict inequality:
> $$A_{\alpha}^{+} = \{x \in X \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > \alpha\}$$

### F. Convexity
$\tilde{A}$ is **Convex** if for all $x_1, x_2 \in X$ and $\lambda \in [0,1]$:
$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(\lambda x_1 + (1-\lambda)x_2) \geq \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_1), \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x_2))$$

### G. Crossover Point
The point at which $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5$.

---

## 2.2 Standard Operations on Fuzzy Sets

Given two fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ over the same universe $X$:

### 1️⃣ Union (Logical OR)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

### 2️⃣ Intersection (Logical AND)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

### 3️⃣ Complement (Logical NOT)
$$\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$$

### 4️⃣ Algebraic Sum
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 5️⃣ Algebraic Product
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)$$

### 6️⃣ Bounded Sum
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(x) = \min(1,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x))$$

### 7️⃣ Bounded Difference
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) + \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x) - 1)$$

### 8️⃣ Concentration (CON) — Squeezes the fuzzy set
$$\text{CON}(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) = (\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x))^{2}$$

### 9️⃣ Dilation (DIL) — Stretches the fuzzy set
$$\text{DIL}(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) = (\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x))^{0.5} = 2\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) - (\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x))^{2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Normalization** converts a subnormal fuzzy set to a normal one by dividing all memberships by the height:
> $$\mu_{\tilde{A}_{\text{norm}}}(x) = \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{h(\tilde{A})}$$

---

## 2.3 Algebraic Properties of Fuzzy Set Operations

| # | Property | Union Form | Intersection Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Commutativity | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \tilde{B} \cup \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \tilde{B} \cap \tilde{A}$ |
| 2 | Associativity | $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cup \tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C})$ | $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cap \tilde{C} = \tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cap \tilde{C})$ |
| 3 | Distributivity | $\tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cap \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cap (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{C})$ | $\tilde{A} \cap (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C}) = (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}) \cup (\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{C})$ |
| 4 | Idempotency | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A} = \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A} = \tilde{A}$ |
| 5 | Identity | $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{\emptyset} = \tilde{A}$ | $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{X} = \tilde{A}$ |
| 6 | Involution | $(\tilde{A}^{c})^{c} = \tilde{A}$ | — |
| 7 | De Morgan's Law | $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$ | $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$ |
| 8 | Transitivity | If $\tilde{A} \subseteq \tilde{B}$ and $\tilde{B} \subseteq \tilde{C}$ then $\tilde{A} \subseteq \tilde{C}$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **The Law of Excluded Middle DOES NOT hold in general for fuzzy sets!**
> $$\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^{c} \neq X \quad \text{and} \quad \tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A}^{c} \neq \tilde{\emptyset}$$
> This is a **famous KTU exam trap** — do not claim Boolean identity here!

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Washing Machines:** Fuzzy logic classifies "dirtiness" and "load size" into linguistic grades (Low, Medium, High) and adjusts wash cycle accordingly.
- **Air Conditioners:** Temperature "comfort" is fuzzy; AC control uses membership functions of "Cold", "Cool", "Warm", "Hot".
- **Automotive Braking (ABS):** "Slippery road" is a fuzzy concept; sensors map slip ratio to fuzzy sets.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** Diseases like "Fever" are fuzzy; patient temperature maps to membership grades.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Master Example — Full Computation on Two Fuzzy Sets

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Problem:**
> Let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$. Define two fuzzy sets:
> $$\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.2),\ (2, 0.5),\ (3, 0.8),\ (4, 1.0),\ (5, 0.6)\}$$
> $$\tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.3),\ (2, 0.6),\ (3, 0.9),\ (4, 0.4),\ (5, 0.7)\}$$
> Compute: (a) Union, (b) Intersection, (c) Complement of A, (d) Algebraic Sum, (e) Algebraic Product, (f) Bounded Sum, (g) Bounded Difference, (h) $\alpha$-cut of A for $\alpha = 0.6$.

---

### (a) Union: $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$  →  $\mu(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \mu_{\tilde{B}})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(1) &= \max(0.2, 0.3) = 0.3 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(2) &= \max(0.5, 0.6) = 0.6 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(3) &= \max(0.8, 0.9) = 0.9 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(4) &= \max(1.0, 0.4) = 1.0 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(5) &= \max(0.6, 0.7) = 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.3),\ (2, 0.6),\ (3, 0.9),\ (4, 1.0),\ (5, 0.7)\}}$$

---

### (b) Intersection: $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$  →  $\mu(x) = \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \mu_{\tilde{B}})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(1) &= \min(0.2, 0.3) = 0.2 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(2) &= \min(0.5, 0.6) = 0.5 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(3) &= \min(0.8, 0.9) = 0.8 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(4) &= \min(1.0, 0.4) = 0.4 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}}(5) &= \min(0.6, 0.7) = 0.6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.2),\ (2, 0.5),\ (3, 0.8),\ (4, 0.4),\ (5, 0.6)\}}$$

---

### (c) Complement of $\tilde{A}$: $\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(x) = 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(1) &= 1 - 0.2 = 0.8 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(2) &= 1 - 0.5 = 0.5 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(3) &= 1 - 0.8 = 0.2 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(4) &= 1 - 1.0 = 0.0 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(5) &= 1 - 0.6 = 0.4
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A}^{c} = \{(1, 0.8),\ (2, 0.5),\ (3, 0.2),\ (4, 0.0),\ (5, 0.4)\}}$$

---

### (d) Algebraic Sum: $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}} + \mu_{\tilde{B}} - \mu_{\tilde{A}} \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(1) &= 0.2 + 0.3 - (0.2)(0.3) = 0.5 - 0.06 = 0.44 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(2) &= 0.5 + 0.6 - (0.5)(0.6) = 1.1 - 0.30 = 0.80 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(3) &= 0.8 + 0.9 - (0.8)(0.9) = 1.7 - 0.72 = 0.98 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(4) &= 1.0 + 0.4 - (1.0)(0.4) = 1.4 - 0.40 = 1.00 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B}}(5) &= 0.6 + 0.7 - (0.6)(0.7) = 1.3 - 0.42 = 0.88
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.44),\ (2, 0.80),\ (3, 0.98),\ (4, 1.00),\ (5, 0.88)\}}$$

---

### (e) Algebraic Product: $\mu(x) = \mu_{\tilde{A}} \cdot \mu_{\tilde{B}}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(1) &= (0.2)(0.3) = 0.06 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(2) &= (0.5)(0.6) = 0.30 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(3) &= (0.8)(0.9) = 0.72 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(4) &= (1.0)(0.4) = 0.40 \\
\mu_{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B}}(5) &= (0.6)(0.7) = 0.42
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \odot \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.06),\ (2, 0.30),\ (3, 0.72),\ (4, 0.40),\ (5, 0.42)\}}$$

---

### (f) Bounded Sum: $\mu(x) = \min(1,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}} + \mu_{\tilde{B}})$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu(1) &= \min(1,\ 0.2 + 0.3) = \min(1, 0.5) = 0.50 \\
\mu(2) &= \min(1,\ 0.5 + 0.6) = \min(1, 1.1) = 1.00 \\
\mu(3) &= \min(1,\ 0.8 + 0.9) = \min(1, 1.7) = 1.00 \\
\mu(4) &= \min(1,\ 1.0 + 0.4) = \min(1, 1.4) = 1.00 \\
\mu(5) &= \min(1,\ 0.6 + 0.7) = \min(1, 1.3) = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.50),\ (2, 1.00),\ (3, 1.00),\ (4, 1.00),\ (5, 1.00)\}}$$

---

### (g) Bounded Difference: $\mu(x) = \max(0,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}} + \mu_{\tilde{B}} - 1)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu(1) &= \max(0,\ 0.5 - 1) = \max(0, -0.5) = 0.00 \\
\mu(2) &= \max(0,\ 1.1 - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.1) = 0.10 \\
\mu(3) &= \max(0,\ 1.7 - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.7) = 0.70 \\
\mu(4) &= \max(0,\ 1.4 - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.4) = 0.40 \\
\mu(5) &= \max(0,\ 1.3 - 1) = \max(0,\ 0.3) = 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\tilde{A} \ominus \tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.00),\ (2, 0.10),\ (3, 0.70),\ (4, 0.40),\ (5, 0.30)\}}$$

---

### (h) $\alpha$-Cut of $\tilde{A}$ for $\alpha = 0.6$ → $A_{0.6} = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.6\}$

Checking each element:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(1) = 0.2 &< 0.6 \rightarrow \text{EXCLUDED} \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(2) = 0.5 &< 0.6 \rightarrow \text{EXCLUDED} \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(3) = 0.8 &\geq 0.6 \rightarrow \text{INCLUDED} \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(4) = 1.0 &\geq 0.6 \rightarrow \text{INCLUDED} \\
\mu_{\tilde{A}}(5) = 0.6 &\geq 0.6 \rightarrow \text{INCLUDED}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{A_{0.6} = \{3, 4, 5\}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** $\alpha = 0.6$ is itself included because the $\alpha$-cut uses the inequality $\geq$, not $>$.

---

## 3.2 Verification: De Morgan's Law for Fuzzy Sets

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem:** $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$

**Proof using memberships** (let $x \in X$ be arbitrary):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c}}(x) &= 1 - \mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) \\
&= 1 - \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) \\
&= \min(1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ 1 - \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) \quad \text{(since } 1 - \max(a,b) = \min(1-a, 1-b)\text{)} \\
&= \min(\mu_{\tilde{A}^{c}}(x),\ \mu_{\tilde{B}^{c}}(x)) \\
&= \mu_{\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}}(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since this holds for all $x \in X$:
$$\boxed{(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

The second De Morgan's law $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$ follows by identical reasoning, replacing $\max$ with $\min$.

# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Operations and Properties Relationship Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[FUZZY SET A] --> B[OPERATIONS]
    C[FUZZY SET B] --> B
    B --> B1[Union - max]
    B --> B2[Intersection - min]
    B --> B3[Complement - 1 minus mu]
    B --> B4[Algebraic Sum and Product]
    B --> B5[Bounded Sum and Difference]
    B --> B6[Concentration and Dilation]
    B1 --> P[ALGEBRAIC PROPERTIES]
    B2 --> P
    B3 --> P
    B4 --> P
    B5 --> P
    B6 --> P
    P --> P1[Commutativity]
    P --> P2[Associativity]
    P --> P3[Distributivity]
    P --> P4[De Morgans Law]
    P --> P5[Idempotency]
    P --> P6[Involution]
    P --> P7[Identity]
    A --> Q[STRUCTURAL PROPERTIES]
    C --> Q
    Q --> Q1[Support supp A]
    Q --> Q2[Core of A]
    Q --> Q3[Height h of A]
    Q --> Q4[Normality]
    Q --> Q5[Alpha cut A alpha]
    Q --> Q6[Convexity]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Fuzzy Set Operation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUTA[Input: Fuzzy Set A Memberships] --> OPENG[Operation Engine]
    INPUTB[Input: Fuzzy Set B Memberships] --> OPENG
    OPENG --> OPMOD[Operation Mode Selector]
    OPMOD --> M1[Max based Union]
    OPMOD --> M2[Min based Intersection]
    OPMOD --> M3[1 minus Complement]
    OPMOD --> M4[Algebraic Product]
    OPMOD --> M5[Algebraic Sum]
    OPMOD --> M6[Bounded Sum min 1]
    OPMOD --> M7[Bounded Difference max 0]
    M1 --> OUTPUT[Output Fuzzy Set]
    M2 --> OUTPUT
    M3 --> OUTPUT
    M4 --> OUTPUT
    M5 --> OUTPUT
    M6 --> OUTPUT
    M7 --> OUTPUT
    OUTPUT --> VAL[Property Validator: Checks Commutativity, De Morgans, etc.]
    VAL --> RES[Validated Result]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Choosing the Right Operation

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Given Fuzzy Sets A and B] --> Q1{Need maximum degree?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| U[Use Union with max]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Need minimum degree?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| I[Use Intersection with min]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Need negation?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| C[Use Complement with 1 minus mu]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Need bounded result?}
    Q4 -->|Yes, sum| BS[Use Bounded Sum min 1]
    Q4 -->|Yes, difference| BD[Use Bounded Difference max 0]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Need transformation?}
    Q5 -->|Yes, sharpen| CON[Use Concentration mu squared]
    Q5 -->|Yes, spread| DIL[Use Dilation sqrt mu]
```

   A      │  0.2   │  0.5   │  0.8   │  1.0   │  0.6   │
          ├────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤

          ├────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤

          ├────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤
 A∩B     │  0.2   │  0.5   │  0.8   │  0.4   │  0.6   │  ← min
          ├────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┼────────┤
 A^c     │  0.8   │  0.5   │  0.2   │  0.0   │  0.4   │  ← 1 - μ

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a fuzzy set with an example. Explain the concept of membership function.**

**Model Answer:**

A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on a universe of discourse $X$ is a set of ordered pairs:
$$\tilde{A} = \{(x,\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \ \vert\ x \in X\}$$
where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ is the membership function that assigns a grade in $[0, 1]$ to each $x \in X$.

**Example:** Let $X = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ represent temperature levels. Define a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ = "Comfortable":
$$\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.1),\ (2, 0.4),\ (3, 0.9),\ (4, 0.6),\ (5, 0.2)\}$$
Here, $3$ is the most comfortable with $\mu = 0.9$, while $1$ is barely comfortable with $\mu = 0.1$.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State and prove De Morgan's law for fuzzy sets.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement:** For any two fuzzy sets $\tilde{A}$ and $\tilde{B}$ on universe $X$:
> 1. $(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}$
> 2. $(\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B})^{c} = \tilde{A}^{c} \cup \tilde{B}^{c}$

**Proof of (1):** For any $x \in X$,
$$\mu_{(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B})^{c}}(x) = 1 - \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) = \min(1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x),\ 1 - \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) = \mu_{\tilde{A}^{c} \cap \tilde{B}^{c}}(x)$$

This holds for all $x$, hence proved. $\blacksquare$

(Proof of (2) follows similarly using $\min$ and $\max$ duality.)

---

## 📝 PART B — Choice-Based Long Answer (14 Marks)

### ✏️ Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the various properties of fuzzy sets such as support, core, height, normality, and $\alpha$-cut with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given $\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.1), (2, 0.6), (3, 0.9), (4, 0.7), (5, 0.3)\}$ and $\tilde{B} = \{(1, 0.4), (2, 0.5), (3, 0.8), (4, 0.2), (5, 0.9)\}$. Compute $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B}$, $\tilde{A}^{c}$, algebraic sum, and $A_{0.5}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Properties of Fuzzy Sets

Let $\tilde{A} = \{(x_1, 0.2), (x_2, 0.7), (x_3, 1.0), (x_4, 0.5), (x_5, 0.0)\}$.

1. **Support:** $\text{supp}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\} = \{x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4\}$
   *(excludes $x_5$ since $\mu = 0$)*

2. **Core:** $\text{core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\} = \{x_3\}$

3. **Height:** $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup\{0.2, 0.7, 1.0, 0.5, 0.0\} = 1.0$

4. **Normality:** Since $h(\tilde{A}) = 1.0$, $\tilde{A}$ is **Normal**.

5. **$\alpha$-cut for $\alpha = 0.6$:** $A_{0.6} = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.6\} = \{x_2, x_3\}$

6. **Crossover point:** The element $x$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5 \Rightarrow x_4$.

7. **Convexity:** The set is convex if its $\alpha$-cuts are intervals (or contiguous crisp sets) for all $\alpha$.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — Numerical Computation

| $x$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{B}}$ | $\max$ (Union) | $\min$ (Intersect) | $1 - \mu_{\tilde{A}}$ (Comp) | $\mu_A + \mu_B - \mu_A\mu_B$ (Alg Sum) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.1 | 0.4 | 0.4 | 0.1 | 0.9 | $0.1+0.4-0.04 = 0.46$ |
| 2 | 0.6 | 0.5 | 0.6 | 0.5 | 0.4 | $0.6+0.5-0.30 = 0.80$ |
| 3 | 0.9 | 0.8 | 0.9 | 0.8 | 0.1 | $0.9+0.8-0.72 = 0.98$ |
| 4 | 0.7 | 0.2 | 0.7 | 0.2 | 0.3 | $0.7+0.2-0.14 = 0.76$ |
| 5 | 0.3 | 0.9 | 0.9 | 0.3 | 0.7 | $0.3+0.9-0.27 = 0.93$ |

**Union:** $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B} = \{(1,0.4), (2,0.6), (3,0.9), (4,0.7), (5,0.9)\}$

**Intersection:** $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{B} = \{(1,0.1), (2,0.5), (3,0.8), (4,0.2), (5,0.3)\}$

**Complement:** $\tilde{A}^{c} = \{(1,0.9), (2,0.4), (3,0.1), (4,0.3), (5,0.7)\}$

**Algebraic Sum:** $\tilde{A} \oplus \tilde{B} = \{(1,0.46), (2,0.80), (3,0.98), (4,0.76), (5,0.93)\}$

**$\alpha$-cut at $\alpha = 0.5$:** $A_{0.5} = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq 0.5\} = \{2, 3, 4\}$

---

### ✏️ Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** State and prove the algebraic properties (commutativity, associativity, distributivity, De Morgan's law) of fuzzy set operations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the $\alpha$-cut decomposition theorem with a suitable example. State its significance. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### (a) Model Solution — Algebraic Properties

Let $\tilde{A}, \tilde{B}, \tilde{C}$ be fuzzy sets on $X$, and $x \in X$ arbitrary.

**1. Commutativity:**
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{B}}(x)) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{B}}(x), \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) = \mu_{\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{A}}(x) \quad \blacksquare$$

**2. Associativity:**
$$\mu_{(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cup \tilde{C}}(x) = \max(\max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \mu_{\tilde{B}}), \mu_{\tilde{C}}) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \max(\mu_{\tilde{B}}, \mu_{\tilde{C}})) = \mu_{\tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cup \tilde{C})}(x) \quad \blacksquare$$

**3. Distributivity:** 
$$\mu_{\tilde{A} \cup (\tilde{B} \cap \tilde{C})}(x) = \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \min(\mu_{\tilde{B}}, \mu_{\tilde{C}}))$$
$$= \min(\max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \mu_{\tilde{B}}), \max(\mu_{\tilde{A}}, \mu_{\tilde{C}})) = \mu_{(\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{B}) \cap (\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{C})}(x) \quad \blacksquare$$

**4. De Morgan's Law:** Proven in Section 3.2 above.

---

#### (b) Model Solution — $\alpha$-Cut Decomposition Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Decomposition Theorem):**
> Any fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ can be represented as a union of crisp sets weighted by $\alpha$:
> $$\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha \in (0, 1]} \alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$$
> where $\alpha \cdot A_{\alpha}$ is a fuzzy set whose membership is $\alpha$ for $x \in A_{\alpha}$ and $0$ otherwise.

**Example:** Let $\tilde{A} = \{(1, 0.3), (2, 0.7), (3, 1.0), (4, 0.5)\}$.

Find $\alpha$-cuts:
- $A_{0.3} = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$
- $A_{0.5} = \{2, 3, 4\}$
- $A_{0.7} = \{2, 3\}$
- $A_{1.0} = \{3\}$

Reconstructing:
- $0.3 \cdot A_{0.3} = \{(1,0.3), (2,0.3), (3,0.3), (4,0.3)\}$
- $0.5 \cdot A_{0.5} = \{(2,0.5), (3,0.5), (4,0.5)\}$
- $0.7 \cdot A_{0.7} = \{(2,0.7), (3,0.7)\}$
- $1.0 \cdot A_{1.0} = \{(3,1.0)\}$

Union (taking max at each element):
- $1$: $0.3$
- $2$: $\max(0.3, 0.5, 0.7) = 0.7$
- $3$: $\max(0.3, 0.5, 0.7, 1.0) = 1.0$
- $4$: $\max(0.3, 0.5) = 0.5$

✅ **Reconstruction matches $\tilde{A}$ exactly!**

> [!TIP]
> **Significance:** This theorem bridges **fuzzy sets** and **crisp sets** — it allows us to convert a fuzzy set into a countable (or finite) collection of crisp sets, which is foundational for fuzzy logic controllers and defuzzification in real-world systems like washing machines and ACs.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Law of Excluded Middle trap** — Many students wrongly write $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^{c} = X$. In fuzzy logic, this is **NOT** always true. (Max loss: 2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing Algebraic Sum with Bounded Sum** — Algebraic sum uses subtraction: $a+b-ab$. Bounded sum uses $\min(1, a+b)$. (Max loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting parentheses in $\alpha$-cut** — The condition is $\mu \geq \alpha$ (weak), NOT $\mu > \alpha$ (strong). (Max loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Not normalizing** — If a question asks "is this fuzzy set normal?", check height $h = 1$ explicitly. (Max loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping verbal steps in proofs** — KTU examiners want at least 2-3 lines of verbal reasoning between equations. (Max loss: 1-2 marks)

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Recall Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ A **fuzzy set** maps $X \rightarrow [0, 1]$ via its membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$.
- ✅ **Union** uses $\max$; **Intersection** uses $\min$; **Complement** uses $1 - \mu$.
- ✅ **Algebraic Sum** $= \mu_A + \mu_B - \mu_A \mu_B$; **Algebraic Product** $= \mu_A \mu_B$.
- ✅ **Bounded Sum** $= \min(1, \mu_A + \mu_B)$; **Bounded Difference** $= \max(0, \mu_A + \mu_B - 1)$.
- ✅ **Support** = elements with $\mu > 0$; **Core** = elements with $\mu = 1$.
- ✅ **Normal** fuzzy set has $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$; otherwise, divide by height to normalize.
- ✅ **$\alpha$-cut** $A_\alpha = \{x \ \vert\ \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \geq \alpha\}$ — note the weak inequality.
- ✅ **Convex fuzzy set**: $\alpha$-cuts are contiguous intervals for all $\alpha$.
- ✅ **Concentration** = $\mu^2$ (sharpens); **Dilation** = $\sqrt{\mu}$ or $2\mu - \mu^2$ (broadens).
- ✅ All standard properties (commutativity, associativity, distributivity, De Morgan's, involution, idempotency, identity) **hold**.
- ❌ **Law of Excluded Middle** does **NOT** hold: $\tilde{A} \cup \tilde{A}^{c} \neq X$ in general.
- ❌ **Law of Non-Contradiction** does **NOT** hold: $\tilde{A} \cap \tilde{A}^{c} \neq \emptyset$ in general.
- 🎯 **Decomposition Theorem**: $\tilde{A} = \bigcup_{\alpha} \alpha \cdot A_\alpha$ — bridges fuzzy and crisp worlds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuzzy Membership Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In classical (crisp) set theory, an element $x$ either **belongs to** a set $A$ or **does not belong to** it — there is no middle ground. The membership is binary: either $\mathbf{1}$ (member) or $\mathbf{0}$ (non-member).

In **Fuzzy Set Theory** (introduced by **Lotfi A. Zadeh in 1965**), this rigid boundary is replaced by a continuous grade of membership. A **Fuzzy Membership Function (MF)** is a mathematical function that maps every element $x$ in the universe of discourse $X$ to a real number in the closed unit interval $[0, 1]$, representing the **degree of belonging** of $x$ to a fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$.

Formally:

$$\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) : X \rightarrow [0, 1]$$

where:
- $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ means $x$ is a **full member** of $\tilde{A}$.
- $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0$ means $x$ is **definitely not a member** of $\tilde{A}$.
- $0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1$ means $x$ is a **partial member** of $\tilde{A}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ in $X$ is commonly expressed as:
> $$\tilde{A} = \{(x, \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)) \mid x \in X\}$$
> When $X$ is continuous, this is written as:
> $$\tilde{A} = \int_{X} \frac{\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)}{x} \, dx$$
> The integral sign here is **not** a classical integral — it denotes the **collection/union** of all $x$ values with their respective membership grades.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "How tall is a 'tall' person?"**
>
> In **crisp logic**, we would draw a hard line: anyone $\geq 6$ feet is "tall," anyone below is "not tall." A person who is $5'11.5''$ is suddenly "not tall," which is absurd.
>
> In **fuzzy logic**, the membership function gradually rises from $0$ (e.g., at $4$ feet) to $1$ (e.g., at $7$ feet). A $5'9''$ person might have a membership of $0.65$ in the fuzzy set "tall," a $6'2''$ person might have $0.92$. This captures the **linguistic uncertainty** of human reasoning.

## 1.3 Key Features of a Membership Function

For any fuzzy set $\tilde{A}$ defined on $X$ with membership function $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$:

- **Support:** The set of all $x \in X$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0$.

$$\text{Support}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) > 0\}$$

- **Core:** The set of all $x \in X$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1$ (full membership).

$$\text{Core}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 1\}$$

- **Boundary:** The set of all $x \in X$ where $0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1$ (partial membership).

$$\text{Boundary}(\tilde{A}) = \{x \in X \mid 0 < \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) < 1\}$$

- **Crossover Points:** The values of $x$ where $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) = 0.5$.
- **Fuzzy Singleton:** A fuzzy set whose support contains only a single point (and $\mu = 1$ at that point). These are widely used in **fuzzification** of crisp inputs in fuzzy inference systems.
- **Height:** The maximum value attained by the MF, i.e., $h(\tilde{A}) = \sup_{x \in X} \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x)$. If $h(\tilde{A}) = 1$, the set is called **normal**; otherwise **subnormal**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Membership functions are the **building blocks of fuzzy logic controllers (FLCs)** — the same controllers used in washing machines (LG, Samsung), air conditioners (Mitsubishi), autofocus cameras, anti-lock braking systems (ABS), and subway train braking (Sendai, Japan, 1987 — the world's first FLC application).

## 1.4 Crisp Set vs Fuzzy Set — A Comparative Snapshot

| Property | Crisp Set $A$ | Fuzzy Set $\tilde{A}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Membership value | $0$ or $1$ | Any value in $[0, 1]$ |
| Membership function | $\chi_A(x) \in \{0, 1\}$ | $\mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \in [0, 1]$ |
| Boundary | Sharp, well-defined | Smooth, gradual |
| Notation | $x \in A$ or $x \notin A$ | $x \in \tilde{A}$ with degree $\mu$ |
| Example | "Adult = age $\geq 18$" | "Young = degree of youth" |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 'Why' Behind Membership Functions

The central philosophical shift from crisp to fuzzy logic is this:

> **Human reasoning is approximate, not exact.** Classical set theory cannot represent linguistic terms like "warm," "high speed," or "somewhat old." Membership functions provide a **mathematically rigorous bridge** between linguistic variables and numerical computation.

Every membership function must satisfy three baseline properties:

1. **Boundedness:** $0 \leq \mu_{\tilde{A}}(x) \leq 1$ for all $x \in X$.
2. **Non-emptiness:** The fuzzy set has at least one element (often with non-zero membership).
3. **Context-dependence:** The shape of the MF is decided by **domain expert knowledge**, not derived mathematically.

## 2.2 Classification of Membership Functions

Membership functions are broadly classified into:

- **Piecewise Linear MFs** — Triangular, Trapezoidal (most common in FLCs due to simplicity).
- **Smooth / Curved MFs** — Gaussian, Generalized Bell, Sigmoidal.
- **S-Shaped, Z-Shaped, and Pi-Shaped MFs** — Used for transitions.
- **Singleton MF** — Used in fuzzification of crisp sensor inputs.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes every formula you must memorize for KTU ESE.

| MF Type | Mathematical Formula | Parameters | Shape Characteristics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Triangular (trimf)** | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, \dfrac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$ | $a < b < c$ | Peak at $b$, slopes linearly to $0$ at $a$ and $c$ |
| **Trapezoidal (trapmf)** | $\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}, 1, \dfrac{d-x}{d-c}\right)\right)$ | $a < b \leq c < d$ | Flat top between $b$ and $c$, slopes to $0$ at $a$ and $d$ |
| **Gaussian (gaussmf)** | $\mu(x) = \exp\left(-\dfrac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$ | Center $c$, width $\sigma > 0$ | Smooth bell, infinitely differentiable, never reaches $0$ |
| **Generalized Bell (gbellmf)** | $\mu(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$ | $a > 0$ (width), $b > 0$ (slope), $c$ (center) | Smooth bell with adjustable slopes |
| **Sigmoidal** | $\mu(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-a(x-c)}}$ | Slope $a$, inflection $c$ | Open left/right, used for "very high" or "very low" |
| **S-function** | Piecewise cubic spline from $0$ to $1$ between $x = a$ and $x = b$ | $a$ (foot), $b$ (shoulder) | Smooth S-shaped transition |
| **Z-function** | Mirror of S-function; $1 - S(x)$ | $a$, $b$ | Reverse S-shape |
| **Pi-function ($\pi$-MF)** | Product or composition of S and Z functions | $a, b, c, d$ | Bell-like with flat boundaries |
| **Singleton** | $\mu(x) = 1$ if $x = x_{0}$, else $0$ | Single point $x_{0}$ | Spike at $x_0$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Never confuse the **height** of an MF with its **support width**. Height is the maximum $\mu$ value; support is the domain range where $\mu > 0$. A Gaussian MF has $\text{height} = 1$ but **infinite support** (it never strictly reaches $0$).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| MF Type | Typical Application Domain |
|---|---|
| Triangular | Temperature control in HVAC, washing machine fuzzy controllers |
| Trapezoidal | Speed/lane control in autonomous vehicles, gear-shift logic |
| Gaussian | Image processing (fuzzy edge detection), noise modeling |
| Generalized Bell | Adaptive fuzzy systems (e.g., ANFIS — Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems) |
| Sigmoidal | "Very High" / "Very Low" hedges in linguistic variables |
| Singleton | Fuzzification interface (converting crisp sensor data into fuzzy input) |

## 2.5 Choosing the Right Membership Function — Heuristics

- **Simplicity required** (real-time embedded FLC): Use **Triangular or Trapezoidal**.
- **Smoothness / differentiability required** (training via gradient descent, e.g., ANFIS): Use **Gaussian or Generalized Bell**.
- **Asymmetric uncertainty**: Use **Sigmoidal, S, or Z functions** (e.g., "very cold" is a left-open curve).
- **Single crisp measurement** (e.g., sensor value): Use **Singleton MF** for direct fuzzification.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "design MFs for a given problem," always **justify the choice** with a one-line reason such as "Triangular MF is selected due to its low computational cost and ease of parameter tuning."

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Triangular Membership Function

A triangular MF with parameters $a, b, c$ (where $a < b < c$) is defined over the universe $X = \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the three regions of the function:**

The MF behaves differently in three disjoint intervals: $(-\infty, a]$, $[a, c]$, and $[c, \infty)$.

**Step 2 — Construct the rising line from $a$ to $b$:**

On the interval $[a, b]$, the function rises linearly from $0$ to $1$. Using the two-point form of a line through $(a, 0)$ and $(b, 1)$:

$$\mu(x) = \frac{x - a}{b - a}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the falling line from $b$ to $c$:**

On the interval $[b, c]$, the function falls linearly from $1$ to $0$. Using the two-point form of a line through $(b, 1)$ and $(c, 0)$:

$$\mu(x) = \frac{c - x}{c - b}$$

**Step 4 — Define behaviour outside the support:**

For $x \leq a$ and $x \geq c$, the function is $0$ (outside support).

**Step 5 — Combine all three regions using `min` and `max` operators:**

$$\mu(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x \leq a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ \dfrac{c - x}{c - b}, & b \leq x \leq c \\ 0, & x \geq c \end{cases}$$

Compactly:

$$\mu(x) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{x-a}{b-a}, \frac{c-x}{c-b}\right)\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Gaussian Membership Function

A Gaussian MF is centred at $c$ with standard deviation $\sigma$.

**Step 1 — Begin with the classical Gaussian (normal) probability density function:**

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left(-\frac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Observe that the constant $\dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}$ is only a vertical scaling factor.**

In fuzzy logic, we only need the shape, not the probabilistic normalization. The maximum value of the standard Gaussian is at $x = c$, where $(x - c)^{2} = 0$, giving $\exp(0) = 1$. We discard the scaling constant and set the height to $1$ (making it a *normal* fuzzy set).

**Step 3 — Write the resulting fuzzy MF:**

$$\mu(x) = \exp\left(-\frac{(x-c)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary behaviour:**

- At $x = c$: $\mu(c) = \exp(0) = 1$ ✓ (maximum).
- As $x \to \pm\infty$: $\mu(x) \to 0$ ✓ (asymptotic to zero).
- Symmetric about $x = c$ ✓.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Because the Gaussian MF **never** strictly equals $0$, its support is technically the **entire real line**, not a finite interval. In practice, we truncate it at $\mu = 0.05$ for computational purposes.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Generalized Bell Membership Function

The generalized bell MF is defined as:

$$\mu(x) = \frac{1}{1 + \left\vert \frac{x - c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}}$$

**Step 1 — Verify the maximum value.**

At $x = c$, the term $\left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert = 0$, and $0^{2b} = 0$ (for $b > 0$). Thus:

$$\mu(c) = \frac{1}{1 + 0} = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Verify the asymptotic behaviour.**

As $x \to \pm\infty$, the term $\left\vert \dfrac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b} \to \infty$, so $\mu(x) \to 0$ ✓.

**Step 3 — Determine the crossover points (where $\mu = 0.5$):**

$$0.5 = \frac{1}{1 + \left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b}} \implies 1 + \left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b} = 2 \implies \left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert^{2b} = 1$$

$$\left\vert \frac{x-c}{a} \right\vert = 1 \implies x = c \pm a$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the parameters:**

- $c$ is the **centre** of the curve (point of maximum membership).
- $a$ is the **half-width** of the curve at the crossover point $\mu = 0.5$.
- $b$ is the **slope controller** — larger $b$ gives steeper sides; smaller $b$ gives broader shoulders.

> [!TIP]
> The generalized bell is preferred over the Gaussian in **adaptive systems (ANFIS)** because its parameters $a, b, c$ can be tuned using gradient descent (Gaussian's $\sigma$ and $c$ lack one tunable "slope" parameter).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** Consider the fuzzy set "Comfortable Temperature" defined on $X = [0, 40]^\circ C$ with the following membership function:
>
> - "Cold": Triangular MF with parameters $a = 0, b = 10, c = 20$.
> - "Warm": Triangular MF with parameters $a = 15, b = 22, c = 30$.
> - "Hot": Triangular MF with parameters $a = 25, b = 32, c = 40$.
>
> Calculate the membership grades for $T = 18^\circ C$ in each fuzzy set.

**Solution:**

**For "Cold" with $T = 18$:**

$$\mu_{\text{Cold}}(18) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{18-0}{10-0}, \frac{20-18}{20-10}\right)\right) = \max\left(0, \min(1.8, 0.2)\right) = \max(0, 0.2) = 0.2$$

**For "Warm" with $T = 18$:**

$$\mu_{\text{Warm}}(18) = \max\left(0, \min\left(\frac{18-15}{22-15}, \frac{30-18}{30-22}\right)\right) = \max\left(0, \min(0.4286, 1.5)\right) = \max(0, 0.4286) \approx 0.43$$

**For "Hot" with $T = 18$:**

Since $18 < a = 25$, the temperature lies to the left of the support, so:

$$\mu_{\text{Hot}}(18) = 0$$

**Interpretation:** A temperature of $18^\circ C$ is **20% Cold** and **43% Warm** and **0% Hot** — i.e., it is more "warm" than "cold" but not fully warm. This is the kind of overlapping membership that allows smooth, human-like transitions in fuzzy control.

## 3.5 Core, Support, and Boundary — A Computed Example

For the "Cold" MF with parameters $a = 0, b = 10, c = 20$:

- **Support:** $\text{Support} = \{x \mid \mu(x) > 0\} = (0, 20)$.
- **Core:** $\text{Core} = \{x \mid \mu(x) = 1\} = \{10\}$ (a single point — peak of the triangle).
- **Boundary:** $\text{Boundary} = \{x \mid 0 < \mu(x) < 1\} = (0, 10) \cup (10, 20)$.

For a **trapezoidal** MF with $a = 0, b = 5, c = 15, d = 20$:

- **Support:** $(0, 20)$.
- **Core:** $[5, 15]$ (a flat plateau — a 10-degree interval of full membership).
- **Boundary:** $(0, 5) \cup (15, 20)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference:** A **triangular MF has a single-point core**, while a **trapezoidal MF has a flat core interval** — this is the primary reason trapezoidal MFs are preferred when "full membership" should hold over a range, not just a single point.

    paramD1 --> useE1["USE REAL TIME FLC WASHING MACHINES"]
