# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Fuzzy Logic Controller. List its four main components.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Fuzzy Logic Controller (FLC)** is a knowledge-based, rule-driven control system that uses fuzzy set theory and approximate reasoning to convert crisp sensor inputs into a crisp control signal, **without requiring a mathematical model of the plant**.

The four main components are:
1. **Fuzzification Interface** – converts crisp inputs into fuzzy membership values.
2. **Knowledge Base** – comprises the *Data Base* (membership functions) and *Rule Base* (IF–THEN rules).
3. **Inference Engine** – performs fuzzy reasoning, implication, and aggregation.
4. **Defuzzification Interface** – converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a single crisp value.

---

### **Q2. Compare Mamdani and Takagi–Sugeno fuzzy inference methods.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Mamdani | Takagi–Sugeno |
|---|---|---|
| Consequent (THEN part) | Fuzzy set | Crisp function (linear / constant) |
| Defuzzification | Required (centroid, etc.) | Not required; weighted average of $y_k = f(x)$ |
| Computational cost | High | Low |
| Interpretability | High (linguistic) | Moderate |
| Rule form | IF $x$ is $A$ THEN $y$ is $B$ | IF $x$ is $A$ THEN $y = p x + q$ |
| Output type | Non-linear surface | Linear / polynomial surface |

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** *Either (a) or (b) from Q1, and either (a) or (b) from Q2. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.*

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — Full 14-Mark Problem

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]**

> **Q1 (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Fuzzy Logic Controller. Describe the role of each block.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

A FLC has four functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram).

1. **Fuzzification Interface (2 marks):** Accepts crisp numerical values from sensors (e.g., $e = 2.5$ °C). For each input, computes the degree of membership in every defined fuzzy set. For instance, with triangular MFs, $\mu_{Z}(2.5) = (5 - 2.5)/5 = 0.5$ and $\mu_{P}(2.5) = 2.5/10 = 0.25$. Output: fuzzified values that form the antecedents of rules.

2. **Knowledge Base (2 marks):** Consists of:
   - *Data Base* — stores all membership function definitions (triangular, trapezoidal, Gaussian), scaling factors $G_e, G_{de}, G_u$, and the universes of discourse.
   - *Rule Base* — stores expert IF–THEN rules, e.g., "IF error is Positive AND change-in-error is Positive THEN heater is High."

3. **Inference Engine (2 marks):** Performs three sub-operations:
   - *Matching* — compute firing strength $w_k = \min[\mu_{A_1}(x_1), \mu_{A_2}(x_2)]$ for each rule.
   - *Implication* — clip the consequent fuzzy set at $w_k$ (Mamdani min).
   - *Aggregation* — combine all clipped consequents using max.

4. **Defuzzification Interface (1 mark):** Converts the aggregated fuzzy output into a single crisp value $y^*$. The most common method is the centroid:
   $y^* = \dfrac{\int y \cdot \mu_B(y) \, dy}{\int \mu_B(y) \, dy}$.

> **Examiner's Tip:** Drawing the block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** out of 7 — *never skip it*.

---

> **Q1 (b) Consider a two-input, one-output FLC for an air conditioner. Inputs are Temperature Error (e) and Humidity Error (he), each with three fuzzy sets {Low, Medium, High}. Output is Cooler Power with sets {Off, Low, High}. Design the 9-rule base and explain how the centroid defuzzification is applied on a specific test input.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Rule Base Design (3 marks):** A typical 9-rule table is:

| $e \backslash he$ | **Low** | **Medium** | **High** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low** | Off | Low | Low |
| **Medium** | Low | Low | High |
| **High** | Low | High | High |

Each cell encodes the rule. Example: "IF error is High AND humidity error is Medium THEN power is High."

**(ii) Test Case Firing (2 marks):** Suppose $e$ belongs *mostly to Medium* ($\mu_M = 0.7$, $\mu_H = 0.3$) and $he$ belongs *mostly to Medium* ($\mu_M = 0.8$, $\mu_L = 0.2$). The three active rules are:
- $R_1$: Medium AND Low → Low, $w_1 = \min[0.7, 0.2] = 0.2$
- $R_2$: Medium AND Medium → Low, $w_2 = \min[0.7, 0.8] = 0.7$
- $R_3$: High AND Medium → High, $w_3 = \min[0.3, 0.8] = 0.3$

**(iii) Centroid Defuzzification (2 marks):** Clip "Low" at 0.7, "High" at 0.3, aggregate by max, and compute:

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{\int y \cdot \mu_{B}(y) \, dy}{\int \mu_{B}(y) \, dy}
$$

Numerically, if the Low triangle is centered around 30% and High around 80%, with areas weighted by 0.7 and 0.3:

$$
y^{*} = \dfrac{0.7 \times 30 \times A_{L} + 0.3 \times 80 \times A_{H}}{0.7 \times A_{L} + 0.3 \times A_{H}} \approx 45 \%
$$

The compressor runs at ~**45 % power**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — Full 14-Mark Alternative (Choose Either)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **Q2 (a) Explain the five different methods of defuzzification with suitable mathematical expressions. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

1. **Centroid / Center of Gravity (COG):** $y^* = \dfrac{\int y \cdot \mu_B(y) \, dy}{\int \mu_B(y) \, dy}$
   - *Advantage:* Smooth, considers entire shape of the output set.
   - *Disadvantage:* Computationally expensive (integration).

2. **Bisector of Area (BOA):** $y^*$ such that $\int_{a}^{y^*} \mu_B(y) dy = \int_{y^*}^{b} \mu_B(y) dy$
   - *Advantage:* Geometrical, divides area into two equal halves.
   - *Disadvantage:* Hard to compute for non-convex sets.

3. **Mean of Maximum (MOM):** $y^* = \dfrac{1}{\vert M \vert} \sum_{y \in M} y$ where $M = \{y \mid \mu_B(y) = \mu_{\max}\}$
   - *Advantage:* Fast; ignores base shape.
   - *Disadvantage:* Loses information about the distribution.

4. **Smallest of Maximum (SOM):** $y^* = \min M$
   - *Advantage:* Conservative, used in safety-critical systems.
   - *Disadvantage:* Can lead to sluggish response.

5. **Largest of Maximum (LOM):** $y^* = \max M$
   - *Advantage:* Aggressive, faster response.
   - *Disadvantage:* Can overshoot; not suitable for delicate processes.

---

> **Q2 (b) Consider a FLC with one input $x \in [0, 10]$ and one output $y \in [0, 10]$. The fuzzy sets for $x$ are "Small" (triangular: a=0, b=0, c=5), "Medium" (a=2, b=5, c=8), and "Large" (a=5, b=10, c=10). The output $y$ has sets "Low" (a=0, b=0, c=5), "Medium" (a=2, b=5, c=8), and "High" (a=5, b=10, c=10). Rules: (1) IF x is Small THEN y is Low; (2) IF x is Medium THEN y is Medium; (3) IF x is Large THEN y is High. For crisp input $x = 4$, perform the complete FLC process and find the crisp output using the centroid method.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 – Fuzzification of $x = 4$ (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{S}(4) &= \max\left[0, \min\left(\dfrac{4-0}{5}, \dfrac{5-4}{5}\right)\right] = \max[0, \min(0.8, 0.2)] = 0.2 \\
\mu_{M}(4) &= \max\left[0, \min\left(\dfrac{4-2}{3}, \dfrac{8-4}{3}\right)\right] = \max[0, \min(0.667, 1.333)] = 0.667 \\
\mu_{L}(4) &= \max\left[0, \min\left(\dfrac{4-5}{5}, 1\right)\right] = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Firing Strengths (1 mark):** With singleton output MFs we can directly use the firing strengths as output values: $w_1 = 0.2$ (Small→Low), $w_2 = 0.667$ (Medium→Medium), $w_3 = 0$ (Large→High). The consequent values are the centers: $b_{Low}=0$, $b_{Med}=5$, $b_{High}=10$.

**Step 3 – Defuzzification using Sugeno-style weighted average (2 marks):** Or, using the **Mamdani** centroid of the clipped triangles:

After clipping, "Low" triangle (height now 0.2) has area $0.2 \times 5 / 2 = 0.5$, and "Medium" triangle (height 0.667) has area $0.667 \times 6 / 2 = 2.0$.

Crisp output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y^{*} &= \dfrac{0.2 \times (0 \cdot 5/3) + 0.667 \times 5 + 0 \times 10}{0.5 + 2.0 + 0} \quad \text{(weighted centers)} \\
&\approx \dfrac{0.2 \times 1.667 + 0.667 \times 5}{2.5} \\
&\approx \dfrac{0.333 + 3.333}{2.5} = \dfrac{3.666}{2.5} \approx 1.47
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to clip** the consequent set at the firing strength — marks deducted in Step 4.
> 2. **Inverted min/max logic:** students write MAX for AND and MIN for OR. AND = MIN, OR = MAX. **Mnemonic:** *A*ND = *A*ll conditions must hold → take the *smallest* = MIN.
> 3. **Discrete centroid formula** is preferred over continuous integration for KTU numerical problems — show the summation table explicitly.
> 4. **Unit consistency:** Error UOD is in °C, not normalized; scale factors $G_e$ may be required to map it to $[-1, +1]$. Forgetting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Rule base completeness:** When drawing the 3×3 table, mark $R_4$ (Z AND N) and other "deemed dead" rules as having zero firing — do not skip them.
> 6. **Centroid denominator ≠ 0:** If no rule fires, the controller must default to a safe value. Mentioning this shows maturity.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ FLC = **Fuzzification + Knowledge Base + Inference + Defuzzification**.
- ✅ It is a **model-free controller** — it does *not* require a transfer function of the plant.
- ✅ Invented by **Lotfi A. Zadeh (1965)** at UC Berkeley.
- ✅ Uses **linguistic variables** (Cold, Warm, Hot) instead of crisp numbers.
- ✅ Membership grades $\mu \in [0, 1]$ — *0 = false, 1 = true, anything in between is "partially true"*.
- ✅ Two most common fuzzy inference systems: **Mamdani (1975)** and **Takagi–Sugeno (1985)**.
- ✅ Rule base of size $m \times n$ for $m$ sets on input 1 and $n$ sets on input 2.
- ✅ **AND** operator = MIN, **OR** operator = MAX, **NOT** operator = $1 - \mu$.
- ✅ **Mamdani implication** = MIN (clips the consequent). **Mamdani aggregation** = MAX.
- ✅ **Five defuzzification methods:** Centroid (most popular), Bisector, MOM, SOM, LOM.
- ✅ **Centroid** is the default — it produces the smoothest, most physically meaningful output.
- ✅ **Scaling factors** $G_e, G_{de}, G_u$ map physical UODs to a normalized internal UOD.
- ✅ Triangular and trapezoidal MFs are most common in KTU exam problems.
- ✅ Real-world uses: **AC, washing machines, ABS, Sendai subway, rice cookers, cameras**.
- ✅ FLC is *non-linear* by construction — it can handle systems where PID tuning fails.
- ✅ For a 14-mark problem, always draw the **block diagram** (≥ 2 marks) and show the **discrete centroid table** for numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Genetic Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Genetic Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the principles of **natural selection** and **genetics** (Darwinian evolution + Mendelian inheritance). It belongs to the broader class of **Evolutionary Algorithms (EAs)** and is used to find approximate (or exact) solutions to search, optimization, and machine learning problems by evolving a population of candidate solutions over successive generations.

A GA operates on a population of **chromosomes** (encoded candidate solutions), applies stochastic operators — **Selection, Crossover, and Mutation** — and uses a **fitness function** to drive the search toward optimal or near-optimal regions of the solution space.

**Historical Context (KTU Board Favorite):**
- Conceptualized by **John Henry Holland** in the 1960s–1970s (University of Michigan).
- Formalized in his 1975 book *Adaptation in Natural and Artificial Systems*.
- Popularized by **David E. Goldberg** (1989) for real-world engineering problems (e.g., gas pipeline control).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Survival of the Fittest Animals"**

Imagine a forest with **100 rabbits** of varying speeds. Foxes (predators) catch the **slowest** rabbits first. The **fastest** rabbits survive and reproduce, passing their "speed genes" to offspring. Over many generations, the entire rabbit population becomes faster — this is **natural selection**.

A **Genetic Algorithm** mimics exactly this:
- **Rabbits** → Candidate solutions (chromosomes).
- **Speed of rabbit** → Fitness of the solution.
- **Surviving rabbits** → Selected parents for the next generation.
- **Offspring inheriting genes** → Crossover (recombination of two parents).
- **Random gene mutation** → Mutation (small random changes).
- **Many generations** → Iterative optimization.

**The result:** The population "evolves" toward the **best (optimal) solution** automatically, without the programmer needing to know *how* to find the solution — only *what* counts as a good solution (the fitness function).

---

### 1.3 Why Genetic Algorithms? — Motivation

Traditional optimization methods (calculus-based, exhaustive search) fail when:
- The search space is **very large** (combinatorial explosion).
- The objective function is **non-differentiable**, **discontinuous**, or **noisy**.
- The problem has **multiple local optima** (multimodal).
- The problem is a **black-box** (only inputs/outputs are known).

> [!TIP]
> GAs are **gradient-free, population-based, and stochastic** — making them ideal for **NP-hard, non-convex, and real-world engineering optimization** problems.

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology Mapping Table

| Biological Term | GA Equivalent | Description |
|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Individual / Organism | **Chromosome / String** | One candidate solution |
| Population | **Population / Generation** | Set of candidate solutions |
| Gene | **Gene / Locus** | Single parameter / variable |
| Allele | **Allele** | Value of a gene (e.g., 0 or 1) |
| Fitness | **Fitness Function / Objective** | Measure of solution quality |
| Natural Selection | **Selection Operator** | Choosing parents based on fitness |
| Mating / Reproduction | **Crossover Operator** | Combining two parents to form offspring |
| Mutation | **Mutation Operator** | Random small changes in genes |
| Generations | **Iterations** | Evolutionary cycles |

---

### 1.5 Standard Constants & Parameters

The following are **standard GA parameters** (commonly tuned defaults):

- **Population Size (N):** **50 – 200** (typical default = **50**).
- **Crossover Probability (Pc):** **0.6 – 0.9** (typical default = **0.7**).
- **Mutation Probability (Pm):** **0.001 – 0.1** (typical default = **0.01**).
- **Number of Generations (T):** **100 – 1000** (problem-dependent).
- **Encoding Length (L):** Depends on precision and variable range.

---

### 1.6 Applications of GA in Engineering

- **Routing & Scheduling** (TSP, job-shop scheduling).
- **Neural Network training** (weight optimization).
- **VLSI design & circuit layout**.
- **Robotics** (path planning, gait optimization).
- **Bioinformatics** (DNA sequence alignment, protein folding).
- **Control Systems** (PID tuning, fuzzy controller design).
- **Financial Modeling** (portfolio optimization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of a Generic GA (Goldberg's Simple GA)

A canonical GA proceeds in **five well-defined stages** per generation. Understanding these stages is **mandatory** for KTU exams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 1 — Initialization**
> Randomly generate an initial population of **N** chromosomes (candidate solutions). Each chromosome is a string/vector representation of the decision variables.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 2 — Fitness Evaluation**
> Decode each chromosome into the problem's solution space, then evaluate the **fitness function f(x)** for every individual. Fitness is the *driving force* of selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 3 — Selection (Reproduction)**
> Select parent chromosomes for mating with probability **proportional to fitness**. Higher fitness = higher chance of being chosen. Common methods:
> - **Roulette Wheel Selection**
> - **Rank Selection**
> - **Tournament Selection**
> - **Elitism** (preserve best individual)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 4 — Crossover (Recombination)**
> With probability **Pc**, two parents are combined to produce **two offspring**. Common types:
> - **Single-point Crossover**
> - **Two-point / Multi-point Crossover**
> - **Uniform Crossover**
> - **Arithmetic Crossover** (for real-valued GAs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Stage 5 — Mutation**
> With probability **Pm**, randomly alter one or more gene values in the offspring. Prevents **premature convergence** and maintains **genetic diversity**.

The cycle (Stages 2–5) repeats until a **termination condition** is met:
- Maximum generations reached.
- Satisfactory fitness level achieved.
- No improvement over consecutive generations (convergence).

---

### 2.2 Chromosome Encoding Schemes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encoding** translates a candidate solution into a chromosome that the GA can manipulate. The choice of encoding directly impacts GA performance.

| Encoding Type | Chromosome Form | Example Problem | Pros / Cons |
|---------------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Binary Encoding** | String of 0s and 1s | Discrete optimization | Simple; classic; but limited precision |
| **Real-Valued (Floating-Point)** | Vector of real numbers | Continuous optimization | High precision; natural for engineering |
| **Integer / Permutation Encoding** | Ordered list of integers | TSP, scheduling | Respects problem constraints |
| **Tree Encoding (GP)** | LISP-like expression trees | Symbolic regression | Used in Genetic Programming |

**Binary Encoding Example:**
To encode a variable $x \in [a, b]$ with precision $p$ decimal places, the required number of bits is:

$$
L = \lceil \log_2((b - a) \cdot 10^p + 1) \rceil
$$

Decoding formula (binary string → real value):

$$
x = a + \text{decimal}(bin) \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1}
$$

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Fitness Proportion (Roulette) | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Probability of selecting individual $i$ |
| 2 | Expected Count | $E_i = N \cdot \dfrac{f_i}{\bar{f}}$ | Expected number of copies of $i$ in next gen |
| 3 | Mean Fitness | $\bar{f} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f_i$ | Average fitness of population |
| 4 | Max Fitness | $f_{\max} = \max(f_i)$ | Best individual fitness |
| 5 | Mutation Rate (per bit) | $P_m \in [0.001, 0.1]$ | Probability of flipping a bit |
| 6 | Crossover Rate | $P_c \in [0.6, 0.9]$ | Probability of crossover |
| 7 | Schema Order | $o(H)$ | Number of fixed (non-`*`) positions in schema $H$ |
| 8 | Schema Defining Length | $\delta(H)$ | Distance between first and last fixed positions |
| 9 | Schema Theorem (Holland) | $E[m(H, t+1)] \geq m(H,t) \cdot \dfrac{f(H)}{\bar{f}} \cdot \left[1 - P_c \cdot \dfrac{\delta(H)}{L-1} - P_m \cdot o(H)\right]$ | Growth equation of short, low-order, high-fitness schemata |
| 10 | Population Diversity | $\text{var}(f) = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (f_i - \bar{f})^2$ | Measures convergence |

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER** use the pipe symbol `|` inside any table cell. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in LaTeX to prevent markdown table breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PID Controller Tuning:** GA searches optimal $(K_p, K_i, K_d)$ for minimum error.
- **Antenna Design:** GA optimizes geometry for maximum gain / minimum SLL.
- **Job-Shop Scheduling:** Minimizing makespan in manufacturing.
- **Power System Optimization:** Economic dispatch, unit commitment.
- **Machine Learning:** Feature selection, hyperparameter tuning of ANNs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Step-by-Step Execution of a Simple GA

Below is the **canonical pseudo-code** of a simple Genetic Algorithm (Goldberg, 1989), expanded with full explanatory annotations as expected in KTU answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm: Simple Genetic Algorithm**
> **Input:** Population size $N$, crossover probability $P_c$, mutation probability $P_m$, maximum generations $T$.
> **Output:** Best (or near-best) solution found.

---

**Step 1 — Initialize Population**

Generate $N$ chromosomes randomly. If binary encoding with length $L$ is used:

$$
x_i^{(0)} = (b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_L), \quad b_j \in \{0, 1\}
$$

for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$ and $j = 1, 2, \ldots, L$. Each bit is independently sampled from a **uniform distribution** over $\{0, 1\}$.

---

**Step 2 — Evaluate Fitness**

For each chromosome $x_i$ in the current generation $t$, compute:

$$
\text{fit}(x_i^{(t)}) = f(\text{decode}(x_i^{(t)}))
$$

where $\text{decode}(\cdot)$ converts the binary string into the real-valued decision vector, and $f(\cdot)$ is the user-defined objective function.

---

**Step 3 — Compute Selection Probabilities (Roulette Wheel)**

For each individual $i$:

$$
P_i = \frac{\text{fit}(x_i^{(t)})}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \text{fit}(x_j^{(t)})}
$$

The expected number of copies of individual $i$ in the next generation's mating pool is:

$$
E_i = N \cdot P_i = N \cdot \frac{\text{fit}(x_i^{(t)})}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \text{fit}(x_j^{(t)})} = \frac{\text{fit}(x_i^{(t)})}{\bar{f}}
$$

where $\bar{f}$ is the mean fitness of the population.

---

**Step 4 — Selection via Roulette Wheel**

Spin a "roulette wheel" $N$ times. Each individual $i$ occupies a sector of angle:

$$
\theta_i = 2\pi \cdot P_i
$$

A random number $r \in [0, 1]$ is drawn; cumulative probabilities are computed:

$$
C_k = \sum_{j=1}^{k} P_j
$$

The selected individual is the smallest $k$ such that $C_k \geq r$.

---

**Step 5 — Crossover (Single-Point)**

For each pair of selected parents, draw $r \in [0, 1]$:

- If $r \leq P_c$: perform crossover.
- Else: copy parents directly to offspring pool.

A crossover point $k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$ is chosen randomly. Given parents:

$$
P_1 = (b_1^{(1)}, b_2^{(1)}, \ldots, b_L^{(1)})
$$

$$
P_2 = (b_1^{(2)}, b_2^{(2)}, \ldots, b_L^{(2)})
$$

Two offspring are produced:

$$
O_1 = (b_1^{(1)}, b_2^{(1)}, \ldots, b_k^{(1)}, b_{k+1}^{(2)}, \ldots, b_L^{(2)})
$$

$$
O_2 = (b_1^{(2)}, b_2^{(2)}, \ldots, b_k^{(2)}, b_{k+1}^{(1)}, \ldots, b_L^{(1)})
$$

---

**Step 6 — Mutation (Bit-Flip)**

For each gene (bit) in each offspring, draw $r \in [0, 1]$:

- If $r \leq P_m$: flip the bit (i.e., $0 \to 1$ or $1 \to 0$).
- Else: keep unchanged.

For a binary chromosome, the expected number of mutated bits is $L \cdot P_m$.

---

**Step 7 — Replacement & Termination**

Replace the old population with the new offspring population. Increment generation counter $t = t + 1$. If $t < T$ **and** no convergence, go to **Step 2**. Else, terminate and return the best chromosome found.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — Fitness Proportion Calculation**

Consider a population of **N = 4** chromosomes with fitness values:

$$
f_1 = 10, \quad f_2 = 25, \quad f_3 = 15, \quad f_4 = 50
$$

**Step 1 — Total Fitness:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{4} f_i = 10 + 25 + 15 + 50 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Mean Fitness:**

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{100}{4} = 25
$$

**Step 3 — Selection Probabilities (Roulette):**

$$
P_1 = \frac{10}{100} = 0.10
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{50}{100} = 0.50
$$

**Step 4 — Expected Number of Copies (N = 4):**

$$
E_1 = 4 \cdot 0.10 = 0.4
$$

$$
E_2 = 4 \cdot 0.25 = 1.0
$$

$$
E_3 = 4 \cdot 0.15 = 0.6
$$

$$
E_4 = 4 \cdot 0.50 = 2.0
$$

**Interpretation:** Individual 4 (highest fitness) contributes **2 copies** on average; individual 1 contributes **0.4** copies. The next generation will be biased toward the fitter individuals — this is the engine of evolution.

---

### 3.3 Analytic Operator Impact Matrix

| Operator | Effect on Population | Risk if Misused | KTU Tip |
|----------|---------------------|-----------------|---------|
| **Selection** (high pressure) | Speeds up convergence | Premature convergence to local optima | Use **Tournament selection** with $k=2,3$ for balanced pressure |
| **Crossover** (high Pc) | Exploits good building blocks | Loss of diversity if $P_c = 1.0$ | Use **Pc ≈ 0.7–0.9** |
| **Mutation** (high Pm) | Maintains diversity, explores new regions | Disrupts good schemata if too high | Use **Pm ≈ 0.001–0.05** for binary GAs |
| **Elitism** | Preserves best individual(s) | None (always beneficial) | Copy top **1–2%** directly to next gen |
| **Large N** | Better exploration, smoother search | High computational cost | Use **N = 50–200** |

---

### 3.4 Advantages and Limitations of GA

> [!TIP]
> **Advantages:**
> - Gradient-free — works on non-differentiable, discontinuous functions.
> - Global search — uses population to escape local optima.
> - Parallelizable — fitness evaluations are independent.
> - Flexible — works with any problem that has a fitness function.
> - Robust — performs well in noisy, dynamic, multimodal environments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitations:**
> - Computationally expensive for very large populations / long chromosomes.
> - No guarantee of global optimum (no free lunch theorem).
> - Parameter tuning (N, Pc, Pm) is non-trivial.
> - May converge prematurely to local optima.
> - Cannot directly handle constraints (needs penalty methods).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Flowchart of a Genetic Algorithm

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **complete iterative flow** of a canonical GA, from initialization to termination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START GA]) --> initA[Initialize Population]
    initA --> evalA[Evaluate Fitness of All Individuals]
    evalA --> condA{Convergence or Max Gen Reached}
    condA -- No --> selA[Selection Operator]
    selA --> crossA[Crossover Operator with Probability Pc]
    crossA --> mutA[Mutation Operator with Probability Pm]
    mutA --> formA[Form New Offspring Population]
    formA --> evalA
    condA -- Yes --> outBest[Output Best Chromosome]
    outBest --> stopA([END GA])

    style startA fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b3d6b
    style stopA fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b3d6b
    style condA fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#a8500a
    style selA fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#155715
    style crossA fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#4f2c7a
    style mutA fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff,stroke:#7a1414
    style evalA fill:#17becf,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0a6b78
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GA System

This architecture diagram maps the **interactions among the core modules** of a GA engine (useful for KTU long-answer / 14-mark questions where block diagrams are required).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    popMod[Population Module] --> encMod[Encoding Module]
    encMod --> fitMod[Fitness Evaluation Module]
    fitMod --> selMod[Selection Module]
    selMod --> crossMod[Crossover Module]
    crossMod --> mutMod[Mutation Module]
    mutMod --> repMod[Replacement Module]
    repMod --> termMod[Termination Module]
    termMod --> outMod[Output Best Solution]
    termMod -- Not Terminated --> popMod
    paramMod[GA Parameters: N, Pc, Pm, T] --> popMod
    paramMod --> selMod
    paramMod --> crossMod
    paramMod --> mutMod

    style popMod fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style encMod fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style fitMod fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style selMod fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style crossMod fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
    style mutMod fill:#e377c2,color:#ffffff
    style repMod fill:#7f7f7f,color:#ffffff
    style termMod fill:#bcbd22,color:#000000
    style outMod fill:#17becf,color:#ffffff
    style paramMod fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
```

---

### 4.3 Single-Point Crossover Schematic

Visual representation of how a single-point crossover recombines two parent chromosomes into two offspring at crossover point $k$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Parent 1: 1 1 0 1 0 0 1 1"] --> cut1
    P2["Parent 2: 0 0 1 0 1 1 0 0"] --> cut2
    cut1["Crossover Point k = 4"] --> O1
    cut2["Crossover Point k = 4"] --> O2
    P1 -- Genes 1 to 4 --> O1
    P2 -- Genes 5 to 8 --> O1
    P2 -- Genes 1 to 4 --> O2
    P1 -- Genes 5 to 8 --> O2
    O1["Offspring 1: 1 1 0 1 1 1 0 0"]
    O2["Offspring 2: 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 1"]
```

---

### 4.4 GA Operators — Block Summary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    opsA[GA Operators] --> opsB[Selection]
    opsA --> opsC[Crossover]
    opsA --> opsD[Mutation]

    opsB --> opsB1[Roulette Wheel]
    opsB --> opsB2[Tournament Selection]
    opsB --> opsB3[Rank Based Selection]

    opsC --> opsC1[Single Point]
    opsC --> opsC2[Two Point]
    opsC --> opsC3[Uniform Crossover]

    opsD --> opsD1[Bit Flip Mutation]
    opsD --> opsD2[Swap Mutation]
    opsD --> opsD3[Gaussian Mutation]

    style opsA fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Genetic Algorithm. List any four applications of GA in engineering.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a population-based metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by the **principles of natural selection and genetics** (Holland, 1975). It evolves a population of candidate solutions using **selection, crossover, and mutation** operators guided by a **fitness function** to find optimal/near-optimal solutions.

**Applications (any four, 0.5 marks each):**
1. **PID controller tuning** in control systems.
2. **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** and routing optimization.
3. **Neural network weight training** and architecture optimization.
4. **VLSI circuit design and layout**.
5. **Job-shop scheduling** in manufacturing.
6. **Feature selection** in machine learning.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]**
**Explain the role of the fitness function in a Genetic Algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **fitness function** $f(x)$ is the **objective function** that quantitatively measures the **quality or goodness** of a candidate solution (chromosome) in the problem domain. It serves three critical roles:
>
> 1. **Evaluation** — assigns each individual a scalar score representing its merit.
> 2. **Selection Driver** — used by the selection operator to probabilistically favor high-fitness individuals for reproduction.
> 3. **Convergence Indicator** — tracking fitness across generations shows whether the GA is progressing toward the optimum.
>
> It is the **only link** between the GA and the actual problem — the GA itself has no knowledge of the problem domain.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examination (ESE) follows a **Module-Level Internal Choice**. Students answer **ONE full question (a) + (b)** from the chosen module. Below are **Question A** and **Question B** for this topic.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive GA Theory + Operators*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, ESE Pattern]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete cycle of a Genetic Algorithm**. Discuss each phase in detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A **Genetic Algorithm** iteratively evolves a population of candidate solutions through five main phases:

**1. Initialization (1 mark):**
A population of $N$ chromosomes is generated randomly. Each chromosome encodes a candidate solution using **binary, real-valued, or permutation encoding**.

**2. Fitness Evaluation (1 mark):**
Each chromosome $x_i$ is decoded and evaluated using the **fitness function** $f(x_i)$. Fitness is the scalar measure of how "good" the solution is for the given problem.

**3. Selection (1.5 marks):**
Fitter individuals are probabilistically selected as parents. Common methods:
- **Roulette Wheel:** $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.
- **Tournament:** Randomly pick $k$ individuals, select the fittest.
- **Rank-Based:** Rank-based probability instead of raw fitness.

**4. Crossover (1.5 marks):**
With probability $P_c$, pairs of parents exchange genetic material to form offspring.
- **Single-Point Crossover:** Split at point $k$, swap tails.
- **Two-Point / Uniform Crossover:** Swap at two or more points.

**5. Mutation (1 mark):**
With probability $P_m$, each gene is randomly altered (e.g., bit flip in binary GA). Mutation prevents **premature convergence** and maintains **diversity**.

**6. Termination (1 mark):**
The loop repeats for $T$ generations or until **convergence** (no fitness improvement). The best chromosome is returned as the solution.

*(A clear block diagram showing: Init → Fitness → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → New Population → Loop-back should be drawn. Full marks require the diagram + written explanation.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the following selection mechanisms in GA with suitable formulas:
**(i) Roulette Wheel Selection**
**(ii) Tournament Selection**
**(iii) Elitism**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Roulette Wheel Selection (2.5 marks):**
Each individual $i$ is assigned a sector on a "roulette wheel" proportional to its fitness. The probability of selection is:

$$
P_i = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}
$$

The wheel is "spun" $N$ times to produce the mating pool. Higher fitness → larger sector → higher chance of selection. **Drawback:** Sensitive to fitness scaling; super-individuals can dominate.

**(ii) Tournament Selection (2.5 marks):**
- Randomly select $k$ individuals (tournament size, typically $k = 2$ or $3$).
- Choose the one with **highest fitness** as the winner.
- Repeat $N$ times to fill mating pool.

**Advantages:** No fitness scaling required; easy to implement; tunable selection pressure via $k$.

**(iii) Elitism (2 marks):**
The **top $e$ individuals** (e.g., top 1–2%) are copied **unchanged** to the next generation. This guarantees the **best solution found so far is never lost** due to stochastic operators. It is **almost always beneficial** and is standard in modern GAs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **omit the formulas** for $P_i$ in Roulette Wheel — this is a guaranteed **1–2 mark loss**.
> - Tournament selection requires you to **define the tournament size $k$** explicitly.
> - Elitism must be described as **preserving the best, not just "selecting the best"** — copying unchanged is the key phrase.
> - For 7-mark sub-parts, KTU expects **at least 2–3 lines of explanation per method**; one-liners lose marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Encoding, Operators, and Worked Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, ESE Pattern]**

**(a) (i)** Compare **Binary, Real-Valued, and Permutation encoding** in Genetic Algorithms. Give one example problem suited to each. **(4 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Encoding | Chromosome Form | Best Suited For | Example Problem |
|----------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Binary** | String of 0/1 bits | Discrete, low-dimensional search | Knapsack Problem, Feature Selection |
| **Real-Valued** | Vector of floating-point numbers | Continuous, high-dimensional search | PID Tuning, Function Optimization |
| **Permutation** | Ordered list of unique integers | Sequencing / ordering problems | Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), Job-Shop Scheduling |

**Trade-off:** Binary encoding is simple but suffers from **Hamming cliff** issues; real-valued encoding gives **higher precision** and **faster convergence**; permutation encoding requires **custom operators** (e.g., OX, PMX crossover) to maintain validity.

---

**(a) (ii)** A chromosome of length $L = 8$ bits encodes a variable $x$ in the range $[-2, 2]$. Decode the binary string `01101100` and find the corresponding real value. Take precision $p = 3$ decimal places. **(3 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Binary to Decimal Conversion:**

The binary string `01101100` equals (MSB to LSB):

$$
\text{decimal} = 0 \cdot 2^7 + 1 \cdot 2^6 + 1 \cdot 2^5 + 0 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 1 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 0 \cdot 2^0
$$

$$
= 0 + 64 + 32 + 0 + 8 + 4 + 0 + 0 = 108
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Decoding Formula:**

$$
x = a + \text{decimal}(bin) \cdot \frac{b - a}{2^L - 1}
$$

$$
x = -2 + 108 \cdot \frac{2 - (-2)}{2^8 - 1}
$$

$$
x = -2 + 108 \cdot \frac{4}{255}
$$

$$
x = -2 + 108 \cdot 0.015686
$$

$$
x = -2 + 1.6941
$$

$$
\boxed{x \approx -0.3059}
$$

---

**(b)** For a population of **N = 5** chromosomes with fitness values $f = \{20, 40, 10, 80, 50\}$:
**(i) Compute the selection probability for each individual using Roulette Wheel.**
**(ii) Find the expected number of copies of each individual in the next generation.**
**(iii) Discuss why elitism should be incorporated in this population.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Selection Probabilities (3 marks):**

Total fitness:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{5} f_i = 20 + 40 + 10 + 80 + 50 = 200
$$

Mean fitness:

$$
\bar{f} = \frac{200}{5} = 40
$$

Probabilities:

$$
P_1 = \frac{20}{200} = 0.10
$$

$$
P_2 = \frac{40}{200} = 0.20
$$

$$
P_3 = \frac{10}{200} = 0.05
$$

$$
P_4 = \frac{80}{200} = 0.40
$$

$$
P_5 = \frac{50}{200} = 0.25
$$

**Verification:** $\sum P_i = 0.10 + 0.20 + 0.05 + 0.40 + 0.25 = 1.00$ ✓

**(ii) Expected Copies (2 marks):**

Using $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ with $N = 5$:

$$
E_1 = 5 \cdot 0.10 = 0.5
$$

$$
E_2 = 5 \cdot 0.20 = 1.0
$$

$$
E_3 = 5 \cdot 0.05 = 0.25
$$

$$
E_4 = 5 \cdot 0.40 = 2.0
$$

$$
E_5 = 5 \cdot 0.25 = 1.25
$$

**Total expected copies:** $0.5 + 1.0 + 0.25 + 2.0 + 1.25 = 5.0 = N$ ✓

**Interpretation:** Individual 4 (fittest, $f_4 = 80$) is expected to produce 2 offspring; Individual 3 (least fit, $f_3 = 10$) is expected to produce only 0.25 — strong selection pressure.

**(iii) Role of Elitism (2 marks):**

> Since **Individual 3 has the lowest fitness** ($f_3 = 10$) and **Individual 4 is the fittest** ($f_4 = 80$), the roulette wheel will heavily favor 4 while 3 may even disappear from the gene pool. **Elitism** — copying the best individual(s) (here, individual 4) unchanged into the next generation — ensures the **best solution found so far is never lost** due to stochastic operators like crossover or mutation. It guarantees **monotonic improvement** of the best fitness across generations and is **standard practice** in modern GA implementations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sums not verified** — Always verify $\sum P_i = 1$ and $\sum E_i = N$; missing this loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong decoding formula** — Students sometimes invert the range or forget the $(b-a)$ factor.
> 3. **Confusing fitness vs. objective** — In minimization problems, GA uses a *transformed* fitness (e.g., $1/(1+f)$ or $\text{max} - f$); clarify this if asked.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — For 7-mark theory questions, **always include a labeled flowchart**. Omission = 2–3 mark loss.
> 5. **Skipping the termination condition** — Many students forget to mention convergence/max-generation stopping criteria.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Introduction to Genetic Algorithm**

- [ ] **GA = Metaheuristic, population-based, gradient-free, stochastic** optimization technique.
- [ ] **Founder:** John Holland (1975); popularizer: David Goldberg (1989).
- [ ] **Five Stages per generation:** Init → Fitness → Selection → Crossover → Mutation → Replace.
- [ ] **Encoding types:** Binary, Real-Valued, Integer, Permutation, Tree (GP).
- [ ] **Roulette Wheel Probability:** $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$.
- [ ] **Expected Copies:** $E_i = N \cdot P_i = f_i / \bar{f}$.
- [ ] **Tournament Selection:** Pick $k$, choose fittest; tunable pressure.
- [ ] **Elitism:** Copy best $e$ individuals unchanged — guarantees monotonic improvement.
- [ ] **Crossover types:** Single-point, Two-point, Uniform, Arithmetic.
- [ ] **Mutation types:** Bit-flip (binary), Gaussian (real), Swap (permutation).
- [ ] **Typical Parameters:** $N = 50$–$200$, $P_c = 0.7$, $P_m = 0.01$.
- [ ] **Decoding formula (binary → real):** $x = a + \text{dec}(bin) \cdot (b - a)/(2^L - 1)$.
- [ ] **Schema Theorem (Holland):** Short, low-order, high-fitness schemata grow exponentially.
- [ ] **Termination criteria:** Max generations, fitness threshold, no improvement in $k$ generations.
- [ ] **GAs are best for:** NP-hard, multimodal, non-differentiable, black-box problems.
- [ ] **Always include a labeled flowchart** in 7+ mark theory answers.
- [ ] **Verify sums:** $\sum P_i = 1$, $\sum E_i = N$ — easy marks if shown.
- [ ] **Don't confuse fitness with objective** — for minimization, transform fitness.
- [ ] **Key advantage:** Global search via population; **key limitation:** no guarantee of global optimum.
- [ ] **Must-know historical tags:** Holland 1975, Goldberg 1989 — frequently asked in 3-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operators in Genetic Algorithm - Coding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operators in Genetic Algorithm - Coding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Genetic Algorithm (GA) Operators?

**Genetic Algorithm (GA)** is a stochastic, population-based metaheuristic optimization technique inspired by Charles Darwin's theory of natural evolution. The algorithm mimics the process of biological evolution by iteratively refining a population of candidate solutions using biologically inspired **operators**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *GA operators* are stochastic transformation mechanisms that act upon a coded representation of candidate solutions (called *chromosomes* or *individuals*) within a population to generate new offspring. They form the evolutionary engine that drives the search toward optimality in three principal stages — **Selection (Reproduction), Crossover (Recombination), and Mutation**.

### 1.2 What is "Coding" in GA?

Before any operator can be applied, every candidate solution must be **encoded** into a chromosome-like structure (a string of symbols). This is the **coding (encoding) step** and is *prerequisite* to applying the three GA operators. The choice of coding directly determines the types of crossover and mutation operators that can be used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Encoding (Coding):** The mapping of a problem's decision variables (phenotype) into a chromosome string (genotype) suitable for evolutionary manipulation. It is the foundational representation upon which all GA operators operate.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Survival of the Fittest Code"

Imagine a **royal tournament** where thousands of **knights** (candidate solutions) compete in a **forest arena** (search space). Each knight carries a **scroll of instructions** (the chromosome / code) describing how to find a hidden treasure (the optimal solution).

- The **king's judge** evaluates each knight's fitness by how close they get to the treasure.
- **Selection Operator** = The judge picks the strongest knights to advance to the next round.
- **Crossover Operator** = Two winning knights exchange half their scrolls to produce **offspring knights** with combined strengths.
- **Mutation Operator** = Occasionally, a scribe makes a small random scribble error on a scroll, introducing a **novel strategy** that might just lead to the treasure.
- **Coding** = The *language* in which the scroll is written (binary runes, decimal numbers, real coordinates, etc.).

The better the *coding* scheme, the more efficiently the scrolls can be mixed, mutated, and read by the tournament system.

### 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants and Metrics

- **Population Size (N):** Typically **20 – 200** individuals (KTU standard is often 50 – 100).
- **Crossover Probability (Pc):** Usually **0.6 – 0.9**.
- **Mutation Probability (Pm):** Usually **0.001 – 0.1** (kept low to preserve good genetic material).
- **Chromosome Length (L):** Determined by precision requirements of the variable.
- **Maximum Generations (Gmax):** Termination criterion, commonly **100 – 1000**.

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU 2024, the three operators (Selection, Crossover, Mutation) and the major coding schemes (Binary, Real, Permutation) are examinable weightage-bearing topics in Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Triad of GA Operators — Operational Breakdown

#### A. Selection (Reproduction) Operator
**Why:** To probabilistically favor high-fitness individuals so that their genetic material propagates into the next generation.
**How:** Allocates reproductive slots in proportion to fitness.

| Method | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Roulette Wheel Selection** | Probability proportional to fitness | Simple, classical | Premature convergence on skewed fitness |
| **Tournament Selection** | Pick k individuals, select best | Preserves diversity, parameter k controls pressure | Requires extra random draws |
| **Rank-Based Selection** | Selection based on rank, not raw fitness | Avoids fitness-proportion scaling issues | Slower convergence |
| **Elitism** | Copy top individuals directly | Guarantees monotonic best-fitness | Can trap in local optima |

#### B. Crossover (Recombination) Operator
**Why:** To combine the genetic material of two parents to produce offspring that *may* inherit the best traits of both.
**How:** Exchanges substring segments between parent chromosomes.

| Method | Cut / Rule | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Point Crossover** | One cut point | Binary, integer coding |
| **Two-Point (Multi-Point) Crossover** | Two cut points | Maintains schema better |
| **Uniform Crossover** | Per-gene coin flip | Highly disruptive |
| **Order Crossover (OX)** | Preserves sequence | Permutation (TSP) |
| **Arithmetic Crossover** | Linear combination | Real-coded GA |
| **Blend Crossover (BLX-α)** | Extension interval | Real-coded GA |

#### C. Mutation Operator
**Why:** To maintain **genetic diversity** and prevent premature convergence to local optima.
**How:** Introduces small random perturbations to a chromosome.

| Method | Effect | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit Flip Mutation** | 0 → 1, 1 → 0 | Binary coding |
| **Gaussian Mutation** | Add N(0, σ) | Real-coded GA |
| **Swap Mutation** | Swap two gene positions | Permutation |
| **Inversion Mutation** | Reverse a substring | Permutation |
| **Uniform Mutation** | Replace gene with random value | Real / integer coding |

### 2.2 Coding (Encoding) Schemes — The Foundation

| Coding Type | Chromosome Form | Example (L = 8) | Typical Operators |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary** | String of 0s and 1s | `10110010` | Bit-flip mutation, 1/2-point crossover |
| **Real-Valued (Floating-Point)** | Vector of reals | `[3.14, -2.71, 5.20]` | BLX-α, Gaussian mutation |
| **Integer (Discrete)** | String of integers | `[2, 5, 1, 7, 4, 3, 8, 6]` | Swap, scramble mutation |
| **Permutation** | Each integer appears once | `[3, 1, 4, 2, 7, 5]` | Order crossover, swap, inversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Coding Matters:** A poor coding scheme creates a rugged, deceptive fitness landscape, no matter how sophisticated the operators are. Binary coding provides maximal schema processing, while real-coded GA is preferred for continuous, high-dimensional search.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_i = \dfrac{f_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j}$ | Roulette-wheel probability of individual $i$ | $0 \le P_i \le 1$ |
| 2 | $E_i = N \cdot P_i$ | Expected count of individual $i$ in mating pool | Integer $\ge 0$ |
| 3 | $P_c$ | Crossover probability | $0.6 - 0.9$ |
| 4 | $P_m = \dfrac{1}{L}$ | Per-bit mutation probability (Rechenberg's 1/L rule) | $1/L$ |
| 5 | $L = \lceil \log_2 \left( \dfrac{(b-a)}{10^p} \cdot 2 \right) \rceil$ | Binary string length for variable range $[a, b]$ with $p$ decimals | Integer |
| 6 | $x = a + \dfrac{\text{decoded}}{2^L - 1} \cdot (b - a)$ | Decoded real value from binary chromosome | Continuous |
| 7 | $\Delta k = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mid f_i - \bar{f} \mid}{N \cdot \bar{f}}$ | Population diversity metric | $0$ to $1$ |
| 8 | $F(x) = \dfrac{1}{1 + C(x)}$ | Fitness from cost $C$ (minimization) | $> 0$ |
| 9 | $F(x) = C(x) - C_{\min} + \epsilon$ | Windowing / scaling (Deb's) | $> 0$ |
| 10 | $\text{BLX-}\alpha: x_i^{'} \in [x_{\min} - I\alpha, x_{\max} + I\alpha]$ | Blend crossover spread; $I = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | $\alpha = 0.5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the above table, vertical bars denoting absolute value are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Do not use the bare pipe symbol `|` inside a table row.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Binary Coded GA** → Neural network weight optimization, feature selection, VLSI test pattern generation.
- **Real-Coded GA** → Aerodynamic shape optimization, controller tuning (PID), antenna design, path planning of robots.
- **Permutation GA** → Travelling Salesman Problem, job-shop scheduling, vehicle routing, DNA fragment assembly.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Binary Coding of a Continuous Variable

**Problem:** Encode the variable $x \in [-3.0,\ 7.5]$ with precision $p = 1$ decimal place (i.e., 0.1 resolution) into a binary chromosome.

#### Step 1 — Compute Required Range Span

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Range span} &= b - a \\
&= 7.5 - (-3.0) \\
&= 10.5
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Number of Discrete Intervals

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of intervals} &= \dfrac{b - a}{10^{p}} \\
&= \dfrac{10.5}{10^{-1}} \\
&= 105
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute Minimum Binary String Length

$$
\begin{aligned}
2^L - 1 &\ge 105 \\
2^L &\ge 106 \\
L &\ge \log_2(106) \\
L &\ge 6.727 \\
L &= 7 \text{ bits (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Verification of Achievable Precision

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \dfrac{b - a}{2^L - 1} \\
&= \dfrac{10.5}{2^7 - 1} \\
&= \dfrac{10.5}{127} \\
&\approx 0.0827
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0.0827 < 0.1$, precision requirement is met.

#### Step 5 — Decode a Sample Chromosome

Take chromosome `1001101` (binary = 77 decimal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= a + \dfrac{\text{decimal}}{2^L - 1} \cdot (b - a) \\
x &= -3.0 + \dfrac{77}{127} \cdot 10.5 \\
x &= -3.0 + 0.6063 \cdot 10.5 \\
x &= -3.0 + 6.366 \\
x &= 3.366
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Roulette Wheel Selection Probabilities

**Given Population (N = 5):**

| Individual | Fitness $f_i$ |
|---|---|
| A | 10 |
| B | 25 |
| C | 15 |
| D | 5 |
| E | 45 |

#### Step 1 — Compute Total Fitness

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tot} &= \sum_{i=1}^{5} f_i \\
&= 10 + 25 + 15 + 5 + 45 \\
&= 100
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Selection Probability $P_i = f_i / F_{tot}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= 10 / 100 = 0.10 \\
P_B &= 25 / 100 = 0.25 \\
P_C &= 15 / 100 = 0.15 \\
P_D &= 5 / 100 = 0.05 \\
P_E &= 45 / 100 = 0.45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $\sum P_i = 0.10 + 0.25 + 0.15 + 0.05 + 0.45 = 1.00$ ✓

#### Step 3 — Compute Expected Count $E_i = N \cdot P_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_A &= 5 \times 0.10 = 0.5 \\
E_B &= 5 \times 0.25 = 1.25 \\
E_C &= 5 \times 0.15 = 0.75 \\
E_D &= 5 \times 0.05 = 0.25 \\
E_E &= 5 \times 0.45 = 2.25
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $\sum E_i = 5.0$ ✓

#### Step 4 — Build Cumulative Probability Wheel

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_A &= 0.10 \\
C_B &= 0.10 + 0.25 = 0.35 \\
C_C &= 0.35 + 0.15 = 0.50 \\
C_D &= 0.50 + 0.05 = 0.55 \\
C_E &= 0.55 + 0.45 = 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

Spin a uniform random number $r \in [0, 1)$ — the first $C_i \ge r$ wins.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Single-Point Crossover

**Parents (L = 8):**
- P1 = `10110010`
- P2 = `01001101`
- Crossover point: 4
- $P_c = 0.85$ → crossover occurs.

**Step 1 — Slice parents at point 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P1 &= \underbrace{1011}_{L} \vert \ \underbrace{0010}_{R} \\
P2 &= \underbrace{0100}_{L} \vert \ \underbrace{1101}_{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Exchange right halves:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O1 &= P1[L] \cup P2[R] = 1011 \conc 1101 = \mathbf{10111101} \\
O2 &= P2[L] \cup P1[R] = 0100 \conc 0010 = \mathbf{01000010}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Symbol $\conc$ denotes string concatenation; the vertical separator `\vert` is used outside the markdown table to avoid syntax breakage.)

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Bit-Flip Mutation

**Pre-mutation chromosome:** `10110010` (L = 8)
**Mutation probability per bit:** $P_m = 0.10$

**Step 1 — For each bit, draw $r_i \sim U(0,1)$:**

| Position | Gene | $r_i$ | Mutate? | New Gene |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.06 | No | 1 |
| 2 | 0 | 0.42 | No | 0 |
| 3 | 1 | 0.18 | No | 1 |
| 4 | 1 | 0.03 | No | 1 |
| 5 | 0 | 0.71 | No | 0 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.15 | No | 0 |
| 7 | 1 | 0.09 | No | 1 |
| 8 | 0 | 0.95 | **Yes** | 1 |

**Resulting mutant:** `10110011` — only one bit flipped out of 8.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Crossover

| Condition | Recommended Crossover |
|---|---|
| Discrete, binary, L $\le$ 20 | Single-point |
| Schema preservation critical | Two-point |
| Need aggressive mixing | Uniform |
| Permutation (ordering) problem | Order Crossover (OX) or PMX |
| Continuous, real-valued decision vars | BLX-$\alpha$ or SBX |
| Multi-modal, deceptive landscape | Uniform or Uniform scanning |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block-Level Functional Architecture of a GA Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Optimization Problem] --> A1[Define Objective Function and Constraints]
    A1 --> A2[Choose Coding Scheme Binary Real Permutation]
    A2 --> A3[Initialize Population N Random Chromosomes]
    A3 --> A4[Decode Chromosomes to Phenotypes]
    A4 --> A5[Evaluate Fitness f x for Each Individual]
    A5 --> A6{Stop Criteria Met Max Gen or Convergence}
    A6 -- No --> A7[Selection Operator Pick Parents]
    A7 --> A8[Crossover Operator Pc Produce Offspring]
    A8 --> A9[Mutation Operator Pm Perturb Genes]
    A9 --> A10[Evaluate Offspring Fitness]
    A10 --> A11[Form Next Generation Elitism Optional]
    A11 --> A6
    A6 -- Yes --> A12[Return Best Solution and Chromosome]
    A12 --> A13[Stop]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Single-Point Crossover Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Parent1 Chromosome 10110010] --> CUT1[Cut at Position 4]
    P2[Parent2 Chromosome 01001101] --> CUT2[Cut at Position 4]
    CUT1 --> SW[Swap Right Halves]
    CUT2 --> SW
    SW --> O1[Offspring1 10111101]
    SW --> O2[Offspring2 01000010]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Bit-Flip Mutation Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Input Chromosome 10110010] --> RNG[Generate Uniform Random Vector r1 r2 ... rL]
    RNG --> CMP{For each bit, ri less than Pm?}
    CMP -- Yes --> FLIP[Flip the bit 0 to 1 or 1 to 0]
    CMP -- No --> KEEP[Keep original bit]
    FLIP --> OUT[Output Mutant Chromosome]
    KEEP --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Selection Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Need a Selection Mechanism] --> S1{Fitness values positive?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S2[Fitness proportional methods]
    S1 -- No --> S3[Rank or Tournament methods]
    S2 --> S2A[Roulette Wheel Selection]
    S2 --> S2B[Stochastic Universal Sampling]
    S3 --> S3A[Tournament Selection size k]
    S3 --> S3B[Rank Based Linear or Exponential]
    S3 --> S3C[Truncation Selection]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The above Mermaid block diagrams are designed to avoid reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone node names. All node IDs are alphanumeric with a letter prefix, and labels contain clean uppercase text only — no markdown formatting tags inside the double-quoted labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*List and briefly explain any three commonly used coding schemes in Genetic Algorithm.*

**Model Answer:**

The three principal coding schemes are:

1. **Binary Coding** — Each decision variable is encoded as a string of 0s and 1s. Compact and enables schema processing. Best for discrete and small-precision problems.
2. **Real-Valued (Floating-Point) Coding** — Each gene is a real number from a defined interval. Avoids the Hamming cliff problem of binary coding and is preferred for high-dimensional continuous optimization (e.g., controller tuning).
3. **Permutation Coding** — Each integer from 1 to N appears exactly once. Used for ordering problems like the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and job-shop scheduling, where the relative order of elements matters.

*(3 marks — 1 for naming + 2 for explanations)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is mutation probability kept very low in a Genetic Algorithm? Justify with an example.*

**Model Answer:**

The mutation probability $P_m$ is kept very low (typically 0.001 – 0.1, often $1/L$ where $L$ is chromosome length) for the following reasons:

1. **Conservation of Good Genetic Material:** High $P_m$ would destroy the high-fitness schemata built up by crossover, turning the GA into a random search.
2. **Secondary Search Role:** Mutation is meant to be a *background operator* that injects diversity, not the primary exploration mechanism (which is crossover).
3. **Fine-Tuning:** It fine-tunes local search around promising solutions.

**Example:** For a binary chromosome `10110101` of length 8, if $P_m = 0.125$ (i.e., 1/L), only ~1 bit flips on average. If $P_m$ were 0.5, on average 4 bits would flip, destroying the chromosome's structure.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Coding + Selection (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain **binary coding** in Genetic Algorithm. A variable $x$ lies in the range $[-5.0, \ 10.0]$ and must be represented with a precision of 0.05. Determine the required chromosome length $L$ and decode the chromosome `1010110`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Roulette Wheel Selection** method. For the population of 6 individuals with fitness values $\{2, 8, 12, 3, 10, 5\}$, compute the selection probability and expected count for each. Also draw the cumulative probability wheel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

Binary coding represents each decision variable as a string of 0s and 1s. The interval $[a, b]$ is divided into $2^L - 1$ discrete steps.

**Step 1 — Range:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
b - a &= 10.0 - (-5.0) = 15.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of intervals required:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Intervals} &= \dfrac{15.0}{0.05} = 300
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find minimum L:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
2^L - 1 &\ge 300 \\
2^L &\ge 301 \\
L &\ge \log_2(301) \approx 8.23 \\
L &= 9 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decode `1010110`:**
For L = 9, we extend/pad chromosome. The given 7-bit value = `1010110` (binary) = 86. But L = 9 is required. Assuming the given is the value of length-9 string, the binary `1010110` represents 86.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= a + \dfrac{\text{decoded}}{2^L - 1} \cdot (b - a) \\
x &= -5.0 + \dfrac{86}{2^9 - 1} \cdot 15.0 \\
x &= -5.0 + \dfrac{86}{511} \cdot 15.0 \\
x &= -5.0 + 0.1683 \cdot 15.0 \\
x &= -5.0 + 2.524 \\
x &= -2.476
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram explanation + 3 for L derivation + 2 for decoding)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Roulette Wheel Selection (RWS):** Each individual is assigned a sector on a virtual roulette wheel whose arc length is proportional to its fitness. A random spin selects the individual whose sector the pointer lands on. This implements **fitness-proportionate selection**, giving higher-fitness individuals a proportionally higher chance of being selected as parents.

**Population fitness (N = 6):** $f = \{2, 8, 12, 3, 10, 5\}$

**Step 1 — Total fitness:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{tot} &= 2 + 8 + 12 + 3 + 10 + 5 = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Selection probabilities $P_i = f_i / F_{tot}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 2/40 = 0.05 \\
P_2 &= 8/40 = 0.20 \\
P_3 &= 12/40 = 0.30 \\
P_4 &= 3/40 = 0.075 \\
P_5 &= 10/40 = 0.25 \\
P_6 &= 5/40 = 0.125
\end{aligned}
$$

Check: $\sum P_i = 1.000$ ✓

**Step 3 — Expected count $E_i = N \cdot P_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_1 &= 6 \times 0.05 = 0.30 \\
E_2 &= 6 \times 0.20 = 1.20 \\
E_3 &= 6 \times 0.30 = 1.80 \\
E_4 &= 6 \times 0.075 = 0.45 \\
E_5 &= 6 \times 0.25 = 1.50 \\
E_6 &= 6 \times 0.125 = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative wheel:**

| Cutoff | Sector |
|---|---|
| 0.000 – 0.050 | Individual 1 |
| 0.050 – 0.250 | Individual 2 |
| 0.250 – 0.550 | Individual 3 |
| 0.550 – 0.625 | Individual 4 |
| 0.625 – 0.875 | Individual 5 |
| 0.875 – 1.000 | Individual 6 |

*(7 marks — 2 for description + 3 for probability and count + 2 for cumulative wheel)*

---

#### **Question B — Crossover + Mutation (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain **single-point** and **two-point crossover** with examples. Given parents:
- P1 = `11001011`
- P2 = `10110100`

Apply **single-point crossover at position 5** and **two-point crossover between positions 2 and 6**, and show the resulting offspring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **bit-flip mutation** in binary-coded GA. Given a chromosome `11001011` of length 8 and mutation probability $P_m = 0.125$, perform mutation using the random vector $\{0.15, 0.85, 0.05, 0.65, 0.20, 0.95, 0.40, 0.10\}$. State the final mutant chromosome. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — (a):**

**Single-Point Crossover:** A single cut point is randomly selected along the chromosome length. The two parents exchange their right-tail segments beyond the cut.

**Two-Point Crossover:** Two cut points are chosen, and the segment between them is swapped between the two parents.

**Single-point at position 5:**

Parents split as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P1 &= \underbrace{11001}_{L} \vert \ \underbrace{011}_{R} \\
P2 &= \underbrace{10110}_{L} \vert \ \underbrace{100}_{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

Offspring after right-tail exchange:
$$
\begin{aligned}
O1 &= 11001 \conc 100 = \mathbf{11001100} \\
O2 &= 10110 \conc 011 = \mathbf{10110011}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two-point between positions 2 and 6:**

Parents split as:
$$
\begin{aligned}
P1 &= 1 \vert \ \underbrace{1001}_{M} \vert \ 011 \\
P2 &= 1 \vert \ \underbrace{0110}_{M} \vert \ 100
\end{aligned}
$$

Middle segments swapped:
$$
\begin{aligned}
O1 &= 1 \conc 0110 \conc 011 = \mathbf{10110011} \\
O2 &= 1 \conc 1001 \conc 100 = \mathbf{11001100}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(7 marks — 2 for explanation + 2.5 for single-point + 2.5 for two-point)*

---

**Model Solution — (b):**

**Bit-Flip Mutation:** Each gene of the offspring is independently examined. With probability $P_m$, the bit is flipped (0 → 1 or 1 → 0); otherwise it is kept unchanged. This re-introduces lost genetic diversity and prevents premature convergence.

**Step-by-step mutation of `11001011`:**

| Position | Gene | $r_i$ | $r_i < P_m$ ? | Action | New Gene |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0.15 | No | Keep | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.85 | No | Keep | 1 |
| 3 | 0 | 0.05 | **Yes** | Flip | 1 |
| 4 | 0 | 0.65 | No | Keep | 0 |
| 5 | 1 | 0.20 | No | Keep | 1 |
| 6 | 0 | 0.95 | No | Keep | 0 |
| 7 | 1 | 0.40 | No | Keep | 1 |
| 8 | 1 | 0.10 | **Yes** | Flip | 0 |

**Final Mutant Chromosome:** `11101010`

*(7 marks — 2 for explanation + 4 for table + 1 for final answer)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $2^L - 1 \ge \text{intervals}$** — students often write $2^L \ge \text{intervals}$, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing `$P_i$` with `$E_i$`** — selection probability and expected count are different quantities; the latter is the former multiplied by $N$.
> 3. **Not summing to 1.0** — failing to verify the cumulative wheel ends at 1.0 reveals an arithmetic error.
> 4. **Crossover tail direction** — students often swap the *left* halves instead of right halves. Read the standard convention.
> 5. **Mutation "all bits mutated"** — interpreting $P_m$ as the *chromosome-level* mutation probability instead of *per-gene* probability.
> 6. **Misalignment of operator with coding** — applying bit-flip mutation to a permutation chromosome is invalid; KTU examiners will deduct full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **GA is a population-based metaheuristic** mimicking Darwinian evolution: selection + variation = adaptation.
- **Three core operators**: Selection (chooses parents), Crossover (recombines), Mutation (perturbs).
- **Coding precedes operators** — binary, real, integer, and permutation are the four canonical schemes.
- **Binary string length formula:** $L = \lceil \log_2 \left( \dfrac{(b - a)}{10^p} \cdot 2 \right) \rceil$ for precision $p$ and range $[a, b]$.
- **Decoding formula:** $x = a + \dfrac{\text{decoded}}{2^L - 1} \cdot (b - a)$.
- **Roulette Wheel probability:** $P_i = f_i / \sum f_j$, **Expected count:** $E_i = N \cdot P_i$.
- **Crossover probability $P_c$** is high (0.6 – 0.9) — primary exploration operator.
- **Mutation probability $P_m$** is low (0.001 – 0.1) — secondary diversity injection; Rechenberg's $1/L$ rule is a good default.
- **Elitism** copies the best individual to the next generation unchanged — guarantees monotonic improvement of best fitness.
- **Permutation problems** (TSP, scheduling) require special operators (OX, PMX, swap, inversion) — never use bit-flip on permutations.
- **Real-coded GA** is preferred for continuous, high-dimensional problems (BLX-$\alpha$, SBX, Gaussian mutation).
- **Termination criteria:** fixed generations, fitness threshold, or convergence (no improvement over $k$ generations).
- **Premature convergence** = population diversity $\Delta k$ collapses; counter it with **fitness scaling, rank selection, or adaptive mutation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
