For **maximization**, the inequalities are simply **reversed** ($\geq$ for the first, $>$ for the second).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Laptop Buying" Intuition

Imagine you are choosing between two laptops, **Laptop A** and **Laptop B**, judged on two criteria:

| Criterion | Laptop A | Laptop B |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Performance Score (higher = better) | **8 / 10** | **7 / 10** |
| Price in ₹ (lower = better) | ₹60,000 | ₹55,000 |

- **Laptop A** has better performance but costs more.
- **Laptop B** is cheaper but has lower performance.

> [!NOTE] **Intuition Check:** Is one laptop *universally* better? **No!** Each has a clear advantage in one criterion and a clear disadvantage in the other. Neither laptop **dominates** the other — they are both *non-dominated*. A rational buyer must perform a **trade-off** to choose.

Now suppose a third laptop **C** appears with Performance = 8/10 and Price = ₹55,000:

> **Laptop C dominates both A and B**, because it is at least as good in *every* criterion and strictly better in *at least one*. Any rational buyer would prefer C over A or B. Thus, A and B are **dominated solutions**, and C is a **non-dominated (Pareto-optimal) solution**.

This trade-off curve is the essence of **Pareto Optimality** — a set of solutions where you cannot improve one objective without worsening another.

### 1.3 Pareto Optimality — The Top of the Class

> [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition — Pareto-Optimal Solution**
> 
> A decision vector $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{X}$ is said to be **Pareto-optimal** (or **non-dominated**) if and only if:
> $$\nexists \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} \; \text{such that} \; F(\mathbf{x}) \prec F(\mathbf{x}^{*})$$
> 
> In words: *No other feasible solution dominates $\mathbf{x}^{*}$.*

The collection of all Pareto-optimal solutions forms the **Pareto-Optimal Set** ($\mathcal{P}^{*}$), and the projection of this set into objective space is called the **Pareto-Optimal Front** ($\mathcal{PF}^{*}$).

---

### 1.4 Key Vocabulary You MUST Memorize

| Term | Plain-English Meaning | Mathematical Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Decision Vector** | The variables we control (e.g., laptop specs) | $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ |
| **Objective Vector** | The outcomes we care about (e.g., price, speed) | $\mathbf{u} = F(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1, \dots, f_k)$ |
| **Feasible Region** | All admissible solutions | $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ |
| **Domination** | One solution is uniformly better | $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ |
| **Non-Dominated** | Cannot be improved in all objectives | $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{P}^{*}$ |
| **Pareto Front** | Trade-off curve in objective space | $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Logic of Domination — Why It Works

The concept of domination is built on **three foundational pillars** that any KTU answer must demonstrate:

- **Pillar 1 — Partial Order, Not Total Order:** Classical comparisons (greater, less) assume a single scalar. MOO produces a *vector*, which only admits a *partial* order. Domination formalizes this partial order.
- **Pillar 2 — Transitivity of Dominance:** If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$. This means a single "best of the best" can be found by iteratively removing dominated solutions.
- **Pillar 3 — Reflexivity & Asymmetry:** A solution does *not* dominate itself in the *strict* sense, and if $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \not\prec \mathbf{x}^{(1)}$.

### 2.2 Weak vs. Strict vs. Strong Domination

| Relation Type | Condition (minimization) | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Weak Domination** ($\preceq$) | $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ | Better or equal in *every* objective (no strictness required) |
| **Strict Domination** ($\prec$) | $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ **AND** $\exists j: f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ | At least as good everywhere, strictly better somewhere |
| **Strong Domination** | $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ | Strictly better in *every* objective (rare and restrictive) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] **Master these equations — they appear verbatim in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Domain |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Multi-Objective Problem (MOP) | $\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}} F(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, f_k(\mathbf{x}))$ | $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n, \; f_i : \mathbb{R}^n \to \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Domination ($\prec$, minimize) | $\mathbf{u} \prec \mathbf{v} \iff (\forall i: u_i \leq v_i) \land (\exists j: u_j < v_j)$ | Vector partial order |
| 3 | Domination ($\prec$, maximize) | $\mathbf{u} \prec \mathbf{v} \iff (\forall i: u_i \geq v_i) \land (\exists j: u_j > v_j)$ | Vector partial order |
| 4 | Pareto-Optimality | $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{P}^{*} \iff \nexists \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} : F(\mathbf{x}) \prec F(\mathbf{x}^{*})$ | Definition in decision space |
| 5 | Pareto-Optimal Set | $\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} \mid \nexists \mathbf{x}' : F(\mathbf{x}') \prec F(\mathbf{x})\}$ | Subset of $\mathcal{X}$ |
| 6 | Pareto-Optimal Front | $\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{F(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{P}^{*}\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k$ | Subset of objective space |
| 7 | Non-Dominated Sorting Rank | $\text{rank}(\mathbf{x}) = 1 + \text{number of solutions dominating } \mathbf{x}$ | Integer $\geq 1$ |

### 2.4 Properties of the Dominance Relation (Board-Favorite)

- **Irreflexive:** $\mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ (no solution strictly dominates itself).
- **Asymmetric:** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \Rightarrow \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$.
- **Transitive:** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \land \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$.
- **Antisymmetric (Weak):** $\mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y} \land \mathbf{y} \preceq \mathbf{x} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Utility:** Dominance-based ranking is the engine behind **NSGA-II** (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II), which is the most cited evolutionary MOO solver. It is widely used in *structural design optimization* (minimize weight, maximize stiffness), *controller tuning* (minimize error, minimize control effort), and *machine learning hyperparameter search*.

---

### 2.5 Worked Logic — Identifying a Non-Dominated Set by Hand

The standard KTU board algorithm is:

1. Evaluate $F(\mathbf{x})$ for all $N$ candidate solutions.
2. Initialize the non-dominated set $\mathcal{S} = \emptyset$.
3. For each solution $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$, check whether any other $\mathbf{x}^{(j)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$.
4. If *no* such $\mathbf{x}^{(j)}$ exists, add $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ to $\mathcal{S}$.
5. The resulting set $\mathcal{S}$ is the Pareto-Optimal Set $\mathcal{P}^{*}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical 2-Objective Demonstration (Board-Exam Standard)

Consider the **minimization** problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x}} \; F(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1(\mathbf{x}), \; f_2(\mathbf{x}))$$

with **six** candidate solutions whose objective vectors are listed below:

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A | 4 | 9 |
| B | 6 | 6 |
| C | 8 | 4 |
| D | 3 | 10 |
| E | 7 | 7 |
| F | 5 | 8 |

**Step 1 — Compare A vs. each other:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A \text{ vs } B &: (4 \leq 6) \text{ and } (9 \leq 6)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO — A is worse in } f_2 \\
A \text{ vs } C &: (4 \leq 8) \text{ and } (9 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
A \text{ vs } D &: (4 \leq 3)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO — A is worse in } f_1 \\
A \text{ vs } E &: (4 \leq 7) \text{ and } (9 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
A \text{ vs } F &: (4 \leq 5) \text{ and } (9 \leq 8)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** No solution dominates A. So **A is non-dominated**.

**Step 2 — Compare D vs. each other:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D \text{ vs } A &: (3 \leq 4) \text{ and } (10 \leq 9)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
D \text{ vs } B &: (3 \leq 6) \text{ and } (10 \leq 6)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
D \text{ vs } C &: (3 \leq 8) \text{ and } (10 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
D \text{ vs } E &: (3 \leq 7) \text{ and } (10 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
D \text{ vs } F &: (3 \leq 5) \text{ and } (10 \leq 8)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** **D is non-dominated** (lowest $f_1$ in the set).

**Step 3 — Compare F vs. each other:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \text{ vs } A &: (5 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
F \text{ vs } B &: (5 \leq 6) \text{ and } (8 \leq 6)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
F \text{ vs } C &: (5 \leq 8) \text{ and } (8 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
F \text{ vs } D &: (5 \leq 3)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
F \text{ vs } E &: (5 \leq 7) \text{ and } (8 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** **F is non-dominated.**

**Step 4 — Compare B, C, and E:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \text{ vs } E &: (6 \leq 7) \text{ and } (6 \leq 7) \text{ AND }(6<7) \text{ in both} \;\;\Rightarrow B \prec E \\
C \text{ vs } E &: (8 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
C \text{ vs } B &: (8 \leq 6)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $B \prec E$. E is **dominated** by B and should be discarded.

**Step 5 — Check if B is dominated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B \text{ vs } A &: (6 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
B \text{ vs } C &: (6 \leq 8) \text{ and } (6 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**B is non-dominated.** Same check shows **C is non-dominated**.

**Step 6 — Final Non-Dominated Set $\mathcal{P}^{*}$:**

$$
\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{A, B, C, D, F\}
$$

**Step 7 — Pareto Front $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$** (plot these five points in $(f_1, f_2)$ space):

$$
\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{(4,9),\, (6,6),\, (8,4),\, (3,10),\, (5,8)\}
$$

**Step 8 — Visual ordering (frontier points from "best $f_1$" to "best $f_2$"):**

$$\text{Front} = \{(3,10) \to (4,9) \to (5,8) \to (6,6) \to (8,4)\}$$

This monotone-decreasing trace is the **Pareto-Optimal Front** — E lies *below-right* of this curve and is therefore dominated.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Relation Do I Use?

> [!IMPORTANT] **Use this table during the KTU viva when asked to justify your dominance check.**

| Situation | Recommended Relation | Why |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Comparing two feasible candidate solutions | **Strict Domination ($\prec$)** | Standard Pareto rank check in NSGA-II |
| Initial archive insertion (is the new point useful?) | **Weak Domination ($\preceq$)** | Detects equal-in-all-dimensions replacements |
| Theoretical "absolute best" proofs | **Strong Domination** | For single-objective special cases |
| Tied solutions (all $f_i$ equal) | **No Domination** | Treated as non-dominated with respect to each other |

### 3.3 Algebraic Derivation of the Front Equation (Continuous Case)

For a continuous MOO with two objectives, the Pareto Front often satisfies an implicit relation derived via the **Lagrangian Necessary Condition**:

$$
\nabla f_1(\mathbf{x}^{*}) + \lambda \, \nabla f_2(\mathbf{x}^{*}) = \mathbf{0}, \quad \lambda \geq 0
$$

Solving for the scalar $\lambda$ links the trade-off slope at the front:

$$
\frac{df_2}{df_1}\bigg|_{\mathcal{PF}^{*}} = -\frac{1}{\lambda}
$$

This equation says the **marginal rate of substitution** between objectives along the front equals $-\lambda^{-1}$, capturing the "cost" in $f_2$ of reducing $f_1$ by one unit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Domination Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Solution A with vector u] --> check1{Q1: Is u_i ≤ v_i for EVERY objective i?}
    check1 -- NO --> indep[Incomparable: Neither dominates]
    check1 -- YES --> check2{Q2: Is u_j < v_j for AT LEAST ONE objective j?}
    check2 -- NO --> weakEq[Weak Domination: u ⪯ v, equal in all]
    check2 -- YES --> strictDom[A strictly dominates B]
    indep --> paretoSet[Add both to candidate Pareto set]
    weakEq --> discard[Discard the weaker one]
    strictDom --> discard2[Discard dominated solution B]
    paretoSet --> nextCheck{Any other solution dominates A?}
    nextCheck -- NO --> finalSet[Mark A as Pareto-optimal]
    nextCheck -- YES --> discard3[Discard A]
    finalSet --> output[Output: Pareto-Optimal Set P star]
    discard --> output
    discard2 --> output
    discard3 --> output
```

### 4.2 Objective-Space Pareto Front Architecture (2D)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph domRegion["DOMINATED REGION (lower-right)"]
        E1["E: 7,7 — DOMINATED by B: 6,6"]
    end
    subgraph paretoFront["PARETO OPTIMAL FRONT PF*"]
        D1["D: 3,10 — best f1"]
        A1["A: 4,9 — non-dominated"]
        F1["F: 5,8 — non-dominated"]
        B1["B: 6,6 — non-dominated"]
        C1["C: 8,4 — best f2"]
    end
    D1 --> A1 --> F1 --> B1 --> C1
    style domRegion fill:#fdd
    style paretoFront fill:#dfd
```

### 4.3 Non-Dominated Sorting (NSGA-II Layered View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pop["Population of N solutions"] --> rank1{"Compute non-domination rank"}
    rank1 --> F1["Front 1: rank = 1 (best, Pareto-optimal)"]
    rank1 --> F2["Front 2: rank = 2 (dominated only by Front 1)"]
    rank1 --> F3["Front 3: rank = 3 (dominated by Fronts 1 and 2)"]
    rank1 --> Fmore["Front k: ... (worst)"]
    F1 --> sel["Selection: prefer lower rank"]
    F2 --> sel
    F3 --> sel
    Fmore --> sel
    sel --> nextGen["Next Generation"]
```

### 4.4 Decision-Engine Block Diagram for Dominance Checking

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Decision Vector x in X"] --> B["Objective Function Evaluator F of x"]
    B --> C["Vector u = f1, f2, ..., fk"]
    C --> D["Pairwise Comparator"]
    D --> E["Dominance Test: 2k inequality checks"]
    E --> F{"Is u <= v in all i AND u < v in some j?"}
    F -- YES --> G["Output: x1 dominates x2"]
    F -- NO --> H["Output: Incomparable or x2 dominates x1"]
    G --> I["Update Pareto Archive"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Convergence Check: stop?"]
    J -- NO --> A
    J -- YES --> K["Final Pareto Front PF star"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> **Define Pareto Dominance in a multi-objective optimization problem. State the conditions for one solution to dominate another in a minimization problem.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pareto Dominance is a partial-ordering relation used in multi-objective optimization to compare two or more decision vectors. For a minimization problem with $k$ objectives, a solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ **dominates** $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ (written $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$) if and only if:

1. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **no worse** than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in **every** objective:
$$f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)}), \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, k$$

2. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **strictly better** in **at least one** objective:
$$\exists j \in \{1, \dots, k\} : f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$$

If neither dominates the other, both are *non-dominated* and lie on the Pareto-Optimal Front.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**  
> **Differentiate between Pareto-Optimal Set and Pareto-Optimal Front with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Pareto-Optimal Set ($\mathcal{P}^{*}$) | Pareto-Optimal Front ($\mathcal{PF}^{*}$) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Space** | Decision / Variable space ($\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$) | Objective space ($F(\mathbf{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^k$) |
| **Definition** | All $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{X}$ that are not dominated by any other feasible $\mathbf{x}$ | Image of $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ under the objective mapping $F$ |
| **Use** | Input to the decision-maker | Visualized trade-off curve given to the user |
| **Example** | All (material, thickness) combinations minimizing a beam's weight and deflection | The (weight, deflection) curve plotted in 2D objective space |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the formal definition of **Pareto Optimality**. With the help of a suitable block diagram, describe the role of a **Pareto Archive** in a multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA) like NSGA-II.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Definition (3 marks):**  
A decision vector $\mathbf{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{X}$ is **Pareto-optimal** if there exists **no** other feasible vector $\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}$ that dominates it:
$$\nexists \, \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} : F(\mathbf{x}) \prec F(\mathbf{x}^{*})$$

**Pareto-Optimal Set (2 marks):**
$$\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} \mid \nexists \, \mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{X} : F(\mathbf{x}') \prec F(\mathbf{x})\}$$

**Pareto-Optimal Front (2 marks):**
$$\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{F(\mathbf{x}) \mid \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{P}^{*}\} \subset \mathbb{R}^k$$

The **Pareto Archive** in NSGA-II stores the best non-dominated solutions found across generations. New candidate solutions are compared against archived entries via the dominance test; dominated candidates are discarded while non-dominated ones are added. The archive is the source of the final $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ returned to the user.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the following six solutions in a **minimization** problem with two objectives $f_1$ and $f_2$, identify the **Pareto-Optimal Set** and the **Pareto-Optimal Front** by performing a complete dominance analysis. Show every pairwise comparison.

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| P | 2 | 8 |
| Q | 5 | 4 |
| R | 6 | 6 |
| S | 1 | 9 |
| T | 4 | 5 |
| U | 3 | 7 |

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compare S vs. all (lowest $f_1$ candidate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \text{ vs } P &: (1 \leq 2) \text{ and } (9 \leq 8)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
S \text{ vs } Q &: (1 \leq 5) \text{ and } (9 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
S \text{ vs } R &: (1 \leq 6) \text{ and } (9 \leq 6)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
S \text{ vs } T &: (1 \leq 4) \text{ and } (9 \leq 5)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
S \text{ vs } U &: (1 \leq 3) \text{ and } (9 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**S is non-dominated.** ✓

**Step 2 — Check P:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P \text{ vs } U &: (2 \leq 3) \text{ and } (8 \leq 7)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
P \text{ vs } S &: \text{fails } f_1 \text{ comparison (}2 \not\leq 1\text{)} \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**P is non-dominated.** ✓

**Step 3 — Check U:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U \text{ vs } P &: (3 \leq 2)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
U \text{ vs } S &: (3 \leq 1)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**U is non-dominated.** ✓

**Step 4 — Check T:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T \text{ vs } Q &: (4 \leq 5) \text{ and } (5 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
T \text{ vs } U &: (4 \leq 3)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**T is non-dominated.** ✓

**Step 5 — Check Q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \text{ vs } T &: (5 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
Q \text{ vs } U &: (5 \leq 3)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q is non-dominated.** ✓

**Step 6 — Check R:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \text{ vs } Q &: (6 \leq 5)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO} \\
R \text{ vs } T &: (6 \leq 4)? \;\;\Rightarrow \text{NO}
\end{aligned}
$$

**R is dominated by Q** (Q has $f_1 = 5 < 6$ and $f_2 = 4 < 6$). **Discard R.** ✗

**Final Results:**

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{S, P, U, T, Q\}, \quad \mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{(1,9),\,(2,8),\,(3,7),\,(4,5),\,(5,4)\}}
$$

---

> **Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Weak Domination**, **Strict Domination**, and **Strong Domination** with their mathematical definitions. Provide one numerical example for each in a 2-objective minimization scenario.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Type | Mathematical Condition | Numerical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Weak Domination** ($\preceq$) | $\forall i: u_i \leq v_i$ | A = (3, 4) **weakly dominates** B = (3, 5) (equal in $f_1$, better in $f_2$) |
| **Strict Domination** ($\prec$) | $\forall i: u_i \leq v_i$ AND $\exists j: u_j < v_j$ | A = (3, 4) **strictly dominates** C = (5, 6) (better in both) |
| **Strong Domination** | $\forall i: u_i < v_i$ | A = (3, 4) **strongly dominates** D = (7, 9) (strictly better in *every* dimension) |

**Why they differ (2 marks):** Weak domination allows ties, strict domination is the **standard Pareto criterion** (the one KTU exams expect by default), and strong domination is the **most restrictive** — it requires uniform improvement, which is rarely achievable in conflicting objectives.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three logical pillars** of the dominance relation (Irreflexivity, Asymmetry, Transitivity). Why is each property necessary for ranking solutions in NSGA-II?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Pillar 1 — Irreflexivity (2 marks):**  
$\mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ (for strict domination).  
A solution cannot dominate itself. This guarantees that the *count* of dominating solutions is well-defined and that the rank of a solution is never infinite for self-comparison. NSGA-II uses this to assign the **front index 1** (rank 1) to all non-dominated solutions without confusion.

**Pillar 2 — Asymmetry (2 marks):**  
If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \not\prec \mathbf{x}^{(1)}$.  
This prevents contradictory pairings. If asymmetry failed, a single solution could both dominate and be dominated by another, breaking the partial order. NSGA-II relies on this for **crowding-distance comparison** within a front.

**Pillar 3 — Transitivity (2 marks):**  
$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \land \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)} \Rightarrow \mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$.  
This enables the construction of layered fronts in $\mathcal{O}(MN^2)$ time (NSGA-II's fast non-dominated sort). Without transitivity, no stable front hierarchy would exist.

**Engineering Significance (1 mark):**  
These three properties mathematically justify that **dominance induces a valid partial order** on the feasible set, which is the foundation of any MOO solver.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the strict "AND" condition:** Many students write only the first condition ($\forall i: u_i \leq v_i$). This describes *weak* domination, not *strict* domination. **Always state both conditions** to score full marks.
> 2. **Mixing minimization and maximization signs:** In a maximization problem, the inequalities are *reversed* ($\geq$ and $>$). Mark off if signs are wrong.
> 3. **Stopping after the first dominated solution found:** The question asks for the **full** Pareto Set — you must verify that no other solution dominates a candidate before declaring it Pareto-optimal.
> 4. **Confusing Pareto Set with Pareto Front:** Set = decision space; Front = objective space. Examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this slip.
> 5. **Omitting the strict-in-at-least-one clause:** A solution that ties another in *all* objectives does **not** strictly dominate it; both are non-dominated.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOP)** involves $k \geq 2$ objective functions, yielding a vector-valued objective $F(\mathbf{x}) = (f_1, \dots, f_k)$.
- **Pareto Dominance** is a **partial order** on objective vectors; it is the standard tool to compare solutions in MOO.
- The **strict dominance** condition has **two** parts: (i) no worse in *every* objective, (ii) strictly better in *at least one* objective.
- A **Pareto-optimal** solution has **no** feasible dominator — it is *non-dominated*.
- The **Pareto-Optimal Set** ($\mathcal{P}^{*}$) lives in **decision space**; the **Pareto-Optimal Front** ($\mathcal{PF}^{*}$) lives in **objective space**.
- Dominance satisfies **irreflexivity, asymmetry, and transitivity** — these three properties are mandatory to mention in any 7+ mark question.
- **NSGA-II** uses **non-dominated sorting** to rank solutions into layers (fronts) for selection.
- A **dominated solution** should be **discarded**; a **non-dominated** one goes into the **Pareto archive**.
- For **minimization** problems use $\leq$ and $<$; for **maximization** use $\geq$ and $>$.
- **Weak domination** allows ties; **strong domination** requires strict improvement in *every* objective.
- The Pareto Front is typically a **monotone trade-off curve** — improvement in one objective is paid for by worsening in another.
- Mastering dominance is the **prerequisite** for understanding NSGA-II, SPEA2, and MOEA/D in subsequent modules.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Dominance Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Properties of Dominance Relation

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dominance Relation (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> In Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO), given two decision vectors $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X}$ (the feasible decision space), a vector $\mathbf{x}$ is said to **dominate** $\mathbf{y}$ (denoted $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$ or $\mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y}$) if and only if $\mathbf{x}$ is **no worse** than $\mathbf{y}$ in all objectives and **strictly better** in at least one objective. This binary relation forms the mathematical foundation of **Pareto Optimality** and is central to NSGA-II, SPEA2, and MOEA/D algorithms covered in Module 4.

For a minimization problem with $M$ objectives, the formal mathematical statement is:

$$
\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \iff
\begin{cases}
\forall\, i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\} : f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \\
\exists\, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, M\} : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y})
\end{cases}
$$

The associated strict partial order operators commonly used in KTU examination answers are:

- **Weak Dominance** ($\preceq$): $\forall i, \; f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ — equal in all objectives allowed.
- **Strict Dominance** ($\prec\prec$): $\forall i, \; f_i(\mathbf{x}) < f_i(\mathbf{y})$ — strictly better in every objective.
- **Standard (Pareto) Dominance** ($\prec$): The combination stated above.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** When the KTU question says "x dominates y" without qualifier, it implicitly means the **Pareto Dominance** $\prec$, not the weak or strict variant. Always verify the minimization/maximization context.

## 2. Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Choosing a Laptop**
> Imagine you are buying a laptop and you care about two objectives: **(i)** lower **Price** and **(ii)** higher **Battery Life**. Suppose you see three laptops in a shop:
> - **Laptop A**: ₹50,000, 8 hours battery
> - **Laptop B**: ₹60,000, 10 hours battery
> - **Laptop C**: ₹70,000, 12 hours battery
>
> Now ask: *Is A better than B?* A is cheaper (good), but has less battery (bad). A does **not dominate** B because no single laptop beats the other on both criteria.
>
> But if you had a fourth **Laptop D**: ₹45,000, 9 hours battery — then **D dominates A** because D is cheaper *and* has more battery. D is strictly better on both fronts.
>
> However, between A and D, neither is "objectively the best" without knowing your personal preferences — and *that* is exactly the kind of conflict the **Dominance Relation** formalises in MOO.

**Why this matters in Soft Computing:** When a Genetic Algorithm optimises two competing objectives simultaneously (e.g., minimising cost *and* maximising accuracy of a neural network), it cannot pick a single "best" solution. Instead, it returns a **set of non-dominated solutions** (the Pareto Front) — solutions where no other solution is uniformly better. The *properties* of the dominance relation guarantee that this set is well-defined, consistent, and algorithmically searchable.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Five Foundational Properties

The Dominance Relation $\prec$ on the objective space $\mathcal{Z} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{M}$ exhibits the following **five** algebraic properties. KTU examiners frequently ask 3-mark and 7-mark questions that test these properties with proof or counter-proof.

### Property 1: Irreflexivity (No Self-Dominance)

A solution **cannot dominate itself** in any Pareto sense. Formally:

$$
\forall\, \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} : \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}
$$

**Intuition:** A solution is never strictly better than itself in at least one objective. There is always a tie in all objectives when comparing a vector to itself, so the strict-improvement clause of Pareto dominance fails.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Irreflexivity makes the dominance relation a *strict* partial order, not a partial order. A genuine partial order must be **reflexive** ($x \preceq x$). Students often write "dominance is a partial order" — this is a 1-mark deduction in valuation.

### Property 2: Asymmetry (Asymmetric Dominance)

If $\mathbf{x}$ dominates $\mathbf{y}$, then $\mathbf{y}$ **cannot** dominate $\mathbf{x}$. Formally:

$$
\forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X} : \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \implies \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}
$$

**Intuition:** If A is at least as good as B in every objective and strictly better in some, then it is logically impossible for B to also be at least as good as A in every objective (otherwise they would be identical and strict improvement could not hold).

### Property 3: Antisymmetry (in the Weak-Dominance Sense)

For the **weak dominance** operator $\preceq$, the relation satisfies antisymmetry:

$$
\forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X} : \mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \preceq \mathbf{x} \implies \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}
$$

**Intuition:** If two solutions are no worse than each other in every objective, they must be identical. This is the standard definition of antisymmetry adapted to vector-valued functions.

### Property 4: Transitivity (Chain of Dominance)

If $\mathbf{x}$ dominates $\mathbf{y}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ dominates $\mathbf{z}$, then $\mathbf{x}$ dominates $\mathbf{z}$:

$$
\forall\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathcal{X} : \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}
$$

**Intuition:** Dominance composes along chains. If A is at least as good as B in every objective and B is at least as good as C in every objective, then A must be at least as good as C in every objective. The strict-improvement requirement also carries through.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Transitivity Matters Algorithmically:** Transitivity is the property that allows Pareto-based GA *sorting* (like the **Non-Dominated Sort** of NSGA-II) to be performed efficiently. If dominance were not transitive, we could not iteratively eliminate dominated solutions in a single forward sweep, and the algorithm's complexity would explode.

### Property 5: Non-Totality (Incomparability Exists)

The dominance relation is **not total** — there exist feasible solutions that are mutually non-dominated. Formally:

$$
\exists\, \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X} : \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}
$$

**Intuition:** Not every pair of solutions can be compared. Some solutions trade off one objective for another (e.g., lower cost but lower quality). These incomparable solutions form the **Pareto Front** — the engineer's final decision set.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** Students often claim "dominance is a total order". This is **false** for $M \geq 2$ objectives. A total order requires every pair to be comparable; in MOO, the existence of non-dominated pairs is precisely *why* we need a Pareto front in the first place.

## 2. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Mathematical Statement | Type | Engineering Utility |
|---|----------|------------------------|------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Pareto Dominance** | $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \iff \forall i\, f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \;\wedge\; \exists j\, f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y})$ | Definition | Ranking candidate designs |
| 2 | **Weak Dominance** | $\mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y} \iff \forall i\, f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ | Definition | Filtering weakly-better designs |
| 3 | **Strict Dominance** | $\mathbf{x} \prec\prec \mathbf{y} \iff \forall i\, f_i(\mathbf{x}) < f_i(\mathbf{y})$ | Definition | Identifying strictly superior designs |
| 4 | **Irreflexivity** | $\forall \mathbf{x} : \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ | Property | Ensures non-trivial Pareto front |
| 5 | **Asymmetry** | $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \implies \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ | Property | Prevents dominance cycles |
| 6 | **Transitivity** | $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$ | Property | Enables efficient non-dominated sort |
| 7 | **Antisymmetry (weak)** | $\mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \preceq \mathbf{x} \implies \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{y}$ | Property | Confirms weak dominance is a partial order |
| 8 | **Non-Totality** | $\exists \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} : \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ | Property | Justifies existence of Pareto front |
| 9 | **Strict Partial Order** | $\prec$ is irreflexive + transitive | Classification | Categorical placement of $\prec$ |
| 10 | **Non-Dominated Set** | $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X} \mid \not\exists\, \mathbf{y} : \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{x}\}$ | Output | Final deliverable of MOO-GA |

## 3. Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where Dominance Properties Are Used in Practice:**
> - **Mechanical Design:** Optimising weight *vs.* strength of a chassis beam — Pareto front gives the design engineer a menu of trade-offs.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Minimising switching loss *vs.* minimising conduction loss in power MOSFET selection.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Cost *vs.* safety factor in dam design.
> - **Computer Science:** Latency *vs.* accuracy in deep-learning model deployment on edge devices.
> - **Bioinformatics:** Sensitivity *vs.* specificity trade-off in classifier threshold tuning.

In every case, the **transitivity** of dominance guarantees that if a designer ranks designs A, B, C, and the rankings form a chain, the relative ordering is consistent. The **non-totality** of dominance tells the designer honestly that some designs are simply *incomparable* without further preference information.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. Proof of Irreflexivity

**Statement:** $\forall \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{X}, \; \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$.

**Proof (by contradiction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume, for contradiction, that } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{x}. \\
&\text{By the definition of Pareto dominance:} \\
&\qquad \exists\, j \in \{1, \ldots, M\} : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}) \\
&\text{But for any real-valued function and any input:} \\
&\qquad f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}) \text{ is a strict inequality with both sides identical.} \\
&\text{Such a statement is false for any well-defined function } f_j : \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}. \\
&\text{Hence our assumption is contradicted.} \\
&\therefore \; \mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2. Proof of Asymmetry

**Statement:** $\forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X}, \; \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \implies \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$.

**Proof (direct):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}. \\
&\text{By definition, two conditions hold simultaneously:} \\
&\qquad \text{(C1)} \; \forall i : f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \\
&\qquad \text{(C2)} \; \exists j : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y}) \\
&\text{Now suppose, for contradiction, that } \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{x}. \\
&\text{Then by definition of } \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{x}: \\
&\qquad \text{(C3)} \; \forall i : f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}) \\
&\text{Combining (C1) and (C3) for any objective index } i: \\
&\qquad f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \;\wedge\; f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}) \\
&\qquad \implies f_i(\mathbf{x}) = f_i(\mathbf{y}) \quad \text{(antisymmetry of real numbers)} \\
&\text{This equality must hold } \forall i \in \{1, \ldots, M\}. \\
&\text{But (C2) requires } \exists j : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y}), \text{ which directly contradicts } f_j(\mathbf{x}) = f_j(\mathbf{y}). \\
&\text{Therefore, } \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{x} \text{ cannot hold.} \\
&\therefore \; \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \implies \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3. Proof of Transitivity

**Statement:** $\forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathcal{X}, \; \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$.

**Proof (direct, component-wise):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assume } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \text{ and } \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z}. \\
&\text{By definition of } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}: \\
&\qquad \forall i : f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \quad \text{...(A)} \\
&\qquad \exists j : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y}) \quad \text{...(B)} \\
&\text{By definition of } \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z}: \\
&\qquad \forall i : f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z}) \quad \text{...(C)} \\
&\qquad \exists k : f_k(\mathbf{y}) < f_k(\mathbf{z}) \quad \text{...(D)} \\
&\text{From (A) and (C), for every } i: \\
&\qquad f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z}) \\
&\qquad \therefore f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z}) \quad \text{(transitivity of } \leq \text{ in } \mathbb{R}) \\
&\text{This establishes the first Pareto condition for } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}. \\
&\text{For the second condition, observe that either } j = k \text{ or } j \neq k: \\
&\quad \textbf{Case 1: } j = k. \text{ Then from (B) and (D):} \\
&\qquad f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y}) < f_j(\mathbf{z}) \\
&\qquad \therefore f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{z}) \\
&\quad \textbf{Case 2: } j \neq k. \text{ Pick index } j \text{ from (B):} \\
&\qquad f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_j(\mathbf{z}) \quad \text{(using (C) for index } j) \\
&\qquad \therefore f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{z}) \\
&\text{In both cases, } \exists j : f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{z}), \text{ satisfying the strict-improvement clause.} \\
&\text{Hence both Pareto conditions hold, and } \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}. \\
&\therefore \; \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4. Worked Numerical Example — Identifying Dominance

Consider $M = 2$ minimization objectives, with five candidate solutions $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}, \mathbf{E}$ having objective values as given in the table below:

| Solution | $f_1$ (min) | $f_2$ (min) |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| A | 4 | 5 |
| B | 6 | 2 |
| C | 3 | 4 |
| D | 5 | 5 |
| E | 7 | 3 |

**Step 1 — Compare A and B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&f_1(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \leq f_1(\mathbf{B}) = 6 \quad \checkmark \\
&f_2(\mathbf{A}) = 5 \not\leq f_2(\mathbf{B}) = 2 \quad \times
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion: Neither A dominates B nor B dominates A. They are **incomparable** (non-totality property illustrated).

**Step 2 — Compare A and C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&f_1(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \not\leq f_1(\mathbf{C}) = 3 \quad \times
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion: A does not dominate C. But check the reverse:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&f_1(\mathbf{C}) = 3 \leq f_1(\mathbf{A}) = 4 \quad \checkmark \\
&f_2(\mathbf{C}) = 4 \leq f_2(\mathbf{A}) = 5 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both conditions for $\mathbf{C} \prec \mathbf{A}$ are satisfied. Hence **C dominates A**.

**Step 3 — Compare C and B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&f_1(\mathbf{C}) = 3 \leq f_1(\mathbf{B}) = 6 \quad \checkmark \\
&f_2(\mathbf{C}) = 4 \not\leq f_2(\mathbf{B}) = 2 \quad \times
\end{aligned}
$$

Conclusion: Incomparable.

**Step 4 — Identify the Non-Dominated Set (Pareto Front):**

A solution is in the Pareto front if **no other solution dominates it**.

- **A** is dominated by C → not in front.
- **B** — check all others. C: $f_2$ fails. D: $f_1(\mathbf{D})=5 \leq 6 \checkmark$, $f_2(\mathbf{D})=5 \not\leq 2 \times$. E: $f_1(\mathbf{E})=7 \not\leq 6 \times$. So **B is not dominated by any**.
- **C** — check A: $f_1(\mathbf{A})=4 \not\leq 3 \times$. B: $f_2$ fails. D, E fail. So **C is not dominated**.
- **D** — check C: $f_1(\mathbf{C})=3 \leq 5 \checkmark$, $f_2(\mathbf{C})=4 \leq 5 \checkmark$. So **C dominates D** → D is not in front.
- **E** — check B: $f_1(\mathbf{B})=6 \leq 7 \checkmark$, $f_2(\mathbf{B})=2 \leq 3 \checkmark$. So **B dominates E** → E is not in front.

**Final Pareto Front:** $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}\}$.

This pair forms a **trade-off curve**: B has lower $f_2$ but higher $f_1$, while C has lower $f_1$ but higher $f_2$. The dominance relation correctly refuses to declare a winner — this is the essence of multi-objective reasoning.

## 5. Analytical Matrix — Property Verification Summary

| Property | $\prec$ (Pareto) | $\preceq$ (Weak) | $\prec\prec$ (Strict) | $\leq$ (Scalar) |
|----------|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|-----------------|
| Irreflexive | ✓ | ✗ (reflexive) | ✓ | ✗ (reflexive) |
| Asymmetric | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Antisymmetric | N/A | ✓ | N/A | ✓ |
| Transitive | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Total | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Strict Partial Order | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 1. Mermaid Flowchart — Dominance Check Algorithm

The following flowchart depicts the canonical decision logic used inside NSGA-II's non-dominated sort subroutine to test whether $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$ for a pair of solutions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive two solutions x and y] --> B[Initialize flag betterEqual = true, flag strictlyBetter = false]
    B --> C[Loop over all M objectives, i = 1 to M]
    C --> D{Is f_i x less than equal to f_i y?}
    D -- Yes --> E{Is f_i x strictly less than f_i y?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Set strictlyBetter = true]
    E -- No --> G[No change, continue loop]
    F --> H[Increment i, continue loop]
    G --> H
    D -- No --> I[Set betterEqual = false]
    I --> J[Break loop immediately]
    H --> K{Is i greater than M?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L{betterEqual == true AND strictlyBetter == true?}
    J --> M[Return: x does NOT dominate y]
    L -- Yes --> N[Return: x dominates y, x precedes y]
    L -- No --> M
```

## 2. Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Dominance Operators

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BASE[Binary Relations on R to the M]
        R1[Reflexive]
        R2[Irreflexive]
        R3[Symmetric]
        R4[Asymmetric]
        R5[Antisymmetric]
        R6[Transitive]
        R7[Total]
        R8[Partial]
    end

    subgraph SPO[Strict Partial Order]
        S1[Irreflexive]
        S2[Asymmetric]
        S3[Transitive]
    end

    subgraph PO[Partial Order]
        P1[Reflexive]
        P2[Antisymmetric]
        P3[Transitive]
    end

    subgraph TO[Total Order]
        T1[Partial Order]
        T2[Comparability of all pairs]
    end

    BASE --> SPO
    BASE --> PO
    SPO -.Pareto Dominance Maps Here.-> SPO
    PO -.Weak Dominance Maps Here.-> PO
    PO --> TO
    SPO --> SPO
    style SPO fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style PO fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style TO fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d
    style BASE fill:#f5e6e6,stroke:#7b241c
```

## 3. Mermaid Conceptual Map — Dominance Properties & Their Implications

```mermaid
mindmap

        Definition
            Pareto Dominance
            Weak Dominance
            Strict Dominance
        Properties

                No self dominance
            Asymmetric
```

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Define the dominance relation used in multi-objective optimization. State any three of its properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Given two solutions $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y} \in \mathcal{X}$ in a minimization MOO problem, $\mathbf{x}$ is said to dominate $\mathbf{y}$ (denoted $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$) if $\forall i \in \{1, \ldots, M\}, f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ and $\exists j \in \{1, \ldots, M\}, f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y})$.
>
> **Three properties (1.5 marks, 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Irreflexivity:** $\mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$ for all $\mathbf{x}$.
> 2. **Asymmetry:** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \implies \mathbf{y} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$.
> 3. **Transitivity:** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$ and $\mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between weak dominance and strict dominance. Is Pareto dominance a total order? Justify.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Weak vs Strict (2 marks):**
> - **Weak Dominance** $\mathbf{x} \preceq \mathbf{y}$: requires $f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ for *all* $i$, with no strict-improvement requirement.
> - **Strict Dominance** $\mathbf{x} \prec\prec \mathbf{y}$: requires $f_i(\mathbf{x}) < f_i(\mathbf{y})$ for *all* $i$ — must be better in *every* objective.
> - **Pareto Dominance** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$: requires weak dominance in all objectives **plus** strict improvement in at least one — a middle ground.
>
> **Total Order? (1 mark):** No, Pareto dominance is **not** a total order for $M \geq 2$ objectives. There exist non-dominated pairs (e.g., trade-off solutions), violating the totality axiom which requires every pair to be comparable.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. Module-4 choice questions are common. **Solve either Option A or Option B in full.**

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** State and prove the **transitivity property** of the Pareto dominance relation. Show clearly why this property is essential for the **non-dominated sorting** procedure in NSGA-II. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the four candidate solutions given below (with two minimization objectives $f_1$ and $f_2$), construct the complete **dominance graph**, identify the **Pareto-optimal set**, and verify the **irreflexivity** and **asymmetry** properties on this dataset. **(7 marks)**

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|----------|-------|-------|
| P1 | 2 | 8 |
| P2 | 4 | 4 |
| P3 | 6 | 2 |
| P4 | 5 | 5 |

#### Model Solution for Q-A (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement (1 mark):** $\forall \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z} \in \mathcal{X}, \; \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \;\wedge\; \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z} \implies \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$.
>
> **Proof (4.5 marks):** Presented in full in Section 3 of these notes. Key steps:
> 1. From $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$: $f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ for all $i$ and strict improvement in some $j$.
> 2. From $\mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z}$: $f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z})$ for all $i$ and strict improvement in some $k$.
> 3. Compose: $f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z})$, so $f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{z})$ for all $i$.
> 4. Case-split on $j = k$ or $j \neq k$ to establish $f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{z})$.
> 5. Both Pareto conditions hold, so $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{z}$. $\blacksquare$
>
> **Importance to NSGA-II (1.5 marks):** Transitivity guarantees that if a solution is dominated, all its dominators are also dominated by the same chain's apex. This allows NSGA-II to perform **rank assignment in a single forward pass** over the population, yielding $O(MN^2)$ complexity. Without transitivity, dominance would be inconsistent and ranking would be ambiguous.

#### Model Solution for Q-A (b):

**Step 1 — Pair-wise dominance comparison (3 marks):**

| Pair | $f_1$ check | $f_2$ check | Result |
|------|-------------|-------------|--------|
| P1 vs P2 | $2 \leq 4$ ✓ | $8 \not\leq 4$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| P1 vs P3 | $2 \leq 6$ ✓ | $8 \not\leq 2$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| P1 vs P4 | $2 \leq 5$ ✓ | $8 \not\leq 5$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| P2 vs P3 | $4 \leq 6$ ✓ | $4 \not\leq 2$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| P2 vs P4 | $4 \leq 5$ ✓ | $4 \leq 5$ ✓ | **P2 ≺ P4** |
| P3 vs P4 | $6 \not\leq 5$ ✗ | — | P3 does not dominate P4 |

**Step 2 — Dominance graph (2 marks):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    P2Node[P2] -- dominates --> P4Node[P4]
    P1Node[P1]
    P3Node[P3]
```

Only one directed edge exists: P2 → P4. P1, P2, P3 are mutually non-dominated.

**Step 3 — Pareto-optimal set (1 mark):** $\mathcal{P} = \{\text{P1}, \text{P2}, \text{P3}\}$.

**Step 4 — Property verification (1 mark):**
- **Irreflexivity:** No self-loop in the graph; e.g., P1 ≮ P1 because $f_2(8) \not< f_2(8)$.
- **Asymmetry:** The single edge P2 → P4 has no reverse edge P4 → P2, confirming asymmetry on this dataset.

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** List the **five key properties** of the dominance relation in multi-objective optimization. For each property, provide a one-line intuitive justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing engineer must select a motor minimising **cost** ($f_1$) and **energy consumption** ($f_2$). Five motors have been shortlisted with the following values. Identify the **Pareto-optimal motors**. Justify your answer by listing all dominance relations and verifying **transitivity** on the dataset. **(7 marks)**

| Motor | Cost (₹) | Energy (W) |
|-------|----------|------------|
| M1 | 1000 | 80 |
| M2 | 1500 | 50 |
| M3 | 2000 | 40 |
| M4 | 1200 | 70 |
| M5 | 1800 | 55 |

#### Model Solution for Q-B (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Five properties (each 1 mark for statement + 0.4 for intuition, totalling 7 marks):**
>
> 1. **Irreflexivity** — A solution is never strictly better than itself in any objective, so $\mathbf{x} \not\prec \mathbf{x}$.
> 2. **Asymmetry** — If A is at least as good as B in every objective and strictly better in one, B cannot reciprocate.
> 3. **Transitivity** — If A dominates B and B dominates C, then A dominates C; dominance composes along chains.
> 4. **Antisymmetry (weak)** — If A and B are mutually weakly-dominated, they must be identical vectors.
> 5. **Non-totality** — For $M \geq 2$ objectives, there exist non-dominated pairs representing trade-offs.

#### Model Solution for Q-B (b):

**Step 1 — Tabulate all pair-wise comparisons (3 marks):**

| Pair | $f_1$ | $f_2$ | Result |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| M1 vs M2 | $1000 \leq 1500$ ✓ | $80 \not\leq 50$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| M1 vs M3 | $1000 \leq 2000$ ✓ | $80 \not\leq 40$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| M1 vs M4 | $1000 \leq 1200$ ✓ | $80 \not\leq 70$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| M1 vs M5 | $1000 \leq 1800$ ✓ | $80 \not\leq 55$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| M2 vs M3 | $1500 \leq 2000$ ✓ | $50 \not\leq 40$ ✗ | Incomparable |
| M2 vs M4 | $1500 \not\leq 1200$ ✗ | — | M2 ≮ M4 |
| M2 vs M5 | $1500 \leq 1800$ ✓ | $50 \leq 55$ ✓ | **M2 ≺ M5** |
| M3 vs M4 | $2000 \not\leq 1200$ ✗ | — | M3 ≮ M4 |
| M3 vs M5 | $2000 \not\leq 1800$ ✗ | — | M3 ≮ M5 |
| M4 vs M5 | $1200 \leq 1800$ ✓ | $70 \not\leq 55$ ✗ | Incomparable |

**Step 2 — Dominated solutions (1 mark):** M5 is dominated by M2. All others are non-dominated.

**Step 3 — Pareto-optimal set (1 mark):** $\mathcal{P} = \{\text{M1}, \text{M2}, \text{M3}, \text{M4}\}$.

**Step 4 — Transitivity verification (2 marks):** The only dominance edge in the graph is M2 → M5. To verify transitivity, we would need a chain M2 → X → M5 or similar; since no other solution dominates M2 and M5 is only dominated by M2, the chain condition vacuously holds. Formally, no triple ($\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}, \mathbf{z}$) with $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y} \prec \mathbf{z}$ exists in this dataset, so transitivity is **trivially satisfied** (vacuously true).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check both directions of inequality.** A student may write "M1 dominates M2 because 1000 < 1500" — losing 2 marks for ignoring the second objective. **Always check ALL objectives.**
> 2. **Confusing Pareto dominance with the lexicographic order.** Lexicographic order compares objectives sequentially, while Pareto dominance checks all objectives simultaneously. The two differ fundamentally.
> 3. **Mis-stating transitivity as "if A dominates C and B dominates C then..."** — this is wrong. Transitivity is a *chain* property, not a *common-dominatee* property.
> 4. **Claiming Pareto dominance is a total order.** This is the single most common conceptual error. MOO exists *because* dominance is partial.
> 5. **Ignoring minimization vs maximization context.** A 1-mark deduction if the answer does not specify the optimization direction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Definition:** $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{y}$ iff $\forall i, f_i(\mathbf{x}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{y})$ AND $\exists j, f_j(\mathbf{x}) < f_j(\mathbf{y})$.
> - **Three dominance variants:** Pareto $\prec$, weak $\preceq$, strict $\prec\prec$ — know the distinction.
> - **Irreflexivity** holds for $\prec$ and $\prec\prec$, but **not** for $\preceq$.
> - **Asymmetry** holds for $\prec$ and $\prec\prec$, but **not** for $\preceq$ (which is antisymmetric instead).
> - **Transitivity** holds for all three dominance variants — this is the *algorithmic* property that powers NSGA-II.
> - **Non-totality** is the reason MOO produces a *set* of solutions (the Pareto front) rather than a single optimum.
> - $\prec$ is a **Strict Partial Order** — irreflexive, asymmetric, transitive. It is **not** a partial order (which requires reflexivity).
> - $\preceq$ is a **Partial Order** — reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive. (Reflexive: every solution is weakly dominated by itself.)
> - The **Pareto Front** = set of all non-dominated solutions in the feasible region.
> - **Transitivity proof** requires the case-split $j = k$ vs $j \neq k$ — examiners reward this rigor.
> - **Asymmetry proof** uses the antisymmetry of $\leq$ in $\mathbb{R}$ — a key sub-lemma.
> - Always declare the **optimization direction** (minimization or maximization) before stating dominance.
> - **Real-world analogy:** Trade-off curves in product design (cost vs quality) are visualisations of the Pareto front.
> - **Algorithm connection:** Non-dominated sort, crowding distance, rank assignment in NSGA-II all rely on dominance properties.
> - The dominance relation makes MOO **mathematically tractable** even when no single "best" solution exists.
> - For scalar ($M = 1$) optimization, dominance reduces to the standard $\leq$ relation, and the Pareto front collapses to the unique optimum.

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Pareto Optimality** (also known as **Pareto Efficiency**) is a foundational concept in **multi-objective optimization** that describes a state of resource allocation where it is impossible to make any one objective better off without simultaneously making at least one other objective worse off. In the context of **soft computing** and **evolutionary algorithms** (such as NSGA-II, SPEA2, and MOEA/D), Pareto Optimality is the cornerstone for solving problems involving two or more conflicting objectives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A solution $\vec{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{F}$ (feasible region) is said to be **Pareto Optimal** if there exists no other feasible solution $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ that dominates it. Equivalently, no improvement can be made in any objective function without causing degradation in at least one other objective function.

Mathematically, for a minimization problem with $k$ objectives:

$$\vec{x}^{*} \text{ is Pareto Optimal} \iff \nexists \vec{x} \in \mathcal{F} : f_i(\vec{x}) \leq f_i(\vec{x}^{*}) \;\; \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\} \text{ and } f_j(\vec{x}) < f_j(\vec{x}^{*}) \text{ for at least one } j$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Limited-Budget Shopper"

Imagine you walk into a shop with a **fixed wallet of ₹500**, and you want to maximize:
- **Quantity** of items bought
- **Quality** of items bought

You cannot have *both* maximize quantity *and* maximize quality because high-quality items cost more. If you try to buy a better item, you must sacrifice quantity, and vice versa.

Any "best" combination you settle on is a **Pareto Optimal** solution — because the shopkeeper cannot give you *more* quality *and* *more* quantity for the same ₹500. Any "deal" where you could improve one without hurting the other simply does not exist at that price-point.

The set of all such "best trade-offs" forms the **Pareto Front** — a curve of optimal compromises.

## 1.3 Why Pareto Optimality Matters in Soft Computing

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:**
> Classical optimization (gradient descent, simplex) finds a *single* optimum. Soft computing methods (GA, PSO, ANN-fuzzy hybrids) excel at finding a **set of trade-off solutions** — and this set is exactly the **Pareto Front**. This is why Pareto Optimality is the central concept of every multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA).

| Concept | Role in Soft Computing |
|---|---|
| **Pareto Dominance** | Comparison operator replacing "greater than / less than" |
| **Pareto Optimal Set** | Output of a successful MOEA |
| **Pareto Front** | Visualization of trade-offs to the decision-maker |
| **Non-dominated Sorting** | Ranking mechanism inside NSGA-II |

## 1.4 Historical Context (Board-Expected Knowledge)

The concept is named after the Italian economist **Vilfredo Federico Damaso Pareto** (1848–1923), who introduced it in 1906 during his studies of economic efficiency and income distribution. In engineering and computer science, it was popularized in the 1980s–1990s with the rise of evolutionary multi-objective optimization.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Foundational Definitions (Hierarchical Breakdown)

### 2.1.1 Multi-Objective Optimization Problem (MOP)

A general MOP is formulated as:

$$\text{Minimize} \;\; \vec{F}(\vec{x}) = \left[ f_1(\vec{x}), f_2(\vec{x}), \dots, f_k(\vec{x}) \right]^T$$

subject to:
$$g_i(\vec{x}) \leq 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$
$$h_j(\vec{x}) = 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, p$$
$$x_l \leq x_l \leq x_u, \quad l = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where $\vec{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n) \in \mathcal{F} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **decision vector** and $\mathcal{F}$ is the **feasible region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Convention:** When $k=2$, the problem is a **bi-objective** problem. When $k \geq 3$, it is a **many-objective** problem. NSGA-II is typically applied for $k=2$ to $k=4$; for $k>4$, specialized algorithms like MOEA/D or reference-point methods are used.

### 2.1.2 Pareto Dominance (The "Better-Than" Relation)

A vector $\vec{u} = (u_1, u_2, \dots, u_k)$ is said to **dominate** vector $\vec{v} = (v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k)$ (denoted $\vec{u} \prec \vec{v}$) for a minimization problem if and only if:

$$\forall i \in \{1, \dots, k\} : u_i \leq v_i \quad \text{and} \quad \exists j \in \{1, \dots, k\} : u_j < v_j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** In single-objective optimization, comparing two solutions is trivial ($f(a) < f(b)$ or vice versa). With multiple objectives, a *partial order* is needed. **Pareto Dominance** is precisely this partial order — it is the engine that drives every MOEA.

### 2.1.3 Pareto Optimal Set (Decision Space)

The **Pareto Optimal Set** $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ is the set of all non-dominated feasible solutions:

$$\mathcal{P}^{*} = \left\{ \vec{x} \in \mathcal{F} \;\middle|\; \nexists \vec{x}' \in \mathcal{F} \text{ such that } \vec{F}(\vec{x}') \prec \vec{F}(\vec{x}) \right\}$$

### 2.1.4 Pareto Front (Objective Space)

The **Pareto Front** $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ is the image of the Pareto Optimal Set mapped into objective space:

$$\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \left\{ \vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1(\vec{x}), \dots, f_k(\vec{x})) \;\middle|\; \vec{x} \in \mathcal{P}^{*} \right\}$$

For a bi-objective problem ($k=2$), $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ is a **curve** in $\mathbb{R}^2$. For $k=3$, it is a **surface** in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

## 2.2 Strong vs. Weak Pareto Optimality (Examiner-Favorite Distinction)

| Type | Definition | Mathematical Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Strong Pareto Optimality** | No feasible solution dominates it in *all* objectives | $\nexists \vec{x}' : f_i(\vec{x}') < f_i(\vec{x}^{*}) \;\; \forall i$ |
| **Weak Pareto Optimality** | No feasible solution is strictly better in *all* objectives | $\nexists \vec{x}' : f_i(\vec{x}') < f_i(\vec{x}^{*}) \;\; \forall i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Every strong Pareto optimum is also weakly Pareto optimum, but the converse is not true. Weak Pareto optima may form a larger (and easier to compute) set, but they include dominated solutions under certain metric criteria.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | MOP objective vector | $\vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1, f_2, \dots, f_k)$ | $k$ objectives, $n$ decision variables |
| 2 | Pareto Dominance | $\vec{u} \prec \vec{v} \iff (u_i \leq v_i \;\; \forall i) \land (\exists j : u_j < v_j)$ | Partial order |
| 3 | Strict Dominance | $\vec{u} \prec\prec \vec{v} \iff u_i < v_i \;\; \forall i$ | Rarely achievable |
| 4 | Non-Dominance | $\vec{u} \parallel \vec{v} \iff \vec{u} \not\prec \vec{v} \land \vec{v} \not\prec \vec{u}$ | Incomparable trade-offs |
| 5 | Pareto Optimal Set | $\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F} \mid \nexists \vec{x}' : \vec{F}(\vec{x}') \prec \vec{F}(\vec{x})\}$ | Decision space |
| 6 | Pareto Front | $\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{\vec{F}(\vec{x}) \mid \vec{x} \in \mathcal{P}^{*}\}$ | Objective space |
| 7 | Generational Distance (GD) | $\text{GD} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P}^{*} \vert} \sqrt{\sum_{i} d_i^{2}}$ | Convergence metric |
| 8 | Spacing Metric | $S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{P}^{*} \vert - 1} \sum (d_i - \bar{d})^{2}}$ | Diversity metric |
| 9 | Hypervolume (HV) | $\text{HV} = \text{Vol}\left( \bigcup_{i} \mathcal{H}_i \right)$ | Both convergence + diversity |
| 10 | Crowding Distance (NSGA-II) | $d_i = \sum_{m=1}^{k} \frac{f_m^{i+1} - f_m^{i-1}}{f_m^{\max} - f_m^{\min}}$ | Selection pressure |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Pareto Optimality is used extensively in:

1. **Electrical Engineering:** Multi-objective antenna design (gain vs. bandwidth vs. SLL).
2. **Mechanical Engineering:** Lightweight structural design (mass vs. stiffness vs. cost).
3. **Civil Engineering:** Dam/bridge design (cost vs. safety factor vs. environmental impact).
4. **Computer Science:** Neural architecture search (accuracy vs. latency vs. model size).
5. **Economics/Finance:** Portfolio optimization (return vs. risk).

## 2.5 Crowding Distance — The NSGA-II Tie-Breaker

To maintain **diversity** along the Pareto Front, NSGA-II uses a **crowding distance** metric. For each non-dominated solution $i$, it sums the normalized distances to its immediate neighbors in each objective:

$$d_i = \sum_{m=1}^{k} \frac{f_m^{(i+1)} - f_m^{(i-1)}}{f_m^{\max} - f_m^{\min}}$$

Solutions with **higher crowding distance** are preferred during tournament selection, ensuring the algorithm explores sparse regions of the front and avoids premature convergence to a single point.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Identifying Pareto Optimal Solutions (Bi-Objective)

### Problem Statement

Consider a minimization problem with two objectives $f_1, f_2$. Evaluate the following six candidate solutions:

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| A | 4 | 4 |
| B | 3 | 5 |
| C | 2 | 6 |
| D | 5 | 2 |
| E | 4 | 3 |
| F | 6 | 1 |

**Task:** Identify the Pareto Optimal Set and the corresponding Pareto Front.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the dominance definition systematically.**

Two solutions $\vec{u} = (u_1, u_2)$ and $\vec{v} = (v_1, v_2)$ satisfy $\vec{u} \prec \vec{v}$ (minimization) iff:

$$u_1 \leq v_1 \;\;\text{AND}\;\; u_2 \leq v_2 \;\;\text{AND}\;\; (u_1 < v_1 \;\;\text{OR}\;\; u_2 < v_2)$$

**Step 2 — Compare every pair (we organize this via a dominance matrix).**

| vs. | A (4,4) | B (3,5) | C (2,6) | D (5,2) | E (4,3) | F (6,1) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A** | — | neither | neither | neither | neither | neither |
| **B** | neither | — | neither | neither | neither | neither |
| **C** | neither | neither | — | neither | neither | neither |
| **D** | neither | neither | neither | — | neither | neither |
| **E** | neither | neither | neither | neither | — | neither |
| **F** | neither | neither | neither | neither | neither | — |

Wait — the matrix shows no dominance, but we must check more carefully. Let us redo the comparison.

**Detailed pairwise check:**

- $A=(4,4)$ vs $B=(3,5)$: $A_1 > B_1$ (4 > 3) and $A_2 < B_2$ (4 < 5) → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $A=(4,4)$ vs $C=(2,6)$: $A_1 > C_1$, $A_2 < C_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $A=(4,4)$ vs $D=(5,2)$: $A_1 < D_1$, $A_2 > D_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $A=(4,4)$ vs $E=(4,3)$: $A_1 = E_1$, $A_2 > E_2$ → **E dominates A** ✓.
- $A=(4,4)$ vs $F=(6,1)$: $A_1 < F_1$, $A_2 > F_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $B=(3,5)$ vs $C=(2,6)$: $B_1 > C_1$, $B_2 < C_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $B=(3,5)$ vs $D=(5,2)$: $B_1 < D_1$, $B_2 > D_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $B=(3,5)$ vs $E=(4,3)$: $B_1 < E_1$, $B_2 > E_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $B=(3,5)$ vs $F=(6,1)$: $B_1 < F_1$, $B_2 > F_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $C=(2,6)$ vs $D=(5,2)$: $C_1 < D_1$, $C_2 > D_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $C=(2,6)$ vs $E=(4,3)$: $C_1 < E_1$, $C_2 > E_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $C=(2,6)$ vs $F=(6,1)$: $C_1 < F_1$, $C_2 > F_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $D=(5,2)$ vs $E=(4,3)$: $D_1 > E_1$, $D_2 < E_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $D=(5,2)$ vs $F=(6,1)$: $D_1 < F_1$, $D_2 > F_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).
- $E=(4,3)$ vs $F=(6,1)$: $E_1 < F_1$, $E_2 > F_2$ → **incomparable** ($\parallel$).

**Step 3 — Collect non-dominated solutions.**

The solutions that are **not** dominated by any other are: **B, C, D, E, F**.

Solution A is dominated by E.

**Step 4 — State the Pareto Optimal Set and Pareto Front.**

$$\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{B, C, D, E, F\}$$

$$\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{(3,5), (2,6), (5,2), (4,3), (6,1)\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ contains solutions like $(2,6), (3,5), (4,3), (5,2), (6,1)$, the points form a **monotonically decreasing curve** — this is the typical signature of a well-formed Pareto Front for a bi-objective minimization problem with conflicting objectives.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Derivation of Crowding Distance

**Problem:** Given the Pareto Front $\{A=(1,8), B=(2,5), C=(3,3), D=(5,2), E=(7,1)\}$ (already sorted in $f_1$), compute the crowding distance for solution $C$.

**Step 1 — Sort the solutions by $f_1$ (ascending).**

$$\text{Order: } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow D \rightarrow E$$

**Step 2 — Identify neighbors of $C$.**

Left neighbor: $B = (2, 5)$, Right neighbor: $D = (5, 2)$.

**Step 3 — Compute the normalized objective differences for $f_1$ and $f_2$.**

For $f_1$:

$$\Delta f_1 = \frac{f_1(D) - f_1(B)}{f_1^{\max} - f_1^{\min}} = \frac{5 - 2}{7 - 1} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.5$$

For $f_2$:

$$\Delta f_2 = \frac{f_2(B) - f_2(D)}{f_2^{\max} - f_2^{\min}} = \frac{5 - 2}{8 - 1} = \frac{3}{7} \approx 0.4286$$

**Step 4 — Sum to obtain the crowding distance.**

$$d_C = \Delta f_1 + \Delta f_2 = 0.5 + 0.4286 = 0.9286$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

A crowding distance of $d_C \approx 0.93$ is **large**, meaning $C$ sits in a sparse region of the Pareto Front. NSGA-II will preferentially keep $C$ in the next generation, preserving diversity.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Hypervolume Calculation (2D, Bi-Objective)

**Problem:** Compute the hypervolume of the Pareto Front $\mathcal{PF} = \{(1,5), (3,3), (5,1)\}$ with reference point $R = (6,6)$.

**Step 1 — Visualize the dominated rectangles.**

Each solution $P_i = (x_i, y_i)$ dominates the rectangle from $(x_i, y_i)$ to $R = (6,6)$.

Area of rectangle for $P_1 = (1,5)$:

$$A_1 = (6 - 1) \times (6 - 5) = 5 \times 1 = 5$$

Area of rectangle for $P_2 = (3,3)$:

$$A_2 = (6 - 3) \times (6 - 3) = 3 \times 3 = 9$$

Area of rectangle for $P_3 = (5,1)$:

$$A_3 = (6 - 5) \times (6 - 1) = 1 \times 5 = 5$$

**Step 2 — Take the union (note that $P_1, P_2, P_3$ are mutually non-dominating, so rectangles do not overlap).**

$$\text{HV} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 = 5 + 9 + 5 = 19$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

A larger hypervolume indicates a Pareto Front that is both well-converged (close to true front) and well-distributed (good spread). Hypervolume is the **only quality indicator** that is strictly monotonic with respect to Pareto dominance — a strict improvement in the front guarantees a non-decrease in HV.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Pareto Optimality. Explain the concept of Pareto Dominance with a suitable example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Board-Expected Format):

**Definition (1 Mark):** A solution $\vec{x}^{*} \in \mathcal{F}$ is called **Pareto Optimal** if there exists no other feasible solution $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ that dominates it — i.e., no solution can improve one objective without worsening at least one other.

**Pareto Dominance (1 Mark):** For a minimization problem with $k$ objectives, a vector $\vec{u} = (u_1, \dots, u_k)$ **dominates** $\vec{v} = (v_1, \dots, v_k)$, denoted $\vec{u} \prec \vec{v}$, if and only if:

$$u_i \leq v_i \;\; \forall i \in \{1, \dots, k\} \quad \text{and} \quad \exists j \in \{1, \dots, k\} : u_j < v_j$$

**Example (1 Mark):** Consider $A = (3, 5)$ and $B = (4, 6)$. Since $3 \leq 4$ and $5 \leq 6$ with at least one strict inequality, $A$ dominates $B$. However, $A = (3, 5)$ and $C = (4, 4)$ are **incomparable** (denoted $A \parallel C$) because $A$ is better in $f_1$ but worse in $f_2$.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is the difference between Pareto Optimal Set and Pareto Front?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Pareto Optimal Set ($\mathcal{P}^{*}$) | Pareto Front ($\mathcal{PF}^{*}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Decision space ($\mathbb{R}^n$) | Objective space ($\mathbb{R}^k$) |
| **Elements** | Decision vectors $\vec{x}$ | Objective vectors $\vec{F}(\vec{x})$ |
| **Mapping** | Source set | Image of $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ under $\vec{F}$ |
| **Visualization** | Hard to plot directly | Plottable curve/surface |

**Mathematical Relation (1 Mark):**

$$\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{ \vec{F}(\vec{x}) \mid \vec{x} \in \mathcal{P}^{*} \}$$

That is, the Pareto Front is obtained by **applying the objective function** to every element of the Pareto Optimal Set.
