## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** must be provided between two completely independent question sets.

---

### Question B.1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] — **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Pareto Optimality in multi-objective optimization. Differentiate between **strong** and **weak** Pareto Optimality with mathematical conditions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following six solutions in a bi-objective minimization problem. Construct the Pareto Front. Show all dominance comparisons.

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $S_1$ | 1 | 6 |
| $S_2$ | 2 | 4 |
| $S_3$ | 3 | 3 |
| $S_4$ | 4 | 2 |
| $S_5$ | 5 | 4 |
| $S_6$ | 2 | 5 |

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Concept of Pareto Optimality (3 Marks):**

In multi-objective optimization problems (MOPs), we seek to simultaneously optimize $k \geq 2$ conflicting objective functions. Unlike single-objective problems, no single "best" solution exists — instead, we obtain a **set of trade-off solutions**.

A solution $\vec{x}^{*}$ is **Pareto Optimal** if no other feasible solution $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ can be found such that $\vec{x}$ improves all objectives simultaneously compared to $\vec{x}^{*}$.

The set of all such optimal solutions is the **Pareto Optimal Set** $\mathcal{P}^{*}$, and its image in objective space is the **Pareto Front** $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$.

**Strong vs Weak Pareto Optimality (4 Marks):**

- **Strong Pareto Optimality:** A solution $\vec{x}^{*}$ is **strongly Pareto optimal** if there is no $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ such that $f_i(\vec{x}) < f_i(\vec{x}^{*})$ for **all** $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$. Formally:

$$\nexists \vec{x} \in \mathcal{F} : f_i(\vec{x}) < f_i(\vec{x}^{*}) \;\; \forall i \in \{1, \dots, k\}$$

- **Weak Pareto Optimality:** A solution $\vec{x}^{*}$ is **weakly Pareto optimal** if there is no $\vec{x} \in \mathcal{F}$ such that $f_i(\vec{x}) < f_i(\vec{x}^{*})$ for **all** $i$. Formally:

$$\nexists \vec{x} \in \mathcal{F} : f_i(\vec{x}) < f_i(\vec{x}^{*}) \;\; \forall i \in \{1, \dots, k\}$$

Wait — note that the *textual definitions* of strong and weak optimality above are subtly different in their practical usage. Let us clarify the **standard KTU distinction** more rigorously:

- **Strong Pareto Optimality:** No feasible point strictly dominates $\vec{x}^{*}$ in *every* objective. $\vec{x}^{*}$ lies on the *true* Pareto Front.
- **Weak Pareto Optimality:** No feasible point is *strictly better* in *all* objectives. $\vec{x}^{*}$ lies on the *weak* Pareto Front (which contains the strong front as a subset and may also include boundary-dominated points).

| Type | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| Strong | Real engineering preference; sharp trade-off curve |
| Weak | Easier to compute; useful as algorithmic intermediate |

**Conclusion:** Every strong Pareto optimum is weakly Pareto optimal, but not vice versa. Algorithms often report weak fronts and then refine them.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply dominance test to all pairs.**

| Comparison | Result | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| $S_1 = (1,6)$ vs $S_2 = (2,4)$ | $S_1 \parallel S_2$ | $S_1$ better in $f_1$, $S_2$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_1 = (1,6)$ vs $S_3 = (3,3)$ | $S_1 \parallel S_3$ | $S_1$ better in $f_1$, $S_3$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_1 = (1,6)$ vs $S_4 = (4,2)$ | $S_1 \parallel S_4$ | $S_1$ better in $f_1$, $S_4$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_1 = (1,6)$ vs $S_5 = (5,4)$ | $S_1 \parallel S_5$ | $S_1$ better in $f_1$, $S_5$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_1 = (1,6)$ vs $S_6 = (2,5)$ | $S_1 \parallel S_6$ | $S_1$ better in $f_1$, $S_6$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_2 = (2,4)$ vs $S_3 = (3,3)$ | $S_2 \parallel S_3$ | $S_2$ better in $f_1$, $S_3$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_2 = (2,4)$ vs $S_4 = (4,2)$ | $S_2 \parallel S_4$ | $S_2$ better in $f_1$, $S_4$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_2 = (2,4)$ vs $S_5 = (5,4)$ | $S_2 \prec S_5$ | $2<5$ and $4=4$ (not strict in both, but at least one strict) ✓ $S_2$ dominates $S_5$? Recheck: $S_2_1=2 \leq S_5_1=5$ yes, $S_2_2=4 \leq S_5_2=4$ yes with strict in $f_1$ → $S_2$ **dominates** $S_5$ |
| $S_2 = (2,4)$ vs $S_6 = (2,5)$ | $S_2 \prec S_6$ | $2=2$, $4<5$ → $S_2$ **dominates** $S_6$ |
| $S_3 = (3,3)$ vs $S_4 = (4,2)$ | $S_3 \parallel S_4$ | $S_3$ better in $f_1$, $S_4$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_3 = (3,3)$ vs $S_5 = (5,4)$ | $S_3 \prec S_5$ | $3<5$, $3<4$ → $S_3$ **dominates** $S_5$ |
| $S_3 = (3,3)$ vs $S_6 = (2,5)$ | $S_6 \parallel S_3$ | $S_6$ better in $f_1$, $S_3$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_4 = (4,2)$ vs $S_5 = (5,4)$ | $S_4 \prec S_5$ | $4<5$, $2<4$ → $S_4$ **dominates** $S_5$ |
| $S_4 = (4,2)$ vs $S_6 = (2,5)$ | $S_4 \parallel S_6$ | $S_6$ better in $f_1$, $S_4$ better in $f_2$ |
| $S_5 = (5,4)$ vs $S_6 = (2,5)$ | $S_6 \parallel S_5$ | $S_6$ better in $f_1$, $S_5$ better in $f_2$ |

**Step 2 — Identify non-dominated solutions.**

Dominated solutions: $S_5$ (by $S_2, S_3, S_4$), $S_6$ (by $S_2$).

Non-dominated (Pareto Optimal Set):

$$\mathcal{P}^{*} = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$$

**Step 3 — State the Pareto Front.**

$$\mathcal{PF}^{*} = \{(1,6), (2,4), (3,3), (4,2)\}$$

---

### Question B.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] — **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Internal Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the working of NSGA-II. How is the concept of Pareto Optimality used inside NSGA-II for ranking and selection? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Crowding Distance** for the following Pareto Front (sorted in $f_1$ ascending) in a bi-objective minimization problem: $A = (1, 9), B = (2, 5), C = (3, 3), D = (4, 2), E = (5, 1)$. Which solution has the highest diversity preservation? **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Working of NSGA-II (3 Marks):**

**NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II)**, proposed by **Deb et al. (2002)**, is the most widely used multi-objective evolutionary algorithm. Its procedure is:

1. Initialize a random parent population $P_0$ of size $N$.
2. Evaluate the objective functions for each individual.
3. Perform **Non-Dominated Sorting** to assign ranks (Front 0, Front 1, …).
4. Compute **Crowding Distance** for each individual.
5. Use **Binary Tournament Selection** (based on rank first, then crowding distance) to select parents.
6. Apply **SBX Crossover** and **Polynomial Mutation** to generate offspring $Q_t$.
7. Combine $P_t$ and $Q_t$ to form $R_t$ of size $2N$.
8. Select the best $N$ individuals from $R_t$ to form $P_{t+1}$ (**Elitist Strategy**).
9. Repeat until termination.

**Use of Pareto Optimality in Ranking (2 Marks):**

Pareto Optimality forms the basis of **Non-Dominated Sorting**:
- Solutions that are not dominated by any other are placed in **Front 0** (best rank).
- After removing Front 0, the next set of non-dominated solutions is **Front 1**.
- This continues until every solution is ranked.

**Use of Pareto Optimality in Selection (2 Marks):**

Between two solutions:
- The one with the **lower (better) rank** is chosen.
- If ranks are equal, the one with **higher crowding distance** (more diverse) is chosen.

This ensures that the algorithm converges toward the true Pareto Front **and** maintains a well-spread distribution of solutions along it.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Sort by $f_1$ (already sorted):**

$$\text{Order: } A(1,9) \rightarrow B(2,5) \rightarrow C(3,3) \rightarrow D(4,2) \rightarrow E(5,1)$$

**Step 2 — Boundary points receive infinite crowding distance:**

$$d_A = \infty, \quad d_E = \infty$$

**Step 3 — Compute crowding distance for $B$:**

Neighbors: $A = (1,9)$ and $C = (3,3)$.

For $f_1$:

$$\Delta f_1 = \frac{f_1(C) - f_1(A)}{f_1^{\max} - f_1^{\min}} = \frac{3 - 1}{5 - 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

For $f_2$:

$$\Delta f_2 = \frac{f_2(A) - f_2(C)}{f_2^{\max} - f_2^{\min}} = \frac{9 - 3}{9 - 1} = \frac{6}{8} = 0.75$$

$$d_B = 0.5 + 0.75 = 1.25$$

**Step 4 — Compute crowding distance for $C$:**

Neighbors: $B = (2,5)$ and $D = (4,2)$.

For $f_1$:

$$\Delta f_1 = \frac{4 - 2}{5 - 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

For $f_2$:

$$\Delta f_2 = \frac{5 - 2}{9 - 1} = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375$$

$$d_C = 0.5 + 0.375 = 0.875$$

**Step 5 — Compute crowding distance for $D$:**

Neighbors: $C = (3,3)$ and $E = (5,1)$.

For $f_1$:

$$\Delta f_1 = \frac{5 - 3}{5 - 1} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

For $f_2$:

$$\Delta f_2 = \frac{3 - 1}{9 - 1} = \frac{2}{8} = 0.25$$

$$d_D = 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75$$

**Step 6 — Summary table and conclusion.**

| Solution | Crowding Distance |
|:---:|:---:|
| A | $\infty$ |
| B | **1.25** (highest finite) |
| C | 0.875 |
| D | 0.75 |
| E | $\infty$ |

**Conclusion:** Solution $B = (2,5)$ has the highest *finite* crowding distance, indicating it lies in the **sparsest region** of the front. NSGA-II will preferentially retain $B$ to maintain diversity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Compiled from Board Exam Trends):**
>
> 1. **Incomplete Dominance Check (–2 marks):** Students often check *only one* objective for dominance. **Always check all $k$ objectives** before declaring dominance.
>
> 2. **Confusing $\leq$ with $<$ (–1 to –2 marks):** Pareto Dominance requires *at least one strict* inequality. If $u_i = v_i$ for all $i$, then $u$ and $v$ are *equal*, not dominant.
>
> 3. **Mixing Decision and Objective Spaces (–1 mark):** $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ lives in **decision space** ($\vec{x}$), $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$ lives in **objective space** ($\vec{F}(\vec{x})$). Examiners are strict about this distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Boundary Points Have $\infty$ Crowding Distance (–1 mark):** Endpoints of a sorted front always get infinite crowding distance. Missing this loses marks in NSGA-II questions.
>
> 5. **Treating Pareto Optimality as a *Single* Solution (–3 marks):** Pareto Optimality is a *set* of solutions, not one. Saying "the Pareto optimal solution is…" is a conceptual error.
>
> 6. **Confusing Maximization and Minimization Conventions (–1 to –2 marks):** In maximization, dominance flips: $u_i \geq v_i$ for all $i$ with at least one strict. Always **state the convention** at the top of your answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pareto Optimality (Module 4)**

- [x] **Pareto Optimality** = a state where no objective can be improved without worsening another.
- [x] **MOP** = minimize $\vec{F}(\vec{x}) = (f_1, \dots, f_k)$ subject to constraints, $k \geq 2$.
- [x] **Pareto Dominance** ($\vec{u} \prec \vec{v}$, minimization): $u_i \leq v_i$ for all $i$, with at least one strict.
- [x] **Non-Dominance / Incomparability** ($\vec{u} \parallel \vec{v}$): neither dominates the other.
- [x] **Pareto Optimal Set** $\mathcal{P}^{*}$: all non-dominated feasible solutions in decision space.
- [x] **Pareto Front** $\mathcal{PF}^{*}$: image of $\mathcal{P}^{*}$ in objective space (curve for $k=2$, surface for $k=3$).
- [x] **Strong Optimality** = lies on the true (sharp) front; **Weak Optimality** = lies on a possibly inflated front.
- [x] **NSGA-II** = uses Pareto-based non-dominated sorting + crowding distance; proposed by Deb et al. (2002).
- [x] **Crowding Distance** = sum of normalized neighbor distances per objective; higher ⇒ more diversity.
- [x] **Hypervolume (HV)** = volume of objective space dominated by the front w.r.t. a reference point; the only strictly Pareto-monotonic quality indicator.
- [x] **Generational Distance (GD)** = measures *convergence* to the true front.
- [x] **Spacing Metric** = measures *uniformity* of spread along the front.
- [x] **Real-world applications** = antenna design, structural optimization, portfolio management, neural architecture search.
- [x] **Board Traps**: always check ALL objectives; never call it a "single solution"; distinguish decision vs. objective space; remember the $\leq$-with-one-strict rule.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Procedure for Finding a Non-Dominated Set
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Procedure for Finding a Non-Dominated Set

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Multi-Objective Optimization (MOO)**, when two or more objective functions must be optimized simultaneously, the concept of a single "best" solution no longer exists. Instead, we seek a set of **Pareto-optimal** trade-off solutions. A solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is said to **dominate** another solution $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ (written as $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$) if and only if:

1. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **no worse** than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in **all** objectives, and
2. $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is **strictly better** than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in **at least one** objective.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Dominated Set (Pareto Front)**: The set of all solutions in the decision space that are **not dominated** by any other solution in the feasible space is called the **Pareto-optimal set**. The corresponding set of objective vectors in the objective space is called the **Pareto front** $PF^*$.

For a minimization problem with $M$ objectives:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)} \iff \left(\forall i \in \{1,\dots,M\}: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})\right) \land \left(\exists j \in \{1,\dots,M\}: f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The procedure for finding the non-dominated set is the **cornerstone** of every Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithm (MOEA) such as **NSGA-II**, **SPEA2**, and **MOEA/D**. It allows the algorithm to identify and preserve the "best trade-off" solutions across generations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student choosing between two colleges — **College A** and **College B**:

| Criterion | College A | College B |
|---|---|---|
| Placement Record (higher is better) | 9 / 10 | 7 / 10 |
| Fee (lower is better) | ₹8 Lakh | ₹5 Lakh |
| Campus Life (higher is better) | 6 / 10 | 9 / 10 |

- **College A** dominates in placements, but is expensive.
- **College B** dominates in fee and campus life, but weaker placements.

**Neither college dominates the other overall** — they lie on the same *Pareto front*. Choosing one over the other depends on **your personal weight (preference)**. The non-dominated set is exactly this list of "equally meritorious" options where no single option is universally better.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold Highlights)

- **Pareto Optimality**: State of a solution where no other feasible solution improves one objective without worsening another.
- **Dominance Relation ($\prec$)**: A partial-ordering operator comparing two solutions.
- **Pareto Front ($PF^*$)**: The image of the Pareto-optimal set in objective space.
- **Crowding Distance**: A diversity-preservation metric (used in NSGA-II).
- **Non-Dominated Sorting**: Algorithmic procedure that ranks solutions into successive Pareto fronts.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Procedure

Consider a general multi-objective minimization problem:

$$\min_{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S}} \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \left[f_1(\mathbf{x}),\; f_2(\mathbf{x}),\; \dots,\; f_M(\mathbf{x})\right]^T$$

where:
- $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ is the **decision vector**.
- $\mathcal{S} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is the **feasible decision space**.
- $\mathbf{F}: \mathcal{S} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^M$ maps $\mathbf{x}$ to the **objective space** $\mathcal{Z} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^M$.

### 2.2 Operational Steps to Find the Non-Dominated Set

The procedure follows a structured **pairwise dominance-comparison algorithm**:

- **Step 1 — Population Initialization**: Generate or sample a population $P = \{\mathbf{x}^{(1)}, \mathbf{x}^{(2)}, \dots, \mathbf{x}^{(N)}\}$ of size $N$ inside the feasible space $\mathcal{S}$.
- **Step 2 — Objective Evaluation**: For each individual, compute the $M$-dimensional objective vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}) = (f_1^{(i)}, f_2^{(i)}, \dots, f_M^{(i)})$.
- **Step 3 — Pairwise Dominance Check**: For every pair $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$, test whether $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(j)}$ using the strict-inequality definition.
- **Step 4 — Domination Count Assignment**: For each solution $j$, compute its **domination count** $n_j = \vert \{ i : \mathbf{x}^{(i)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(j)} \} \vert$, the number of solutions dominating it.
- **Step 5 — Front 1 Extraction**: All solutions with $n_j = 0$ form the **first non-dominated front** $\mathcal{F}_1$ (the current Pareto front).
- **Step 6 — Front Removal**: Remove $\mathcal{F}_1$ from the population and decrement the domination count of every solution that was dominated by a member of $\mathcal{F}_1$.
- **Step 7 — Iterate**: Repeat Steps 4–6 to extract successive fronts $\mathcal{F}_2, \mathcal{F}_3, \dots$ until the population is empty.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **time complexity** of the naïve non-dominated sorting procedure is $O(M N^2)$ per generation. NSGA-II improves this to $O(M N^2)$ worst case but $O(N \sqrt{M} \log^{M-1} N)$ on average through a clever "dominance-tree" data structure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form / Condition** | **Notation** | **Engineering Utility** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weak Domination | $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ | $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \preceq \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ | Used in archiving, when objective noise exists |
| Strong Domination | $\forall i: f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) \leq f_i(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ AND $\exists j: f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(1)}) < f_j(\mathbf{x}^{(2)})$ | $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ | Standard Pareto ordering in MOEAs |
| Domination Count | $n_j = \sum_{i \neq j} \mathbb{1}\left[\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(j)}\right]$ | $n_j \in \{0, 1, \dots, N-1\}$ | Ranking criterion for front assignment |
| Pareto Optimality | $\nexists \mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{S}: \mathbf{x}' \prec \mathbf{x}$ | $\mathbf{x} \in PS^*$ | Defines the optimal set |
| Ideal Point | $z_i^* = \min_{\mathbf{x} \in PS^*} f_i(\mathbf{x})$ | $\mathbf{z}^* \in \mathbb{R}^M$ | Reference for hypervolume calculation |
| Crowding Distance | $d_i = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \frac{f_m^{(i+1)} - f_m^{(i-1)}}{f_m^{\max} - f_m^{\min}}$ | $d_i \geq 0$ | Diversity metric in NSGA-II |
| Hypervolume (HV) | $HV = \Lambda\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\vert PS^* \vert} [z_i, \mathbf{r}]\right)$ | $HV \in \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ | Quality indicator for Pareto front |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "$\vert x \vert$" Notation is Risky in Markdown**: I have used `\vert` / explicit `n_j` rather than vertical pipes inside table cells to prevent markdown-table column-splitting errors during PDF rendering by KTU's automated conversion pipeline.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The procedure is fundamental to:
- **Aerospace Design**: Optimizing lift, drag, and fuel burn simultaneously.
- **VLSI Chip Design**: Minimizing power, delay, and area.
- **Structural Engineering**: Weight vs. stiffness vs. cost trade-offs.
- **Machine Learning Hyperparameter Tuning**: Accuracy vs. model size vs. inference time.
- **Smart Grid Optimization**: Cost vs. emissions vs. reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (Hand-Calculation Style)

Consider the **minimization** of two objectives $f_1, f_2$ for **6 candidate solutions** in objective space:

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | 2 | 8 |
| B | 4 | 4 |
| C | 6 | 2 |
| D | 3 | 6 |
| E | 5 | 5 |
| F | 8 | 1 |

We will apply the dominance-comparison procedure to find the non-dominated set.

#### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Dominance Comparisons

Compare every ordered pair $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$:

- **A vs B**: $f_1$: $2 < 4$ ✓, $f_2$: $8 > 4$ ✗. Mixed → **No dominance**.
- **A vs C**: $f_1$: $2 < 6$ ✓, $f_2$: $8 > 2$ ✗. Mixed → **No dominance**.
- **A vs D**: $f_1$: $2 < 3$ ✓, $f_2$: $8 > 6$ ✗. Mixed → **No dominance**.
- **A vs E**: $f_1$: $2 < 5$ ✓, $f_2$: $8 > 5$ ✗. Mixed → **No dominance**.
- **A vs F**: $f_1$: $2 < 8$ ✓, $f_2$: $8 > 1$ ✗. Mixed → **No dominance**.

- **B vs A**: $f_1$: $4 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **B vs C**: $f_1$: $4 < 6$ ✓, $f_2$: $4 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **B vs D**: $f_1$: $4 > 3$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **B vs E**: $f_1$: $4 < 5$ ✓, $f_2$: $4 < 5$ ✓, strict ✓. → **B dominates E** ($B \prec E$).
- **B vs F**: $f_1$: $4 < 8$ ✓, $f_2$: $4 > 1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

- **C vs A**: $f_1$: $6 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **C vs B**: $f_1$: $6 > 4$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **C vs D**: $f_1$: $6 > 3$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **C vs E**: $f_1$: $6 > 5$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **C vs F**: $f_1$: $6 < 8$ ✓, $f_2$: $2 > 1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

- **D vs A**: $f_1$: $3 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **D vs B**: $f_1$: $3 < 4$ ✓, $f_2$: $6 > 4$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **D vs C**: $f_1$: $3 < 6$ ✓, $f_2$: $6 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **D vs E**: $f_1$: $3 < 5$ ✓, $f_2$: $6 > 5$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **D vs F**: $f_1$: $3 < 8$ ✓, $f_2$: $6 > 1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

- **E vs A,B,C,D**: All $f_1$ relations favourable but $f_2$ for E is worse than A,B,C,D — verified above.
- **E vs F**: $f_1$: $5 < 8$ ✓, $f_2$: $5 > 1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

- **F vs A**: $f_1$: $8 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **F vs B**: $f_1$: $8 > 4$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **F vs C**: $f_1$: $8 > 6$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **F vs D**: $f_1$: $8 > 3$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- **F vs E**: $f_1$: $8 > 5$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

#### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Build Domination Count Table

| Solution | Dominated by | $n_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | **0** |
| B | — | **0** |
| C | — | **0** |
| D | — | **0** |
| E | B | **1** |
| F | — | **0** |

> Wait — re-examining F: F has $(f_1=8, f_2=1)$. None of A,B,C,D,E strictly beat it on both. So F is **also non-dominated**.

So $n_F = 0$. The corrected table:

| Solution | Dominated by | $n_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| A | — | **0** |
| B | — | **0** |
| C | — | **0** |
| D | — | **0** |
| E | B | **1** |
| F | — | **0** |

#### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Extract the Non-Dominated Set (Front 1)

Solutions with $n_j = 0$: $\{A, B, C, D, F\}$.

$$\mathcal{F}_1 = \{A, B, C, D, F\} \quad \text{(5 solutions on the Pareto front)}$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Identify the Dominated Solution

Only $E$ has $n_j > 0$, so:

$$\mathcal{F}_2 = \{E\}$$

### 3.2 Mathematical Justification

The dominance operator $\prec$ is a **strict partial order** because it satisfies:

- **Irreflexivity**: $\nexists \mathbf{x}: \mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{x}$ (a solution cannot dominate itself).
- **Asymmetry**: If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \not\prec \mathbf{x}^{(1)}$.
- **Transitivity**: If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$.

Formally, the Pareto set is defined as:

$$PS^* = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S} : \nexists \mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{S},\; \mathbf{x}' \prec \mathbf{x} \}$$

The corresponding Pareto front in objective space is:

$$PF^* = \{ \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbf{x} \in PS^* \} \subseteq \mathcal{Z}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Outcomes in MOO

| **Scenario** | **Outcome** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|
| All $M$ objectives improved | Strict Dominance | Discard dominated solution |
| One better, one equal | Weak Dominance | Discard (in conventional sense) |
| Some better, some worse | Non-Dominated | Preserve on Pareto front |
| All equal | Incomparable (but identical) | Merge / keep one |
| $n_j = 0$ for all $N$ | All are non-dominated | Trivially full front |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Procedural Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Population P of size N] --> B[Evaluate objective vectors Fx for each x in P]
    B --> C[Initialize domination count nj = 0 for all j]
    C --> D{For every pair i, j with i != j}
    D --> E{Check if x_i dominates x_j}
    E -->|Yes| F[Increment nj]
    E -->|No| G[Continue pairing]
    F --> G
    G --> H{All pairs compared?}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I[Extract Front F1: all x with nj = 0]
    I --> J[Store F1 as current Pareto front]
    J --> K[Remove F1 from population P]
    K --> L[Decrement nk for solutions k that were dominated by F1 members]
    L --> M{Population empty?}
    M -->|No| I
    M -->|Yes| N[Combine all fronts into final Pareto set]
    N --> O[End: Return PS star and PF star]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Pareto Extraction Subsystem)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer [INPUT LAYER]
        A1[Population Buffer]
        A2[Objective Function Evaluator]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer [COMPUTE LAYER]
        B1[Pairwise Comparator]
        B2[Domination Count Register]
        B3[Front Ranker]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer [OUTPUT LAYER]
        C1[Pareto Front F1]
        C2[Pareto Front F2]
        C3[Archive Manager]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Pareto Front Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ObjectiveSpace [OBJECTIVE SPACE 2D]
        P1[Solution A: low f1, high f2] -.->|Non-dominated| P3
        P2[Solution B: balanced] -.->|Non-dominated| P3
        P4[Solution C: high f1, low f2] -.->|Non-dominated| P3
        P5[Solution D: mid f1, mid f2 dominated by B]:::dominated
        P3[Pareto Front Curve]
    end

    classDef dominated fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Working Example (Numerical Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Solution A 2,8] -->|non-dom| PF[Pareto Front]
    B[Solution B 4,4] -->|non-dom| PF
    C[Solution C 6,2] -->|non-dom| PF
    D[Solution D 3,6] -->|non-dom| PF
    E[Solution E 5,5] -->|dominated by B| X[Discard]
    F[Solution F 8,1] -->|non-dom| PF
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define dominance and non-dominance in multi-objective optimization. Give a formal example with two objectives.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ **dominates** $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ ($\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$) if it is no worse in all $M$ objectives and strictly better in at least one. A **non-dominated** solution is one that is not dominated by any other feasible solution. For example, with $M=2$ minimization objectives, $A=(2, 8)$ does not dominate $B=(4, 4)$ because $f_1$ improves but $f_2$ worsens. The set of all such non-dominated solutions forms the **Pareto front**.

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the formal definition of a Pareto-optimal set $PS^*$ and Pareto front $PF^*$.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Pareto-optimal set** is $PS^* = \{\mathbf{x} \in \mathcal{S} : \nexists \mathbf{x}' \in \mathcal{S}, \mathbf{x}' \prec \mathbf{x}\}$.
> The **Pareto front** is its image in objective space: $PF^* = \{\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) : \mathbf{x} \in PS^*\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^M$.
> $PS^*$ contains all trade-off solutions; $PF^*$ is the curve/surface of corresponding objective vectors.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step procedure to find the non-dominated set from a given population of solutions in a multi-objective optimization problem. Mention the role of the domination count. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the following 5 solutions of a minimization problem with two objectives, identify the Pareto-optimal set and the dominated solutions. Show all dominance comparisons explicitly. **(7 marks)**

| Solution | $f_1$ | $f_2$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 5 |
| $P_2$ | 3 | 3 |
| $P_3$ | 5 | 2 |
| $P_4$ | 2 | 4 |
| $P_5$ | 4 | 4 |

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The procedure to extract the non-dominated set has seven well-defined steps:

1. **Initialize** a population $P$ of $N$ candidate solutions within the feasible region $\mathcal{S}$.
2. **Evaluate** the objective vector $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}^{(i)})$ for every individual $i \in \{1, \dots, N\}$.
3. **Compare** every ordered pair $(i, j)$ with $i \neq j$ to determine if $\mathbf{x}^{(i)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(j)}$.
4. **Compute** the **domination count** $n_j$ for each solution $j$, defined as the number of solutions that strictly dominate it.
5. **Extract Front 1**: All solutions with $n_j = 0$ constitute the first non-dominated front $\mathcal{F}_1$.
6. **Decrement** the domination count of every solution that was dominated by a member of $\mathcal{F}_1$, and remove $\mathcal{F}_1$ from $P$.
7. **Iterate** Steps 5–6 to extract $\mathcal{F}_2, \mathcal{F}_3, \dots$ until $P$ is empty.

**Role of the domination count**: It serves as a **ranking criterion**. Solutions with $n_j = 0$ are top-ranked and form the Pareto front; $n_j = 1$ means a single solution dominates it (second-rank), and so on. This scalarization via $n_j$ enables the algorithm to perform selection pressure without converting MOO into a single-objective scalar form.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Pairwise dominance comparisons (minimization, $M=2$):**

- $P_1(1, 5)$ vs $P_2(3, 3)$: $f_1$: $1 < 3$ ✓, $f_2$: $5 > 3$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_1$ vs $P_3(5, 2)$: $f_1$: $1 < 5$ ✓, $f_2$: $5 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_1$ vs $P_4(2, 4)$: $f_1$: $1 < 2$ ✓, $f_2$: $5 > 4$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_1$ vs $P_5(4, 4)$: $f_1$: $1 < 4$ ✓, $f_2$: $5 > 4$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_2$ vs $P_1$: $f_1$: $3 > 1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_2$ vs $P_3$: $f_1$: $3 < 5$ ✓, $f_2$: $3 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_2$ vs $P_4(2, 4)$: $f_1$: $3 > 2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $P_2$ vs $P_5(4, 4)$: $f_1$: $3 < 4$ ✓, $f_2$: $3 < 4$ ✓, strict ✓ → **$P_2$ dominates $P_5$** ($P_2 \prec P_5$).
- $P_3$ vs $P_1, P_2, P_4, P_5$: $f_1$ never strictly better → **No dominance from $P_3$**.
- $P_4$ vs $P_5(4, 4)$: $f_1$: $2 < 4$ ✓, $f_2$: $4 = 4$ (equal, not strict) → **No strict dominance** (weak only).
- $P_5$ vs $P_2$: Already covered — $P_2 \prec P_5$.

**Domination count table:**

| Solution | Dominated By | $n_j$ |
|---|---|---|
| $P_1$ | — | **0** |
| $P_2$ | — | **0** |
| $P_3$ | — | **0** |
| $P_4$ | — | **0** |
| $P_5$ | $P_2$ | **1** |

**Result:**

- **Pareto-optimal set (Front 1)**: $PS^* = \{P_1, P_2, P_3, P_4\}$
- **Dominated solution (Front 2)**: $\{P_5\}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define dominance, weak dominance, and strict dominance relations. State the three properties (irreflexivity, asymmetry, transitivity) that make dominance a strict partial order. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing process has three design variables producing two objectives (to be **minimized**). The population has 4 solutions: $S_1 = (3, 7)$, $S_2 = (5, 5)$, $S_3 = (6, 3)$, $S_4 = (4, 6)$. Identify the non-dominated set. Which solutions would be discarded? Compute the crowding distances of the Pareto-front solutions, assuming normalized objectives. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

- **Weak Domination** ($\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \preceq \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$): $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is no worse than $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ in **every** objective (at least one strict inequality required for weak-only, or no strict at all for the loose definition).
- **Strong / Strict Domination** ($\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$): $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ is no worse in all objectives AND strictly better in **at least one** objective.

**Three properties of strict partial order:**

1. **Irreflexivity**: $\nexists \mathbf{x}$ such that $\mathbf{x} \prec \mathbf{x}$. A solution cannot dominate itself.
2. **Asymmetry**: If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \not\prec \mathbf{x}^{(1)}$.
3. **Transitivity**: If $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ and $\mathbf{x}^{(2)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$, then $\mathbf{x}^{(1)} \prec \mathbf{x}^{(3)}$.

These properties guarantee a well-defined ranking and a unique top-most non-dominated front.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Dominance comparisons** ($M=2$, minimization):

- $S_1(3,7)$ vs $S_2(5,5)$: $f_1$ ✓ better, $f_2$ ✗ worse → **No dominance**.
- $S_1$ vs $S_3(6,3)$: $f_1$ ✓, $f_2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $S_1$ vs $S_4(4,6)$: $f_1$ ✓, $f_2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $S_2(5,5)$ vs $S_3(6,3)$: $f_1$ ✓, $f_2$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $S_2$ vs $S_4(4,6)$: $f_1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.
- $S_3$ vs $S_4(4,6)$: $f_1$ ✗ → **No dominance**.

**Domination count:**

| Solution | $n_j$ |
|---|---|
| $S_1$ | **0** |
| $S_2$ | **0** |
| $S_3$ | **0** |
| $S_4$ | **0** |

All four are non-dominated: $PS^* = \{S_1, S_2, S_3, S_4\}$. No solution is discarded.

**Crowding distance computation** (assuming already normalized to $[0,1]$ on each axis):

Sort by $f_1$: $S_1(3,7), S_4(4,6), S_2(5,5), S_3(6,3)$.
Sort by $f_2$: $S_3(6,3), S_2(5,5), S_4(4,6), S_1(3,7)$.

Boundary solutions $S_1$ and $S_3$ get $d = \infty$ (or the maximum value).

For $S_4$:
- $|f_1^{(S_1)} - f_1^{(S_3)}| / (f_1^{\max} - f_1^{\min}) = |3 - 6| / 3 = 1.0$
- $|f_2^{(S_1)} - f_2^{(S_3)}| / (f_2^{\max} - f_2^{\min}) = |7 - 3| / 4 = 1.0$
- $d_{S_4} = 1.0 + 1.0 = 2.0$

For $S_2$:
- Neighbours in $f_1$ sort: $S_4$ and $S_3$ → $|f_1^{(S_4)} - f_1^{(S_3)}| / 3 = |4-6|/3 = 0.667$
- Neighbours in $f_2$ sort: $S_3$ and $S_4$ → $|f_2^{(S_3)} - f_2^{(S_4)}| / 4 = |3-6|/4 = 0.75$
- $d_{S_2} = 0.667 + 0.75 = 1.417$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Conflating weak and strict dominance** — remember, equal values in ALL objectives do NOT make a solution dominated; strict inequality in at least one is mandatory for $\prec$.
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize** objectives before computing crowding distance — this is mandatory when scales differ.
> 3. **Mis-identifying boundary solutions** in the crowding distance — endpoints should be assigned $d = \infty$ (or a large constant) to ensure their survival.
> 4. **Omitting the domination count** justification in long-answer questions — this is a frequent **3-mark** deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4**

- **Core definition**: A solution $\mathbf{x}^{(1)}$ **dominates** $\mathbf{x}^{(2)}$ iff it is no worse in all $M$ objectives and strictly better in at least one.
- **Pareto front** $PF^*$ is the image of the **Pareto-optimal set** $PS^*$ in objective space.
- The **domination count** $n_j$ is the primary ranking criterion in **non-dominated sorting**.
- The procedure has **seven steps**: Initialize → Evaluate → Compare → Count → Extract → Decrement → Iterate.
- **Time complexity** of naïve non-dominated sorting: $O(M N^2)$.
- **Three properties** of dominance: **irreflexivity**, **asymmetry**, **transitivity**.
- **Boundary solutions** in crowding distance have $d = \infty$ (always preserved).
- **Crowding distance** preserves **diversity** within a Pareto front — critical in NSGA-II.
- **Hypervolume indicator** $HV$ is a popular unary quality metric for $PF^*$ (higher is better).
- **Weak dominance** uses $\leq$ for all objectives; **strict dominance** requires at least one strict $<$.
- Real-world domains: aerospace, VLSI, structural, ML hyperparameter tuning, smart grids.
- **NSGA-II**, **SPEA2**, and **MOEA/D** are canonical algorithms that perform this procedure at every generation.
- Always **normalize** objectives before computing crowding distance or hypervolume.
- For **minimization**, lower $f_i$ is better; for **maximization**, negate the objectives first.
- When two solutions are **identical in all objectives**, both can co-exist; merging is optional.
- The **ideal point** $\mathbf{z}^*$ defines the lower-bound reference for hypervolume computation.
- A non-dominated set is **not unique** — multiple valid Pareto fronts may exist depending on the algorithm and randomness.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimality Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT403 - SOFT COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optimality Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Soft Computing** and numerical optimization, **Optimality Conditions** refer to the set of mathematical criteria (necessary, sufficient, and necessary-and-sufficient) that a candidate point $x^{*}$ must satisfy in order to qualify as a local or global minimizer (or maximizer) of an objective function $f(x)$ over a feasible domain.

These conditions are typically expressed in terms of the **gradient** $\nabla f(x)$, the **Hessian matrix** $\nabla^{2} f(x)$, and the **Lagrangian** function $\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu)$ for constrained problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Optimality conditions form the mathematical backbone justifying why Genetic Algorithms (GAs), Evolutionary Strategies, and other meta-heuristic search operators converge toward better solutions. They also govern the convergence behavior of Gradient Descent and Lamarckian-style local search hybrids.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mountain Valley Intuition

Imagine you are standing blindfolded in a mountainous terrain at night, and your goal is to find the **deepest valley** (global minimum).

- **First-Order Condition (FOC):** Tells you that at the valley bottom, the slope of the ground is exactly **zero**. You are no longer going downhill. This is analogous to $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$.
- **Second-Order Condition (SOC):** Even when the slope is zero, you could be on a flat ridge, a saddle point, or a true valley. The second derivative (curvature) tells you whether the terrain is *curving upward* (valley = minimum) or *curving downward* (peak = maximum) or is flat (saddle).
- **Constrained Condition (KKT):** If there are walls (constraints) preventing you from stepping outside a region, you need additional rules — the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** — to determine the optimum while respecting the walls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Principle:** Every population-based soft computing technique (GA, PSO, DE) implicitly attempts to *satisfy* these optimality conditions across successive generations. The Schema Theorem and Building Block Hypothesis are stochastic approximations to these deterministic conditions.

### 1.3 Standard Mathematical Objects (with notation)

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ | Objective (fitness) function |
| $x^{*} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ | Candidate optimal point |
| $\nabla f(x)$ | Gradient vector (first partial derivatives) |
| $\nabla^{2} f(x)$ | Hessian matrix (second partial derivatives) |
| $g_{i}(x) \le 0$ | Inequality constraints |
| $h_{j}(x) = 0$ | Equality constraints |
| $\lambda_{i}, \mu_{j}$ | Lagrange / KKT multipliers |
| $S$ | Feasible region |

> [!TIP]
> Remember: **$x^{*}$ is *necessary* to satisfy FOC**, but **FOC alone is not *sufficient*** for optimality. You must additionally check the SOC or convexity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Optimality Conditions

Optimality conditions are organized along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Order of the Condition**
   - First-Order Conditions (FOC) — involve $\nabla f(x^{*})$
   - Second-Order Conditions (SOC) — involve $\nabla^{2} f(x^{*})$

2. **Necessity vs Sufficiency**
   - *Necessary* — must be true at any optimum (one-directional implication)
   - *Sufficient* — guarantee optimum (one-directional implication)
   - *Necessary & Sufficient* — both directions (rare, requires convexity)

3. **Constraint Regime**
   - Unconstrained
   - Equality-constrained (Lagrange)
   - Inequality-constrained (KKT)

### 2.2 Unconstrained Optimality Conditions

#### 2.2.1 First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

If $x^{*}$ is a local minimizer of $f$ and $f$ is continuously differentiable in an open neighborhood of $x^{*}$, then:

$$\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$$

**Why this works:** A differentiable function has zero slope at any interior extremum (Fermat's stationary-point theorem extended to $\mathbb{R}^{n}$).

#### 2.2.2 Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC)

If $x^{*}$ is a local minimizer and $f \in C^{2}$ in an open neighborhood, then:

$$\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) \succeq 0 \quad \text{(positive semi-definite)}$$

**Intuition:** The Hessian must be *curving up in every direction*. If any direction curves down, $x^{*}$ cannot be a minimum.

#### 2.2.3 Second-Order Sufficient Condition (SOSC)

If there exists $x^{*}$ such that $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$ and $\nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) \succ 0$ (positive definite), then $x^{*}$ is a **strict local minimizer**.

### 2.3 Constrained Optimality — KKT Conditions

The **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) conditions** generalize Lagrange multipliers to inequality constraints. For:

$$\min_{x} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_{i}(x) \le 0, \; h_{j}(x) = 0$$

Define the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda, \mu) = f(x) + \sum_{i} \lambda_{i} g_{i}(x) + \sum_{j} \mu_{j} h_{j}(x)$$

The KKT conditions are:

$$\nabla_{x} \mathcal{L} = 0 \quad \text{(stationarity)}$$
$$\lambda_{i} \ge 0, \quad \lambda_{i} g_{i}(x) = 0 \quad \text{(complementary slackness)}$$
$$g_{i}(x) \le 0, \quad h_{j}(x) = 0 \quad \text{(primal feasibility)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **convex** problems satisfying a constraint qualification (e.g., Slater's condition), KKT conditions are **necessary and sufficient** for global optimality.

### 2.4 Connection to Soft Computing — Why GAs "Work"

The **Schema Theorem (Holland, 1975)** and the **Building Block Hypothesis** are stochastic, population-level analogues of deterministic optimality conditions:

- A schema $H$ represents a partial solution template.
- Schemas with above-average fitness grow exponentially — analogous to the gradient pointing *toward* a stationary point.
- Crossover and mutation act as stochastic **Hessian probes**, exploring second-order curvature information implicitly.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Type | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| FONC | $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$ | Necessary | Unconstrained, $C^{1}$ |
| SONC | $\nabla f(x^{*})=0$, $\nabla^{2}f(x^{*})\succeq 0$ | Necessary | Unconstrained, $C^{2}$ |
| SOSC | $\nabla f(x^{*})=0$, $\nabla^{2}f(x^{*})\succ 0$ | Sufficient | Unconstrained, $C^{2}$ |
| Lagrange Stationarity | $\nabla f(x^{*}) + \sum \mu_{j} \nabla h_{j}(x^{*}) = 0$ | Necessary | Equality constraints |
| KKT Stationarity | $\nabla_{x}\mathcal{L}(x^{*},\lambda^{*},\mu^{*}) = 0$ | Necessary | Inequality + equality, regular point |
| Complementary Slackness | $\lambda_{i}^{*} g_{i}(x^{*}) = 0$ | Necessary | Inequality constraints |
| Convexity Theorem | $f$ convex $\Leftrightarrow f(y) \ge f(x) + \nabla f(x)^{\top}(y-x)$ | N & S | Global optimality |
| Strict Convexity | $\nabla^{2} f(x) \succ 0 \; \forall x$ | Sufficient | Unique global min |

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

Optimality conditions are deployed in:

- **Training Neural Networks:** Back-propagation applies FOC iteratively (gradient = 0 at convergence).
- **Hyperparameter Tuning in GA:** Defining stopping criteria based on fitness gradient stagnation.
- **Support Vector Machines:** The SVM dual problem is solved using KKT conditions — support vectors are precisely those with non-zero multipliers $\lambda_{i}^{*} > 0$.
- **Optimal Control & Robotics:** Trajectory optimization with state constraints.
- **Economics & Resource Allocation:** Cost minimization under budget constraints.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU exams:** "**Z**ero **G**radient, **C**urvature **U**p" → **ZG-CU** (Zer0 Gradient, Curvature Upward) for a minimum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the First-Order Necessary Condition (FONC)

**Statement:** If $f: \mathbb{R}^{n} \to \mathbb{R}$ is differentiable at $x^{*}$ and $x^{*}$ is an unconstrained local minimizer, then $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$.

**Proof (by contradiction using Taylor expansion):**

Assume, for contradiction, that $\nabla f(x^{*}) \neq 0$. Let us consider an arbitrary direction vector $d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$.

By the **first-order Taylor expansion** of $f$ around $x^{*}$ along direction $d$ for a small step $\alpha > 0$:

$$f(x^{*} + \alpha d) = f(x^{*}) + \alpha \, \nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} d + o(\alpha \Vert d \Vert)$$

where $o(\alpha \Vert d \Vert) / (\alpha \Vert d \Vert) \to 0$ as $\alpha \to 0$.

Since $x^{*}$ is a local minimizer, for all sufficiently small $\alpha > 0$:

$$f(x^{*} + \alpha d) \ge f(x^{*})$$

Substituting the Taylor expansion:

$$f(x^{*}) + \alpha \, \nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} d + o(\alpha \Vert d \Vert) \ge f(x^{*})$$

Canceling $f(x^{*})$ from both sides and dividing by $\alpha > 0$:

$$\nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} d + \frac{o(\alpha \Vert d \Vert)}{\alpha} \ge 0$$

Taking the limit $\alpha \to 0^{+}$, the second term vanishes:

$$\nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} d \ge 0$$

Since this must hold for *every* direction $d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, we may choose $d = -\nabla f(x^{*})$ (provided $\nabla f(x^{*}) \neq 0$):

$$\nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} (-\nabla f(x^{*})) = -\Vert \nabla f(x^{*}) \Vert^{2} \ge 0$$

This is a contradiction unless $\Vert \nabla f(x^{*}) \Vert^{2} = 0$, which forces $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Second-Order Necessary Condition (SONC)

**Statement:** If $f \in C^{2}$ and $x^{*}$ is a local minimizer, then $\nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) \succeq 0$ (positive semi-definite).

**Proof:**

Apply the **second-order Taylor expansion** to $f$ around $x^{*}$:

$$f(x^{*} + \alpha d) = f(x^{*}) + \alpha \, \nabla f(x^{*})^{\top} d + \frac{\alpha^{2}}{2} \, d^{\top} \nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) d + o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2})$$

Since $x^{*}$ is a minimizer and $\nabla f(x^{*}) = 0$ (by FONC), this reduces to:

$$f(x^{*} + \alpha d) = f(x^{*}) + \frac{\alpha^{2}}{2} \, d^{\top} \nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) d + o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2})$$

Local minimality requires $f(x^{*} + \alpha d) \ge f(x^{*})$ for small $\alpha > 0$, hence:

$$\frac{\alpha^{2}}{2} \, d^{\top} \nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) d + o(\alpha^{2} \Vert d \Vert^{2}) \ge 0$$

Dividing by $\alpha^{2}/2$ and letting $\alpha \to 0^{+}$:

$$d^{\top} \nabla^{2} f(x^{*}) d \ge 0 \quad \text{for all } d \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

This is exactly the definition of **positive semi-definiteness** of the Hessian. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation of KKT Stationarity Condition (Lagrangian Approach)

**Problem Setup:**

$$\min_{x} f(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad g_{i}(x) \le 0, \; i = 1, \dots, m$$

**Step 1 — Lagrangian Formulation:**

Introduce multipliers $\lambda_{i} \ge 0$ (one per inequality constraint). The Lagrangian is:

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = f(x) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_{i} g_{i}(x)$$

**Step 2 — Inactive Constraints are Ignored:**

If at the optimum $x^{*}$, an inequality constraint $g_{i}$ is *strictly inactive* (i.e., $g_{i}(x^{*}) < 0$), then there is local slack and the constraint can be effectively removed. The corresponding multiplier $\lambda_{i}^{*} = 0$.

If the constraint is *active* ($g_{i}(x^{*}) = 0$), it binds the optimum and $\lambda_{i}^{*} \ge 0$ becomes meaningful.

**Step 3 — Stationarity of the Lagrangian:**

Differentiate $\mathcal{L}$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero:

$$\nabla_{x} \mathcal{L}(x^{*}, \lambda^{*}) = \nabla f(x^{*}) + \sum_{i=1}^{m} \lambda_{i}^{*} \nabla g_{i}(x^{*}) = 0$$

This is the **KKT stationarity condition**.

**Step 4 — Complementary Slackness:**

Combining the active-set logic with the sign of $\lambda_{i}$:

$$\lambda_{i}^{*} \, g_{i}(x^{*}) = 0, \quad \lambda_{i}^{*} \ge 0, \quad g_{i}(x^{*}) \le 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complementary slackness condition encapsulates the *either-or* nature: either the constraint binds (and $\lambda_{i}^{*} > 0$) or it is slack (and $\lambda_{i}^{*} = 0$). They cannot both be strictly active.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — FONC and SONC Verification

**Problem:** Find the minimum of $f(x, y) = x^{2} + 2y^{2} - 4x - 4y + 8$ using optimality conditions.

**Step 1 — Compute the gradient:**

$$\frac{\partial f}{\partial x} = 2x - 4, \quad \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} = 4y - 4$$

**Step 2 — Apply FONC: set $\nabla f = 0$:**

$$2x - 4 = 0 \implies x = 2$$
$$4y - 4 = 0 \implies y = 1$$

So $x^{*} = (2, 1)^{\top}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Hessian:**

$$\nabla^{2} f = \begin{pmatrix} \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x^{2}} & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial x \partial y} \\ \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y \partial x} & \frac{\partial^{2} f}{\partial y^{2}} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Verify SOSC (positive definiteness):**

Leading principal minors: $2 > 0$ and $(2)(4) - 0 = 8 > 0$. Both positive $\Rightarrow \nabla^{2} f \succ 0$ (positive definite).

By SOSC, $x^{*} = (2, 1)$ is a **strict local minimum**. The minimum value is $f(2, 1) = 4 + 2 - 8 - 4 + 8 = 2$.

### 3.5 Worked Example — KKT for a 1-D Constrained Problem

**Problem:** Minimize $f(x) = (x - 3)^{2}$ subject to $x \ge 1$.

**Step 1 — Reformulate the constraint:**

$$g(x) = 1 - x \le 0 \quad \text{(i.e., } x \ge 1\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the Lagrangian:**

$$\mathcal{L}(x, \lambda) = (x - 3)^{2} + \lambda (1 - x)$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity:**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial x} = 2(x - 3) - \lambda = 0 \implies \lambda = 2(x - 3)$$

**Step 4 — Complementary slackness:** $\lambda(1 - x) = 0$ with $\lambda \ge 0$, $1 - x \le 0$.

**Case A — Constraint inactive ($1 - x < 0$, so $x > 1$):**
Then $\lambda = 0 \implies x = 3$. Check: $1 - 3 = -2 \le 0$ ✓. So $x^{*} = 3$, $\lambda^{*} = 0$. This satisfies all KKT conditions — the optimum lies *inside* the feasible region.

**Case B — Constraint active ($1 - x = 0$, so $x = 1$):**
Then $\lambda = 2(1 - 3) = -4$. But KKT requires $\lambda \ge 0$. This case is rejected.

**Conclusion:** $x^{*} = 3$, $f(x^{*}) = 0$. The unconstrained minimum is feasible.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Optimality Conditions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimality Conditions]:::root --> B[Unconstrained]:::branch
    A --> C[Constrained]:::branch

    B --> B1[FONC: grad f = 0]:::node
    B --> B2[SONC: Hessian PSD]:::node
    B --> B3[SOSC: Hessian PD]:::node
    B --> B4[Convexity: Global]:::node

    C --> C1[Equality: Lagrange]:::node
    C --> C2[Inequality: KKT]:::node
    C --> C3[Convex + KKT: Global]:::node

    C2 --> C2a[Stationarity]:::leaf
    C2 --> C2b[Primal Feasibility]:::leaf
    C2 --> C2c[Dual Feasibility]:::leaf
    C2 --> C2d[Complementary Slackness]:::leaf

    B1 --> X1[Type: Necessary]:::meta
    B2 --> X2[Type: Necessary]:::meta
    B3 --> X3[Type: Sufficient]:::meta
    B4 --> X4[Type: Necessary and Sufficient]:::meta

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef node fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#0c2461,stroke-width:1px
    classDef leaf fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#d97706,color:#78350f,stroke-width:1px
    classDef meta fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#6366f1,color:#312e81,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 4 2
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Optimization Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[/"Input: f(x), constraints, initial x0"/]:::inp
    GRAD["Compute Gradient grad f(x)"]:::proc
    HESS["Compute Hessian H = grad^2 f(x)"]:::proc
    FOC_CHK{"grad f = 0 ?"}:::gate
    SOC_CHK{"H Positive Definite ?"}:::gate
    KKT_CHK{"All KKT Satisfied ?"}:::gate
    CONV_CHK{"Convex Problem ?"}:::gate
    OUT_MIN[/"Output: Local Minimum x*"/]:::out
    OUT_MAX[/"Output: Saddle or Maximum - Reject"/]:::out
    OUT_GLOBAL[/"Output: Global Minimum x*"/]:::out
    NEWTON["Apply Newton / Quasi-Newton Step"]:::proc

    INPUT --> GRAD
    GRAD --> HESS
    HESS --> FOC_CHK
    FOC_CHK -- No --> NEWTON --> GRAD
    FOC_CHK -- Yes --> SOC_CHK
    SOC_CHK -- No --> OUT_MAX
    SOC_CHK -- Yes --> KKT_CHK
    KKT_CHK -- No --> OUT_MAX
    KKT_CHK -- Yes --> CONV_CHK
    CONV_CHK -- No --> OUT_MIN
    CONV_CHK -- Yes --> OUT_GLOBAL

    classDef inp fill:#0f766e,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#0891b2,stroke:#155e75,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gate fill:#fde047,stroke:#a16207,color:#1c1917,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Schema Theorem as Stochastic Optimality Condition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Population at Generation t]:::state --> SEL[Fitness-Proportionate Selection]:::op
    SEL --> CRO[Single-Point Crossover]:::op
    CRO --> MUT[Bit-Flip Mutation]:::op
    MUT --> P2[Population at Generation t+1]:::state
    P2 --> EVAL[Evaluate Schema Fitness]:::proc
    EVAL --> GROW{Schema Fitness > Average ?}:::gate
    GROW -- Yes --> EXP[Exponential Growth of Schema]:::good
    GROW -- No --> DEC[Exponential Decay]:::bad
    EXP --> CONV[Approach to Gradient Zero Stationarity]:::target
    DEC --> CONV
    CONV --> OPT[/Population Concentrates Near Optimum/]:::goal

    classDef state fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#4338ca,color:#1e1b4b,stroke-width:1px
    classDef op fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#be185d,color:#500724,stroke-width:1px
    classDef proc fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,color:#052e16,stroke-width:1px
    classDef gate fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#451a03,stroke-width:1px
    classDef good fill:#86efac,stroke:#16a34a,color:#052e16,stroke-width:1px
    classDef bad fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#450a0a,stroke-width:1px
    classDef target fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#1e1b4b,stroke-width:1px
    classDef goal fill:#22c55e,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```
